His deposition by Aurangzeb is partly an economic story. The beauty of the Taj and the fiscal damage it caused are two sides of the same card.
84.remember: Shah Jahan was deposed by his son Aurangzeb and spent his final years in the Agra Fort, staring at the Taj from his cell window.
84.upsc: Tests the architect (Ustad Ahmad Lahauri), use of white marble, and the symmetrical Persian 'Charbagh' garden layout.

85.label: Shivaji crowned Chhatrapati
85.what: Shivaji was crowned 'Chhatrapati' at Raigad (1674). The ceremony gave formal legitimacy to the Maratha state as a sovereign Hindu power.
85.connections: The coronation is the formal establishment of the Maratha state, distinct from Shivaji's birth card (1630) which covers his military career. The Maratha Confederacy card (1720) is the next step: after Shivaji's death, power shifted to the Peshwas, who expanded the Marathas into a pan-Indian confederacy. The coronation gave that expansion its political and symbolic legitimacy.
85.remember: Shivaji had to prove his 'Kshatriya' lineage to skeptical Brahmins by tracing his ancestry to Rajputs before the coronation could proceed.
85.upsc: Tests the significance of the coronation as the first formal Hindu kingship in the Deccan in centuries, the resistance from Brahmin priests who initially questioned his Kshatriya status, and Raigad as the Maratha capital.

86.label: Aurangzeb reimposed Jizya tax
86.what: Aurangzeb reimposed the Jizya tax (1679), reversing Akbar's policy. This alienation led to uprisings by Rajputs, Marathas, and Sikhs.
86.connections: This card is the direct counterpart to Akbar's Jizya abolition (1564). The 115-year gap is the central Akbar-Aurangzeb comparison. Aurangzeb's reimposition came 2 years before his Deccan campaigns (1681), which drained the treasury and army. The two decisions together, Jizya and the Deccan overextension, are the most cited causes of Mughal decline in the 18th century.
86.remember: For UPSC, the smart move is to treat Jizya as politically significant without turning it into a one-line explanation for every later revolt.
86.upsc: Tests Jizya, the Deccan campaigns, relations with Rajputs, Marathas, and Sikhs, and the wider strains on the Mughal Empire.

87.label: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns
87.what: Aurangzeb personally led campaigns in the Deccan from 1681 until his death in 1707, spending 26 years trying to crush the Maratha resistance. The treasury was drained, the army exhausted, and the empire began to collapse.
87.connections: Compare with Alauddin Khilji's Deccan campaigns via Malik Kafur (1296) and Harsha's southern check at the Narmada: northern empires repeatedly overextended south. Aurangzeb's 26-year Deccan campaign is the longest and most costly of all of them. The Maratha Confederacy that expanded after his death (1720) is the direct political consequence of his failure to destroy them.
87.remember: Aurangzeb died in his Deccan camp at 88, having spent the last 26 years of his life at war. He wrote a final letter admitting he had come to rule the world but failed. He was buried in a simple grave with no monument, as he had requested. The greatest Mughal emperor died defeated.
87.upsc: Tests this as the primary cause of Mughal decline: a 26-year war with no decisive victory, a 300-mile long camp following Aurangzeb that became a moving city of 500,000 people, and the Marathas' successful guerrilla resistance.

88.label: Maratha Confederacy
88.what: After the deaths of Shivaji (1680) and his son Sambhaji (1689), effective Maratha power shifted to the Peshwas (prime ministers). Under Bajirao I (1720-1740) the Marathas expanded into a pan-Indian confederacy.
88.connections: The Maratha Confederacy is the political power that fills the vacuum left by Mughal decline. By 1750, the Marathas controlled more Indian territory than any other power. The Third Battle of Panipat (1761) is the event that ends this expansion. After Panipat, the Marathas survived but never recovered their pan-Indian dominance, which created the opening that the EIC eventually exploited.
88.remember: Bajirao I proposed marching to Delhi and placing a Hindu emperor on the Mughal throne. He came within 60 miles of Delhi before turning back. He told his men: let us strike at the trunk, the branches will fall themselves. He died at 40, having never lost a single battle.
88.upsc: Tests the Peshwa system as the real power behind the Maratha throne, Bajirao I's military genius (never lost a battle), the five Maratha chiefs (Bhonsle, Scindia, Holkar, Gaikwad, Peshwa), and why the confederacy was weaker than a centralised state.

89.label: Hyderabad state
89.what: Asaf Jah I, the Mughal Governor of the Deccan, defeated a rival in 1724 and established the independent Hyderabad state. The Nizams ruled Hyderabad for over 200 years until 1948.
89.connections: Hyderabad's founding is part of the 18th century pattern of Mughal provincial governors declaring independence: Hyderabad (1724), Awadh (1720s), Bengal under the Nawabs. Each fragmentation weakened the central Mughal structure and created the patchwork of regional powers the EIC picked off one by one. Hyderabad was the last of these to be integrated, by India in 1948, not by the British.
89.remember: The Nizams of Hyderabad were so wealthy that they were once called the richest family in the world. The last Nizam, Mir Osman Ali Khan, kept the Jacob Diamond (one of the world's largest) as a paperweight. After integration, his wealth was frozen and he died in relative poverty.
89.upsc: Tests the fragmentation of the Mughal Empire in the 18th century, the Nizam as the largest Indian princely state, and the integration of Hyderabad into India in 1948 (Operation Polo).

90.label: Nadir Shah's invasion of Delhi
90.what: Nadir Shah, the Persian ruler, invaded Delhi in 1739 and massacred an estimated 20,000 to 30,000 Delhi residents. He looted the Peacock Throne and the Koh-i-Noor diamond and left within three months.
90.connections: Nadir Shah's invasion is the most visible proof of Mughal military collapse. Compare with Timur's invasion (1398): both came from the northwest, sacked Delhi, looted enormously, and left quickly. But Timur's invasion ended the Tughlaq dynasty's power; Nadir Shah's invasion ended the Mughal empire's credibility. The EIC began its political consolidation within 18 years of this event.
90.remember: Nadir Shah took so much loot that he abolished taxes in Persia for three years. The Peacock Throne alone was worth millions of gold coins.
90.upsc: Tests the humiliation of the Mughals, the looting of the Peacock Throne and Koh-i-Noor, and the collapse of imperial authority.

91.label: Battle of Plassey
91.what: At the Battle of Plassey (1757), Robert Clive defeated Siraj ud-Daulah via the treachery of Mir Jafar, starting British political rule.
91.connections: Plassey is the political start of British India. Pair with Buxar (1764): Plassey gave the EIC political authority through betrayal; Buxar gave it financial authority through actual battle. The Diwani rights that followed Buxar formalised what Plassey informally began. Both cards together tell the complete story of how a trading company became a territorial power.
91.remember: The 'battle' was decided before it began; Mir Jafar commanded the largest part of the Nawab's army and never fired a shot.
91.upsc: Tests the 'Black Hole' incident, the shift from trade to politics, and the massive loot that funded the Industrial Revolution.

92.label: Battle of Plassey
92.what: Robert Clive and the EIC defeated Nawab Siraj ud-Daulah of Bengal at Plassey on 23 June 1757 through the treachery of Mir Jafar, beginning British political control over India.
92.connections: Pair with Buxar (1764). Plassey gave the EIC political power through betrayal; Buxar gave it financial authority through actual battle. Students routinely mix up what each achieved. The wealth transferred from Bengal after Plassey is the context for the Industrial Revolution debate, and for the Bengal famine 186 years later.
92.remember: The battle lasted barely a few hours. Mir Jafar's forces, forming the bulk of the Nawab's army, simply stood aside. Clive had negotiated the betrayal weeks in advance. The real battle was fought in letters, not on the field.
92.upsc: Tests Mir Jafar's betrayal, the Black Hole of Calcutta incident (1756) that preceded it, and the massive wealth transferred from Bengal to Britain that historians link to funding the Industrial Revolution.

93.label: Industrial Revolution in Britain
93.what: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain around 1760 with the mechanisation of textile production using James Watt's steam engine. It transformed Britain from an agrarian to an industrial economy within a century.
93.connections: The Industrial Revolution and the wealth extracted from India after Plassey (1757) are connected: the timing is not accidental. Bengal's textile wealth (extracted after Buxar 1765) funded the investment that drove British industrialisation. India's deindustrialisation (handloom weavers losing to Manchester mills) is the Indian side of the same story. Naoroji's Drain Theory (1867) is the economic argument that connects the two.
93.remember: The timing is not a coincidence. Britain looted Bengal's treasury of millions after Plassey in 1757. The Industrial Revolution began three years later. Indian wealth funded British industrialisation. Indian artisans were then destroyed by the machine-made goods they helped finance.
93.upsc: Tests the connection between Bengal's wealth (looted after Plassey 1757) and British industrial capital, the deindustrialisation of India under colonial rule, and why Indian handloom weavers were ruined.

94.label: Third Battle of Panipat
94.what: The Third Battle of Panipat (1761) saw Ahmad Shah Abdali defeat the Maratha Confederacy, decimating their power in Northern India.
94.connections: Third Panipat closes the three-battle sequence: First Panipat (1526) starts the Mughal Empire; Second Panipat (1556) saves it; Third Panipat (1761) ends the Maratha bid to replace it. After this battle, no Indian power was strong enough to unify the subcontinent, which is precisely the political vacuum the EIC filled over the next 100 years. The battle is the indirect cause of British India.
94.remember: The Maratha defeat was so bad that a coded message to the Peshwa said: 'Two pearls have been dissolved... and of the gold and silver, the total can not even be counted'.
94.upsc: Tests the reasons for defeat (betrayal by allies, lack of food) and how this vacuum allowed the British to rise.

95.label: Battle of Buxar
95.what: The Battle of Buxar (1764) saw the EIC defeat the combined forces of the Mughal Emperor, Nawab of Oudh, and Nawab of Bengal.
95.connections: After Buxar, the chain runs tight: Diwani rights (1765) → Regulating Act (1773) → Pitt's India Act (1784) → Crown Rule (1858). Every major governance act of British India flows from the financial authority Buxar established. Also: Buxar is tested alongside Plassey as a pair. Know exactly what each battle specifically gave the EIC.
95.remember: Plassey gave the EIC political influence, but Buxar gave it financial control. It was the real turning point for British India.
95.upsc: UPSC considers this more important than Plassey. The Treaty of Allahabad (1765) gave EIC 'Diwani' rights (revenue collection) over Bengal.

96.label: Raja Ram Mohan Roy
96.what: Raja Ram Mohan Roy (1772-1833) was a Bengali polymath, social reformer and founder of the Brahmo Samaj. He campaigned against Sati, advocated English education and is called the Father of the Indian Renaissance.
96.connections: Roy is the founding figure of India's modern social reform tradition. The Brahmo Samaj (1828), Sati Abolition (1829), and his newspaper campaigns are all Social Reform cards. He is the intellectual ancestor of every reformer in that category. His death in London (1833) while fighting the orthodox appeal against Sati is the most fitting biographical ending in Indian reform history.
96.remember: Roy was a master of languages; he published newspapers in Bengali, Persian, and English to spread reformist ideas.
96.upsc: Tests his campaign against Sati (abolished 1829), promotion of English education, and his monotheistic reforms.

97.label: Regulating Act
97.what: The Regulating Act of 1773 was the first British attempt to control the EIC. It created the post of Governor-General of Bengal.
97.connections: The first in a sequence of acts by which Parliament progressively took control of the EIC: Regulating Act (1773) → Pitt's India Act (1784) → Charter Acts (1793, 1813, 1833, 1853) → Government of India Act (1858). Each act reduced Company autonomy and increased Crown oversight, until the Company was abolished entirely.
97.remember: The Act failed because the Governor-General had no veto over his council, a design flaw fixed by later acts.
97.upsc: Tests constitutional recognition of EIC's role and the creation of the Supreme Court at Calcutta. Warren Hastings was the first GG.

98.label: American Declaration of Independence
98.what: The American Declaration of Independence (1776) asserted 'Life, Liberty and the pursuit of Happiness' as unalienable rights.
98.connections: The Declaration's 'all men are created equal' influenced every subsequent independence movement including India's. Tilak and others explicitly cited American independence. The irony that Jefferson enslaved people while writing about equality is the gap between principle and practice that recurs across every reform movement in Social Reform. The US founding also directly inspired the French Revolution (1789).
98.remember: Jefferson wrote it in 17 days; the phrase 'All men are created equal' took centuries to apply to all citizens in practice.
98.upsc: Tests its influence on the French Revolution, the Preamble to India's Constitution, and Enlightenment philosophy. The Indian Constitution's Preamble echoes the Declaration's language.

99.label: Pitt's India Act
99.what: Pitt's India Act (1784) established a 'Board of Control' in London to manage EIC's political affairs, creating a dual government.
99.connections: The sequence of parliamentary control: Regulating Act (1773) → Pitt's India Act (1784) → Charter Acts (1813, 1833) → Crown takeover (1858). Each act tightened Parliament's grip on the Company. Pitt's dual government (Board of Control in London, Governor-General on the ground) ran India for the next 70 years.
99.remember: Pitt's Act worked where the Regulating Act failed by giving the Governor-General overriding powers over his council.
99.upsc: Tests the distinction between political and commercial functions. It established the supremacy of the British Parliament over the Company.

100.label: French Revolution
100.what: The French Revolution began with the storming of the Bastille prison on 14 July 1789. It overthrew the monarchy, established the principles of Liberty, Equality and Fraternity, and triggered revolutionary waves across Europe.
100.connections: The French Revolution's Liberty, Equality, Fraternity was explicitly invoked by Ambedkar in the Mahad Satyagraha (1927) and throughout his constitutional work. Compare with the American Revolution: the American model preserved slavery while proclaiming equality; the French model produced terror before liberal democracy. Both influenced Indian political thought in different ways.
100.remember: The fall of the Bastille prison (14 July) is still celebrated as France's national day (Bastille Day).
100.upsc: Tests its causes (inequality, debt) and the Declaration of the Rights of Man. These ideals are central to the Indian Preamble.

101.label: Permanent Settlement of Bengal
101.what: Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement (1793) in Bengal, fixing land revenue permanently with hereditary Zamindars.
101.connections: The Permanent Settlement created zamindars as a loyal class. A century later, those same zamindars' exploitation of peasants is exactly what Gandhi fights in Champaran (1917). The colonial land system and the nationalist response to it are the same story, separated by 120 years.
101.remember: Revenue was fixed 'forever'; this was great for Zamindars in boom years but ruinous for peasants during harvests failures.
101.upsc: Tests the impact: created a loyal class of Zamindars but impoverished peasants and stifled agricultural investment.

102.label: Subsidiary Alliance system
102.what: Lord Wellesley's Subsidiary Alliance system forced Indian rulers to pay for British troops and give up independent foreign policy.
102.connections: Hyderabad signed the Subsidiary Alliance first in 1798, and that relationship locked it into British protection until 1947. When independence came, the Nizam tried to stay independent, and Sardar Patel used military force to end it (Operation Polo, 1948). The debt trap Wellesley designed outlasted the British Empire itself.
102.remember: The alliance was a debt trap: rulers couldn't afford the troops, fell into arrears, and had to cede territory to pay the British.
102.upsc: Tests expansion without annexation. Hyderabad was the first to sign (1798). It made states politically subordinate.

103.label: Fourth Anglo-Mysore War
103.what: The Fourth Anglo-Mysore War (1799) ended with Tipu Sultan's death at Seringapatam. Mysore was partitioned between the EIC and Wodeyars.
103.connections: Four Anglo-Mysore Wars, four numbers: 1767, 1780, 1790, 1799. Each one weakens Mysore further. Tipu's French alliance attempts mirror what Indian rulers would keep trying: external powers as counterweights to British dominance. It never worked. After Tipu, Mysore became a subsidiary state under Wodeyar rule, a template the Subsidiary Alliance then replicated everywhere.
103.remember: Tipu died fighting at his fort's gate. He famously said: 'Better to live one day as a tiger than a thousand years as a sheep.'
103.upsc: Tests Tipu's resistance (rockets, French ties). Know the sequence of all four Anglo-Mysore wars.

104.label: Second Anglo-Maratha War
104.what: The Second Anglo-Maratha War (1803–05) broke the Maratha confederacy and gave the British control over Delhi and the Mughal Emperor.
104.connections: Three Anglo-Maratha Wars: 1775, 1803, 1817. After the Third (1817-18), the Peshwa surrendered and the confederacy ended permanently. The Second War (this card) gave the British control of Delhi and the Mughal Emperor, making the emperor a pensioner of the company that had come to trade in his grandfather's time.
104.remember: The Maratha chiefs Sindhia and Bhonsle were defeated, ending their hope of replacing the Mughals as masters of India.
104.upsc: Tests the three wars (1775, 1803, 1817). After the 2nd war, the Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II became a British pensioner.

105.label: Napoleon became Emperor of France
105.what: Napoleon Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor (1804). He conquered much of Europe but was finally defeated at Waterloo (1815).
105.connections: Napoleon's self-crowning (taking the crown from the Pope's hands and placing it on his own head) is the founding image of secular political authority over religious authority. His Napoleonic Code standardised law across Europe and influenced legal systems globally. The Congress of Vienna (1815) that followed his defeat tried to restore the old order; the 1848 revolutions proved that order was gone.
105.remember: Napoleon took the crown from the Pope's hands and put it on his own head to show he answered to no one.
105.upsc: Tests the 'Napoleonic Code' (civil law foundation) and his role in spreading nationalism across Europe.

106.label: Haitian Revolution
106.what: The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804) led by Toussaint Louverture and Jean-Jacques Dessalines resulted in Haiti becoming the first Black republic and the first country founded by formerly enslaved people.
106.connections: Haiti is the only successful slave revolution in history. France's demand for reparations (150 million francs for lost slaves and plantations) is the founding colonial debt; Haiti only finished paying it in 1947. Compare with India's colonial drain: both are examples of wealth transfer from colonised to coloniser, one through slavery, one through trade exploitation. Both the Drain Theory (1867) and the Haitian debt are the same structural relationship described differently.
106.remember: France demanded Haiti pay 150 million francs as reparations for the loss of its slaves and plantations. Haiti paid this debt until 1947. A country founded by people freed from slavery spent 143 years paying their former enslavers for the crime of becoming free.
106.upsc: Tests as a world history example of successful anti-colonial resistance, its influence on later independence movements, and the brutal French demand for reparations (paid until 1947) that crippled Haiti.

107.label: Charter Act
107.what: The Charter Act of 1813 ended the EIC's trade monopoly in India (except Tea and China trade) and allowed Christian missionaries entry.
107.connections: Charter Acts ran every 20 years: 1793, 1813, 1833, 1853. Each time Parliament imposed new conditions on the EIC's renewal. The education grant in the 1813 Act triggers the Anglicist-Orientalist debate: Macaulay's Minute (1835) follows, then Wood's Despatch (1854). What Indians should be taught, and in which language, was never fully resolved.
107.remember: The 1833 Act would later end *all* EIC trade, but 1813 was the first major blow to its commercial supremacy.
107.upsc: Tests the end of monopoly, the ₹1 lakh education grant, and the start of the 'Anglicist-Orientalist' debate.

108.label: Battle of Waterloo
108.what: The Battle of Waterloo (1815) saw Napoleon's final defeat by the Duke of Wellington and Prussian forces.
108.connections: Waterloo is the military endpoint of the Napoleonic era. The Congress of Vienna (1815) that followed redrew Europe's map and established the Concert of Europe: a system of great-power cooperation that was the direct predecessor of the League of Nations and the UN Security Council's permanent membership structure. The security architecture the world uses today traces back to the post-Waterloo settlement.
108.remember: Napoleon was defeated largely because Prussian reinforcements arrived at 7 PM when Wellington's line was breaking. If they had arrived an hour later, Napoleon would likely have won. History pivoted on 60 minutes.
108.upsc: Tests the Concert of Europe established at the Congress of Vienna (1815) after Napoleon's defeat, the principle of balance of power, and how this shaped 19th century European diplomacy.

109.label: Swami Dayananda Saraswati
109.what: Swami Dayananda Saraswati founded the Arya Samaj (1875), advocating 'Back to the Vedas' and rejecting idol worship.
109.connections: Dayananda's Arya Samaj (1875) is covered in Social Reform. Compare with Ram Mohan Roy (Western rationalism) and Vivekananda (neo-Vedanta synthesis) as three distinct approaches to Hindu reform within the same century. The Arya Samaj's later associations with Hindu nationalism make it the most politically complex of the three.
109.remember: Arya Samaj founded the DAV (Dayananda Anglo-Vedic) schools, which blended modern science with Vedic teaching.
109.upsc: Tests his book 'Satyarth Prakash' and the 'Shuddhi' movement for reconversion to Hinduism.

110.label: Brahmo Samaj
110.what: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded the Brahmo Samaj in Calcutta in 1828, a monotheistic reform movement that rejected idol worship, caste discrimination and advocated for women's rights and English education.
110.connections: The Brahmo Samaj is the first card in India's modern social reform arc. Roy's movement is the origin point from which Arya Samaj (1875), Ramakrishna Mission (1897), and eventually Gandhi's social campaigns all descend. The exam often contrasts Roy's rationalist, Western-influenced approach with later movements that drew on indigenous religious authority. Roy is also the political predecessor to Sati Abolition (1829), which he directly campaigned for.
110.remember: Roy was the first Indian to use the press for social reform, publishing newspapers in Bengali, Persian, and English.
110.upsc: Tests his role in the 1829 Sati ban and his advocacy for English education and women's property rights.

111.label: Sati Abolition Act
111.what: Lord William Bentinck passed the Sati Regulation (1829), making the practice a criminal offence, backed by Raja Ram Mohan Roy.
111.connections: The Sati Abolition Act establishes the pattern of colonial law plus Indian reformer pressure that repeats across this category: Widow Remarriage (1856), Child Marriage Restraint (1929), Hindu Code Bills (1955). Each addresses a different dimension of women's legal personhood. The pattern is: Indian reformer identifies the problem, colonial (or post-colonial) state legislates, enforcement lags the law by decades.
111.remember: Roy personally went to London to argue against the orthodox appeal to restore Sati, he died there in 1833.
111.upsc: Tests Bentinck's role, Raja Ram Mohan Roy's campaign that enabled it, and the orthodox Hindu opposition led by Radha Kanta Deb. UPSC connects this to the broader reform vs. tradition debate.

112.label: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa
112.what: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa was a mystic who taught the essential unity of all religions: 'As many faiths, so many paths'.
112.connections: Ramakrishna's significance flows almost entirely through Vivekananda, who was his disciple. Without Ramakrishna there is no Chicago speech (1893) and no Ramakrishna Mission (1897). His practice of Islam and Christianity connects to Akbar's Din-i-Ilahi (1582) as a recurring Indian pattern of pluralist spiritual inquiry across centuries.
112.remember: Ramakrishna practiced Islam and Christianity for short periods to experience their truths firsthand.
112.upsc: Tests his influence on Swami Vivekananda and the founding of the Ramakrishna Mission after his death.

113.label: Opium Wars between Britain and China
113.what: Britain fought two Opium Wars against China (1839-42 and 1856-60) to force China to accept imports of opium grown in British India. China was defeated and forced to open ports and cede Hong Kong.
113.connections: Britain fighting a war to force China to accept opium imports is the clearest single example of colonial commercial coercion. The opium was grown in Bengal; the profits funded the British India administration. The Unequal Treaties gave Britain Hong Kong. Compare with the Drain Theory (1867): the Opium Wars are the mechanism by which Britain extracted wealth from both China and India simultaneously, using one colony to oppress another.
113.remember: Britain fought a war to force a country to buy drugs. The opium was grown in Bengal. The profits funded the British Indian government. China's defeat in the Opium Wars began its Century of Humiliation. The trade was declared legal by British courts.
113.upsc: Tests the triangular trade (India opium to China, Chinese tea to Britain, British goods to India), the unequal treaties, and as an example of European imperial coercion that parallels India's own experience.

114.label: Doctrine of Lapse
114.what: Lord Dalhousie's Doctrine of Lapse annexed states (Satara, Jhansi, Nagpur) if a ruler died without a natural male heir.
114.connections: Multiple annexation tools operated simultaneously. Subsidiary Alliance (debt trap), Doctrine of Lapse (inheritance trap), Awadh annexed on misgovernance grounds. All three fed 1857. The Doctrine of Lapse is the most remembered because Rani Lakshmibai is the most famous victim, but the Awadh annexation arguably alienated more soldiers.
114.remember: Dalhousie also annexed Awadh (Oudh) in 1856 on grounds of 'misgovernance', further alienating Indian soldiers.
114.upsc: Direct cause of the 1857 revolt. Rani Laxmibai of Jhansi is the most famous victim of this policy.

115.label: Communist Manifesto
115.what: The Communist Manifesto (1848) by Marx and Engels called for a workers' revolution against the capitalist 'Bourgeoisie'.
115.connections: The Communist Manifesto's influence on Indian politics is direct: Nehru's socialism, the CPI's founding, the Five-Year Plans, and the Emergency era's leftward shifts all connect here. Compare with the French Revolution's Liberty-Equality-Fraternity as the two Western intellectual exports most influential on Indian political thought. The Manifesto was published the same year as India's first girls' school (1848): the same year produces reform at both ends of the world.
115.remember: The Communist Manifesto was published on the eve of the 1848 revolutions in Europe. Marx and Engels predicted revolution but the revolutions that year were all defeated. The actual communist revolution came 70 years later.
115.upsc: Tests Marxist theory and its influence on 20th-century revolutions (Russia, China) and Indian socialist movements.

116.label: First girls' school in India
116.what: Savitribai Phule and Jyotirao Phule opened India's first school for girls in Pune on 1 January 1848. Savitribai became India's first woman teacher, defying violent opposition from those who considered women's education taboo.
116.connections: The Phule school predates most social reform legislation by decades. It is the earliest institutional challenge to the double exclusion of caste and gender. Jyotirao Phule was one of the strongest early influences on Ambedkar's thinking. The school is the practical complement to the Satyashodhak Samaj (1873), the ideological companion card. Pair with the Right to Education Act (2009) as the national-scale version of what the Phules started in a single Pune classroom.
116.remember: Savitribai is now officially recognised as India's first woman teacher. A university is named after her. She lived in the same century as Queen Victoria but achieved something Victoria never did: she personally taught girls from the lowest castes how to read, one by one, daily, against violent opposition.
116.upsc: Tests Savitribai Phule as a pioneer of women's education, the intersection of gender and caste in education reform, and her role alongside her husband in the broader Satyashodhak movement.

117.label: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
117.what: Bal Gangadhar Tilak (1856-1920) was a firebrand nationalist who popularised mass mobilisation through Ganesh Chaturthi and Shivaji festivals. He declared Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it.
117.connections: Tilak is the extremist counterpoint to Gokhale in the Congress split. His Ganesh Chaturthi mobilisation (1893) is the first use of mass public religion as nationalist politics. Compare with Gandhi's method: Tilak used confrontation; Gandhi used non-cooperation. The Surat Split (1907) is where the Tilak-Gokhale divide became institutional.
117.remember: Tilak started Ganesh Chaturthi as a public festival in 1893 to bring communities together for nationalist activities under the cover of religion. It became the template for mass mobilisation.
117.upsc: Tests the 'Lal-Bal-Pal' trio, the 'Home Rule League', and his use of Ganesh Chaturthi for mass mobilisation.

118.label: Widow Remarriage Act
118.what: The Hindu Widows Remarriage Act of 1856 legalised the remarriage of Hindu widows, largely due to the persistent campaigning of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar who collected thousands of signatures.
118.connections: The Widow Remarriage Act is the second step in the women's rights legislative sequence after Sati Abolition (1829). Vidyasagar's campaign also addressed widows' property rights: a woman who remarried lost her deceased husband's property, which he fought to change separately. This property dimension connects forward to the Hindu Code Bills (1955), which comprehensively addressed women's inheritance rights nearly a century later.
118.remember: Vidyasagar personally arranged and financed the first widow remarriage; he endured social boycott and death threats.
118.upsc: Tests Vidyasagar's role and the social context. Child widows were common due to child marriage. The Act faced fierce opposition from orthodox Hindus but was a landmark in women's rights.

119.label: First War of Independence
119.what: The Revolt of 1857 began at Meerut (10 May) when sepoys refused greased cartridges. They marched to Delhi and proclaimed Bahadur Shah Zafar Emperor.
119.connections: The revolt's chain is direct: 1857 → EIC dissolved (1858) → Crown Rule → Victoria's Proclamation promising non-interference → Indian Councils Act (1861, first nominated Indians). Also note the naming debate: 'Sepoy Mutiny' (British), 'First War of Independence' (Savarkar), 'Revolt of 1857' (NCERT standard). The name you use signals a political position.
119.remember: The cartridges were allegedly greased with cow and pig fat, deeply offensive to both Hindu and Muslim sepoys.
119.upsc: Tests causes (cartridges, Oudh, Doctrine of Lapse) and leaders: Laxmibai (Jhansi), Nana Sahib (Kanpur), Kunwar Singh (Bihar).

120.label: British Crown took over from EIC
120.what: After the 1857 revolt, the EIC was dissolved and India came under direct Crown Rule (1858). The Governor-General became the 'Viceroy'.
120.connections: Cards 66 and 253 cover the same event from two angles. This card is the political fact (EIC dissolved, Viceroy created), Card 253 is the institutional mechanism (Secretary of State replaces Board of Control). Together they explain how British India was governed for the next 90 years. The Indian Councils Act (1861) is the next step.
120.remember: Bahadur Shah Zafar was exiled to Rangoon; with his death in 1862, the 300-year-old Mughal dynasty officially ended.
120.upsc: Tests the 'Act for the Better Government of India' and Queen Victoria's Proclamation promising non-interference in religion.

121.label: Bipin Chandra Pal
121.what: Bipin Chandra Pal was the 'Father of Revolutionary Thought' in India, part of the Extremist trio Lal-Bal-Pal.
121.connections: Pal is the P in Lal-Bal-Pal, the extremist trio that dominated Congress from 1905 to 1907. His contribution is the articulation of Purna Swaraj (complete independence) as a goal, a full 23 years before Congress formally adopted it at Lahore (1929). The Surat Split (1907) is the event where the extremist-moderate divide became irreconcilable.
121.remember: Pal was one of the fiercest public speakers of his generation. He helped make nationalism sound bold, impatient, and mass-facing.
121.upsc: Tests his advocacy for 'Swadeshi' and total independence long before it became the Congress's official goal.

122.label: Government of India Act
122.what: The Government of India Act 1858 transferred all powers of the East India Company to the British Crown following the 1857 revolt. The Governor-General became the Viceroy, representing the Crown directly.
122.connections: The sequence of governance acts: Regulating Act (1773) → Pitt's India Act (1784) → Charter Acts → Government of India Act 1858 (this card, Crown takes over) → Indian Councils Acts (1861, 1892, 1909) → Montagu-Chelmsford (1919) → GOI Act 1935. Each one is a step in Parliament's evolving theory of how to govern India.
122.remember: The EIC was the most powerful corporation in history. It ruled 250 million people, had its own army, and minted its own coins. In 1858 it was dissolved by a single Act of Parliament. The world's most powerful company was wound up in an afternoon.
122.upsc: Tests the institutional changes: Secretary of State for India replaced Board of Control, India Council was advisory body, and Queen Victoria's Proclamation promised equality and non-interference in religion.

123.label: Indian Councils Act
123.what: The Indian Councils Act (1861) introduced legislative councils and the 'portfolio system', allowing Indians to be nominated for the first time.
123.connections: The ladder of representation: 1861 (Indians nominated, advisory only) → 1892 (elected element added) → 1909 Morley-Minto (separate electorates introduced) → 1919 Montagu-Chelmsford (Dyarchy, real transferred powers) → 1935 GOI Act (provincial autonomy). Each act moved the dial a little, never enough for nationalists and always too much for British conservatives.
123.remember: The portfolio system allowed individual members to take charge of specific departments, the ancestor of the modern cabinet.
123.upsc: Tests the sequence of acts (1861, 1892, 1909) as steps toward self-gov. Indians in 1861 had only advisory roles.

124.label: Rabindranath Tagore
124.what: Rabindranath Tagore (1861-1941) was a Bengali polymath who composed the national anthems of India (Jana Gana Mana) and Bangladesh (Amar Shonar Bangla) and became the first non-European Nobel laureate in literature in 1913.
124.connections: Tagore's Jana Gana Mana is India's national anthem; Amar Sonar Bangla is Bangladesh's. He returned his knighthood after Jallianwala Bagh (1919). His critique of nationalism as dangerous when exclusionary is relevant to every period that follows him. The Nobel Prize card (1913) is the companion; this birth card is the biographical anchor.
124.remember: Tagore wrote both India's and Bangladesh's national anthems. He also wrote the song that inspired Sri Lanka's national anthem. No other person has written national anthems for multiple countries.
124.upsc: Tests his Nobel Prize for 'Gitanjali' (1913) and his return of the knighthood after Jallianwala Bagh (1919).

125.label: Swami Vivekananda
125.what: Swami Vivekananda (1863–1902) introduced Vedanta and Yoga to the West, founding the Ramakrishna Mission in 1897.
125.connections: Pair with the Chicago speech card (1893) and the Ramakrishna Mission card (1897). All three together tell the complete Vivekananda story: this is the biographical context, 1893 is the event, 1897 is the institution. His prediction that he would not live to 40 and his death at 39 runs across all three cards.
125.remember: Vivekananda predicted he would not live to 40; he died at 39, having 'done the work of 300 years' in just 15.
125.upsc: Tests the Chicago speech, the Ramakrishna Mission, his philosophy of practical Vedanta, and his influence on nationalism. Netaji Bose and many revolutionaries cited Vivekananda as their inspiration.

126.label: Lala Lajpat Rai
126.what: Lala Lajpat Rai (1865–1928), the 'Lion of Punjab', died from injuries sustained during a protest against the Simon Commission.
126.connections: The L in Lal-Bal-Pal. His death from injuries sustained during the Simon Commission protest (1928) directly triggered Bhagat Singh's killing of Saunders. The chain: Lajpat Rai beaten, dies, Bhagat Singh retaliates, gets hanged. His death is the proximate cause of Bhagat Singh's most famous act.
126.remember: Lajpat Rai famously declared: 'Every blow struck on my body will be the last nail in the coffin of British imperialism.'
126.upsc: Tests his role in the Lal-Bal-Pal trio, founding the Punjab National Bank, and his influence on Bhagat Singh.

127.label: Dadabhai Naoroji's Drain Theory
127.what: Dadabhai Naoroji (1825-1917), called the Grand Old Man of India, presented his Drain Theory in 1867, demonstrating through statistical analysis that Britain was systematically extracting India's wealth through home charges, trade imbalances, and civil service salaries.
127.connections: The Drain Theory is the intellectual foundation for the economic critique of British rule. Naoroji calculated the annual extraction of Indian wealth to Britain, which demolished every argument that colonialism was beneficial. His election to the British Parliament in 1892 made him simultaneously a subject of the Crown and a legislator of it. Connect to the EIC Diwani rights (1765) as the mechanism that enabled the drain he quantified.
127.remember: Naoroji was elected to the British Parliament in 1892, representing Finsbury, London. A British MP calling for Indian independence while sitting in the House of Commons was so embarrassing to the British government that Lord Salisbury called him a black man. The insult backfired enormously.
127.upsc: Tests the Drain Theory as the first systematic economic critique of colonialism, Naoroji's Poverty and Un-British Rule in India (1901), and his election to the British Parliament (first Indian MP, 1892).

128.label: Suez Canal
128.what: The Suez Canal opened (1869), connecting the Mediterranean and Red Seas. It cut the Europe-India sea route by 7,000 km.
128.connections: The Suez Canal cut the Europe-India sea route by 7,000 km. The 1956 Suez Crisis (Nasser nationalising the canal) ended British and French dominance in the Middle East and established the US and USSR as the new superpowers. Nasser at Suez (1956) also validated non-alignment: a non-aligned country just defeated two colonial powers. India's support for Egypt during the crisis is the foreign policy connection.
128.remember: The 1956 crisis ended British/French dominance in the Middle East, establishing the US and USSR as the new superpowers.
128.upsc: Tests the Suez Crisis of 1956 when Egypt nationalised the canal, the British-French-Israeli invasion, and US and Soviet pressure that forced withdrawal. This was a key moment in India's Non-Aligned Movement.

129.label: Mahatma Gandhi
129.what: Mahatma Gandhi led India's independence movement through Satyagraha (non-violent resistance), pioneering techniques like fasting, hartals and non-cooperation that influenced civil rights movements worldwide.
129.connections: Gandhi's methods (Satyagraha, civil disobedience) appear across Colonial India: Champaran (1917), Non-Cooperation (1920), Salt March (1930), Quit India (1942). This birth card is the biographical anchor. His five Nobel nominations without a win is the detail the committee later acknowledged as an oversight. His assassination (1948) is in Post-Independence.
129.remember: Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize five times but never won. The committee later admitted this was a 'great omission'.
129.upsc: One of the most tested UPSC figures. Know his South Africa years, Champaran Satyagraha (1917), Non-Cooperation Movement (1920), Dandi March (1930), Quit India (1942), and assassination on 30 January 1948.

130.label: Unification of Germany
130.what: Germany was unified as the German Empire on 18 January 1871 at Versailles after Prussia's victory in the Franco-Prussian War. Otto von Bismarck became the first Chancellor of unified Germany.
130.connections: Bismarck declaring the German Empire in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles is the humiliation France never forgot, producing the revanchist sentiment that contributed to WWI (1914). The German Empire's founding also triggered the scramble for colonies (Berlin Conference 1884) as the new power sought overseas territories. The choice of venue for the declaration was Bismarck's deliberate insult.
130.remember: Bismarck declared the German Empire in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles, the palace of the French kings. The humiliation was deliberate. Fifty years later, France returned the favour by forcing Germany to sign the Treaty of Versailles in the same room.
130.upsc: Tests Bismarck's realpolitik, the balance of power in Europe that followed, and how German unification disturbed European stability and contributed to the alliance system that led to WWI.

131.label: Satyashodhak Samaj
131.what: Jyotirao Phule founded the Satyashodhak Samaj (Truth-Seeker's Society) in Pune in 1873, targeting Brahmanical dominance and caste hierarchy. He and his wife Savitribai Phule had already opened schools for girls and lower castes.
131.connections: The Satyashodhak Samaj is the caste-reform companion to the Brahmo Samaj's religious reform. Where Roy targeted practices, Phule targeted the Brahminical social structure directly. His work is the intellectual foundation for Ambedkar's Mahad Satyagraha (1927) and the anti-caste movement of the 20th century. The Phule-to-Ambedkar intellectual line is the most important continuity in Indian social reform history.
131.remember: Savitribai Phule was pelted with stones and dung on her way to teach girls every morning. She carried a spare sari to change into after reaching school. She is now on a postage stamp. The people who pelted her are forgotten.
131.upsc: Tests Phule's Gulamgiri (Slavery), the first school for girls opened by Savitribai Phule in 1848, and Phule's connection to both Ambedkar's anti-caste movement and the Dravidian movement.

132.label: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
132.what: Sardar Patel (1875–1950) was the 'Iron Man of India' who integrated 562 princely states into the Indian Union.
132.connections: Patel's singular achievement is the integration of 562 princely states (Operation Polo 1948, Junagadh 1948). His relationship with Nehru is the central tension of early Indian governance: Patel's realism versus Nehru's idealism on China, on Pakistan, on economic policy. He died in 1950; historians debate whether he would have handled China differently before the 1962 war.
132.remember: Patel integrated 562 princely states in under a year, an act of political consolidation with no parallel in history. He said he used the same technique as Bismarck: blood and iron, though he preferred negotiation.
132.upsc: Tests his role as Deputy PM, integration of Hyderabad/Junagadh, and his status as the 'Patron Saint of Civil Servants'.

133.label: Arya Samaj
133.what: Swami Dayananda Saraswati (1824–1883) founded the Arya Samaj (1875), rejecting idol worship and caste based on birth.
133.connections: The Arya Samaj and Brahmo Samaj (1828) represent the two dominant approaches to 19th century Hindu reform: indigenous authority (Vedic) versus Western rationalism. The Arya Samaj's Shuddhi (reconversion) movement and its later associations with Hindu nationalism make it a more politically complex card than the Brahmo Samaj. Dayananda's back-to-the-Vedas argument also directly influenced Tilak and the Bal-Pal-Lal nationalist tradition.
133.remember: Unlike the Brahmo Samaj, the Arya Samaj sought reform entirely from within the authority of the Vedas.
133.upsc: Tests the 'Shuddhi' movement and the 'Back to the Vedas' slogan. He wrote 'Satyarth Prakash'.

134.label: Aligarh Muslim University
134.what: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan founded the Aligarh Movement and the M.A.O. College (1875) to promote modern education for Muslims.
134.connections: Sir Syed's Aligarh Movement is the Muslim parallel to the Hindu reform movements of the same period. His approach shaped the Muslim political class that eventually supported the Muslim League. The Aligarh intellectual tradition is the origin point for Muslim separatist politics as much as it is an educational reform. Compare with Brahmo Samaj and Arya Samaj as contemporaneous but differently oriented movements: all three seek modern education within a religious identity.
134.remember: The M.A.O. College became Aligarh Muslim University in 1920; it remains a center of modern Islamic thought.
134.upsc: Tests his belief that Muslims had to embrace English education to progress and his eventual 'Two-Nation' stance.

135.label: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
135.what: Muhammad Ali Jinnah (1876-1948) was a major political leader of late colonial India who moved from being associated with Hindu-Muslim unity to becoming the central political force behind the demand for Pakistan.
135.connections: Jinnah's arc runs from Congress member to Muslim League leader to Pakistan's founder. He was personally secular while leading a religious-identity political movement, which is the central paradox his card raises. The Congress-League cooperation ended with the 1916 Lucknow Pact as their last joint effort. Direct Action Day (1946) and Partition (1947) are the concluding Colonial India cards.
135.remember: Jinnah was a secular-minded lawyer who rarely practiced religious rituals, yet he led the movement for a state based on Islamic identity.
135.upsc: Tests his evolution from 'Ambassador of Unity' to the 1940 Lahore Resolution and the demand for Partition.

136.label: Vernacular Press Act
136.what: Viceroy Lytton passed the Vernacular Press Act in 1878 to muzzle Indian language newspapers that were criticising British policies. It allowed magistrates to seize printing presses without trial.
136.connections: Lytton passed two repressive acts in 1878: the Vernacular Press Act and the Arms Act. Both were repealed within a few years as counterproductive. The backlash they created accelerated the Ilbert Bill controversy (1883) and the founding of the INC (1885). Repression built the nationalist movement it was designed to prevent.
136.remember: The Act was called the Gagging Act by Indians. Amrita Bazar Patrika, a Bengali paper targeted by the Act, converted to English overnight to escape its provisions. The British were outmanoeuvred by a printing press decision made in one evening.
136.upsc: Tests the press freedom issue in colonial India, the Act's repeal in 1882 by Ripon, and the contrast with how English language papers (like The Times of India) were treated differently.

137.label: Arms Act
137.what: Viceroy Lytton's Arms Act of 1878 prohibited Indians from possessing arms without a licence, while exempting Europeans. It was a direct response to fears of another 1857-type uprising.
137.connections: Both acts passed in 1878, both repealed quickly. The Vernacular Press Act went in 1882, the Arms Act later. The pattern repeats through colonial history: repressive legislation creates the grievance that feeds the movement it was meant to suppress. These two acts are part of what made the INC's founding in 1885 feel necessary.
137.remember: The Arms Act and the Vernacular Press Act were passed in the same year by the same Viceroy. One silenced Indian voices, the other disarmed Indian bodies. Both were repealed within a few years as counterproductive. But the resentment they created lasted generations.
137.upsc: Tests alongside the Vernacular Press Act (same year, same Viceroy) as examples of repressive Lytton-era legislation. Also tested in context of Indian resentment that fuelled the early nationalist movement.

138.label: Hunter Commission on education
138.what: The Hunter Commission of 1882 reviewed the state of education in India following Wood's Despatch of 1854. It recommended expansion of primary education and greater role for private enterprise in higher education.
138.connections: Education policy is a thread running through colonial India: Macaulay's Minute (1835, English education) → Wood's Despatch (1854, university system) → Hunter Commission (1882, primary neglect) → Sadler Commission (1917). Each report corrected the previous one's blind spots. The debate about what Indians should be taught, and in which language, never really ended.
138.remember: Wood's Despatch is called the Magna Carta of Indian education. The Hunter Commission followed up by pointing out its failures , primary education had been neglected for 28 years while higher education expanded. The same debate continues in independent India.
138.upsc: Tests the sequence of education policy milestones: Macaulay's Minute (1835), Wood's Despatch (1854), Hunter Commission (1882), Sadler Commission (1917-19). Each shifted priorities in colonial education policy.

139.label: Ilbert Bill controversy
139.what: Viceroy Ripon's Ilbert Bill proposed allowing Indian judges to try European offenders in criminal cases. The European community in India launched a fierce campaign against it, forcing a diluted version.
139.connections: The Ilbert Bill controversy is the direct trigger for organized Indian nationalism. Surendranath Banerjee said explicitly that the European backlash convinced him Indians needed their own political organisation. The INC was founded two years later. Cause and effect, two years apart.
139.remember: The European backlash against the Ilbert Bill shocked Indians. It made clear that the British in India would never accept legal equality with Indians. Surendranath Banerjee called it the turning point that made Indians realise they needed a political organisation. The INC was founded two years later.
139.upsc: Tests the racial hierarchy embedded in colonial law, the European opposition that revealed the limits of reform, and how the controversy radicalised Indian opinion and gave momentum to the early Congress.

140.label: Berlin Conference: Africa partitioned
140.what: The Berlin Conference (1884–85) saw European powers divide Africa among themselves without any African representation.
140.connections: The Berlin Conference is the single most consequential event for Africa in the 19th century. No African was present. The straight-line borders of African nations today are the cartographic legacy of European rulers drawing on maps they had never visited. Compare with the partition of India (1947): both involve lines drawn by external powers with consequences still felt today. The scramble for Africa completion card (1914) is the follow-on.
140.remember: By 1914, 90% of Africa was under European control; before the conference, it was only 10%.
140.upsc: Tests the 'Scramble for Africa' and the artificial borders that still cause ethnic conflicts in African nations today.

141.label: Indian National Congress
141.what: The Indian National Congress (INC) was founded (1885) by A.O. Hume. Womesh Bonerjee was the first president at the Bombay session.
141.connections: The early INC (1885-1905) was moderate: petitions, resolutions, prayers. The Partition of Bengal (1905) fractured it: Extremists (Lal-Bal-Pal) wanted confrontation; Moderates wanted negotiation. The Surat Split (1907) made the break formal. Gandhi reunified the Congress after 1920, but on completely different terms: mass civil resistance, not petitions.
141.remember: The first session had only 72 delegates. Hume, a retired British officer, hoped it would prevent another 1857-style revolt.
141.upsc: Tests the 'Safety Valve' theory and the early moderate phase (petitions/prayers) versus later extremism.

142.label: Sudharak
142.what: Gopal Ganesh Agarkar (1856-1895) founded the Marathi journal Sudharak in 1888, arguing that social reform must precede political freedom. He was Tilak's co-founder of the New English School but disagreed sharply on the priority of reform vs. independence.
142.connections: Agarkar is the forgotten figure between Phule and Tilak. His argument that social reform must precede political independence versus Tilak's position that independence must come first is the defining debate of late 19th-century Maharashtrian politics. The two positions represent two theories of change that recur throughout Indian history: change the society first, or change the power structure first.
142.remember: Agarkar and Tilak founded a school together, lived in the same house, and became opponents. Agarkar said: what use is independence if casteism remains? Tilak said: what use is reform without freedom? Both were right. Independent India is still having this argument.
142.upsc: Tests the Agarkar-Tilak debate as the defining 19th century argument in Indian public life: social reform first (Agarkar) or political freedom first (Tilak). This debate influenced Gandhi and continues in modern Indian public discourse.

143.label: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad
143.what: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad (1888-1958) became Congress President at 35, the youngest ever, and served twice. He fiercely opposed partition, arguing that Islam was as much at home in India as Hinduism. He became India's first Education Minister.
143.connections: Azad's prediction that partition would harm Muslims who chose Pakistan is the most prescient political forecast of the independence era. His role as independent India's first Education Minister connects to the Right to Education arc. He is the symbolic representative of the Muslim who chose India over Pakistan, which is why he remains politically significant beyond his ministerial record.
143.remember: Azad predicted that partition would be a tragedy for Muslims. He said Muslims who went to Pakistan would be second class citizens in a Muslim country, while Muslims who stayed in India would be treated with suspicion. He was correct on both counts. His book India Wins Freedom was released 30 years after his death.
143.upsc: Tests his role in the Khilafat Movement, his opposition to partition alongside Gandhi, his 6-year Congress presidency (1940-1946), and his role in establishing IITs and founding the UGC as Education Minister.

144.label: Jawaharlal Nehru
144.what: Jawaharlal Nehru (1889–1964) was India's first PM, architect of the modern state, and a leader of the Non-Aligned Movement.
144.connections: Nehru's fingerprints are on everything in Post-Independence until 1964: Five-Year Plans (1951), Non-Aligned Movement (1961), Sino-Indian War (1962). The Tryst with Destiny speech (1947) is the most associated text. His relationship with Patel and his trust in China are the two judgments history most debates. His daughter Indira and grandson Rajiv follow him in the PM sequence.
144.remember: Nehru's 'Tryst with Destiny' speech is considered one of the 20th century's most iconic pieces of political oratory.
144.upsc: Tests his socialist model, Five-Year Plans, and the failure of the 1962 war which damaged his legacy.

145.label: Age of Consent Act
145.what: The Age of Consent Act (1891) raised the age for girls from 10 to 12. It sparked a fierce debate between reformers and traditionalists.
145.connections: Tilak's opposition to the Age of Consent Act is the moment social reform and political nationalism split. He believed no British-imposed change was legitimate, even correct ones. That position defines the Extremist school. Gandhi later resolved this tension by making social reform and political resistance inseparable parts of the same movement.
145.remember: The act was passed after the 'Phulmoni case', where a 10-year-old girl died from injuries inflicted by her adult husband.
145.upsc: Tests social reform tension. Tilak opposed the act as British interference in Hindu customs, despite favoring reform himself.

146.label: B.R. Ambedkar
146.what: B.R. Ambedkar (1891-1956) was the principal architect of India's Constitution and one of the foremost leaders of Dalit rights. He converted to Buddhism in 1956 in a mass ceremony at Nagpur shortly before his death.
146.connections: Ambedkar's arc spans Social Reform (Mahad 1927), Constitutional (Drafting Committee 1947, resignation 1951), and this Personalities card. The conversion to Buddhism (1956) is the companion card. His three doctorates from Columbia and LSE make him the most credentialed Indian leader of his era. He is the figure who connects more categories than any other in the game.
146.remember: Ambedkar had three doctorates from Columbia and LSE; he was the most educated Indian leader of his time.
146.upsc: Tests his role in the Drafting Committee, the Poona Pact, and his mass conversion to Buddhism in 1956.

147.label: Swami Vivekananda's Chicago speech
147.what: Swami Vivekananda's speech at the Parliament of the World's Religions in Chicago on 11 September 1893, beginning with 'Sisters and Brothers of America', brought him international recognition and introduced many Western audiences to Vedanta and Hindu thought.
147.connections: The Chicago speech is the moment Hinduism became a globally legible spiritual tradition. Pair with Ramakrishna Mission founding (1897) as cause and institutional consequence. The 'Sisters and Brothers of America' opening broke formal protocol and produced the standing ovation before he had said anything substantive. Vivekananda birth card (1863) is the biographical anchor.
147.remember: He began with 'Sisters and Brothers of America', a greeting that resonated deeply with his Western audience.
147.upsc: Tests the impact of the speech on India's global image and the founding of Vedanta societies in the West.

148.label: Subhas Chandra Bose
148.what: Subhas Chandra Bose (1897–1945) led the INA and the Azad Hind government, seeking independence through armed struggle.
148.connections: Bose represents the third path to independence: not Gandhi's non-violence nor Tilak's mass agitation but armed military struggle through the INA. The Azad Hind government (1943) in Singapore is the parallel government he established. The Rani of Jhansi Regiment is the detail most worth remembering for its historical novelty. His mysterious 1945 death makes him the most politically unresolved figure of the independence movement.
148.remember: Bose's 'Rani of Jhansi Regiment' was the first all-women combat unit in Asia, breaking deep gender taboos.
148.upsc: Tests the INA, the Azad Hind Fauj, the Red Fort trials, Bose's split with Gandhi over methods, his escape from India and journey to Germany and Japan, and the circumstances of his reported death.

149.label: Ramakrishna Mission
149.what: Swami Vivekananda founded the Ramakrishna Mission (1897) for spiritual and social service, based on his guru's teachings.
149.connections: The Ramakrishna Mission introduced social service as spiritual practice, distinct from purely devotional or intellectual approaches to reform. It is the organised expression of what Bhakti reformers had argued centuries earlier. Compare with the Servants of India Society (1905) as a secular version of the same impulse: both trained dedicated individuals for public service, one through a spiritual framework, one through a professional one.
149.remember: Vivekananda was the first monk to cross the oceans; he broke the 'Kala Pani' taboo to represent Hinduism abroad.
149.upsc: Tests Vivekananda's 1893 Chicago speech at the Parliament of World Religions which brought international attention to Hinduism. UPSC connects his ideas to religious nationalism and social reform.

150.label: Partition of Bengal
150.what: Lord Curzon partitioned Bengal (1905) into East (Muslim majority) and West (Hindu majority), sparking massive anti-British protests.
150.connections: The Partition of Bengal (1905) and its annulment (1911) are a single political story across six years. The partition triggered the Swadeshi Movement; the annulment proved mass agitation works. That lesson of 1911 directly fuelled nationalist confidence for the next decade. Pair this card with Card 257 to read the full arc.
150.remember: Bengal was the heart of Indian nationalism; Curzon hoped partitioning it would shatter the unity of the independence movement.
150.upsc: Tests the 'Divide and Rule' motive vs. Curzon's 'administrative efficiency' excuse. It triggered the Swadeshi Movement.

151.label: Swadeshi Movement
151.what: The Swadeshi Movement (1905) called for the boycott of British goods and the promotion of Indian products and education.
151.connections: The Swadeshi Movement is the prototype for everything Gandhi scaled up. Boycott of British goods (1905) → Non-Cooperation boycotts (1920) → Civil Disobedience (1930). The methods are continuous; the geographical scale and mass participation increase each time. Gandhi didn't invent these tactics. He nationalised and disciplined ones already in use.
151.remember: Public bonfires of British cloth became a symbol of resistance. Gandhi later refined these methods for national use.
151.upsc: Tests the first mass political movement and the rise of the 'Lal-Bal-Pal' extremist trio in the Congress.

152.label: Servants of India Society
152.what: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded the Servants of India Society in 1905, training Indians to dedicate their lives to public service through selfless work across education, healthcare and social causes.
152.connections: Note: card #341 also covers this same society and year. One should be merged. Gokhale's influence on Gandhi makes this card the direct link between the moderate nationalist tradition and Gandhi's own approach. Compare with the Ramakrishna Mission (1897): both train dedicated individuals for service, one spiritually motivated, one professionally motivated. Gokhale is also the moderate counterpoint to Tilak in the Congress split, which connects this card to the Colonial India category.
152.remember: Gandhi spent a year traveling through India after his return in 1915, following Gokhale's advice to 'know the land first'.
152.upsc: Tests his role as Gandhi's political mentor and his moderate approach versus Tilak's extremism.

153.label: Muslim League
153.what: The All India Muslim League was founded at Dhaka (1906). Its initial goal was protecting Muslim political interests under British rule.
153.connections: Track the League across the deck: founded (1906) → separate electorates won (1909) → brief unity with INC at Lucknow (1916) → Congress refuses coalition (1937) → Lahore Resolution demanding Pakistan (1940) → Direct Action Day (1946). The arc from 'Muslim interests' to 'separate nation' takes 40 years and runs through every major colonial event.
153.remember: The League was founded specifically to counter the INC's claim to represent all Indians, a key part of British 'Divide and Rule'.
153.upsc: Tests the League's evolution from pro-British loyalty to the demand for Pakistan in 1940. Aga Khan III was its first president.

154.label: Bhagat Singh
154.what: Bhagat Singh (1907–1931) was a revolutionary socialist who was hanged at 23 for the Lahore Conspiracy Case.
154.connections: Bhagat Singh was hanged at 23, directly following the chain from Lajpat Rai's death. His reading Lenin on his last night distinguishes him from a purely nationalist martyr: he was a socialist internationalist who saw Indian independence as part of a larger class struggle. Compare with Gandhi's non-violence: same British regime, two completely different theories of how to end it.
154.remember: He spent his last night in jail reading a book by Lenin. He asked his executioners to let him finish the chapter before they took him.
154.upsc: Tests his 'HSRA' role, his pamphlet 'Why I am an Atheist', and his push for political prisoner status for revolutionaries.

155.label: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
155.what: Bal Gangadhar Tilak was sentenced to six years imprisonment in Mandalay (Burma) in 1908 for writing articles deemed to encourage violence against the British after the Muzaffarpur bomb case.
155.connections: Tilak's imprisonment marks the peak of the Extremist phase. He returned from Mandalay in 1914, co-authored the Lucknow Pact in 1916, and died in 1920, the year Gandhi took over the Congress. The six-day Bombay general strike in response to his arrest was the first time Indian workers used mass industrial action as political protest.
155.remember: The workers of Bombay went on a six-day general strike spontaneously to protest Tilak's arrest. No one organised it. It emerged organically. It was the first time Indian workers used mass industrial action as a political tool.
155.upsc: Tests Section 124A (sedition) of the Indian Penal Code which was used against Tilak, and the public response including a six-day general strike in Bombay, the first general strike in Indian history.

156.label: Morley-Minto Reforms
156.what: The Morley-Minto Reforms (1909) introduced separate electorates for Muslims, allowing them to vote for reserved Muslim seats.
156.connections: The separate electorates introduced here are the most consequential institutional decision of the colonial period. They formalised communal identity as the basis of political representation. Every Congress-League tension from this point (Lucknow Pact, Communal Award, Poona Pact, partition) flows through this 1909 design.
156.remember: Morley stated he didn't want parliamentary gov for India, yet these reforms set the stage for democratic competition along religious lines.
156.upsc: Tests the institutionalisation of communalism. Lord Minto is called the 'Father of the Communal Electorate'.

157.label: Capital shifted from Calcutta to Delhi
157.what: King George V moved India's capital from Calcutta to Delhi (1911) during the Delhi Durbar, also annulling the Partition of Bengal.
157.connections: Delhi as capital reconnected the British Raj to Mughal imperial symbolism. It was the same city Bahadur Shah Zafar had ruled in name during 1857, now recast as the seat of British power. New Delhi took 20 years to build; it was inaugurated in 1931, sixteen years before the British left from the same city.
157.remember: The shift took 20 years to complete; New Delhi was inaugurated in 1931, shortly before the British would leave for good.
157.upsc: Tests the symbolic importance of Delhi as the old Mughal capital and the commissioning of Lutyens to design 'New Delhi'.

158.label: Curzon's Bengal Partition
158.what: King George V announced the annulment of the Bengal Partition at the Delhi Durbar of 1911, reuniting Bengal. The capital was simultaneously moved from Calcutta to Delhi. Both were presented as royal gifts.
158.connections: Pair with Card 70 (Partition of Bengal, 1905). That card is the partition; this is the annulment. Together they're a six-year story of British policy reversal under mass pressure. Nationalists drew an exact lesson: sustained agitation can change British policy. That lesson directly feeds the confidence of the Non-Cooperation Movement nine years later.
158.remember: The partition had been announced with great fanfare in 1905. Its annulment six years later was a humiliation for the British who had insisted it was an administrative necessity. It proved that sustained mass agitation could reverse British policy.
158.upsc: Tests the two announcements at the same Durbar: Bengal reunification and capital shift to Delhi. The British framed annulment as a generous concession, not an admission of defeat. UPSC asks about both in the same question.

159.label: Rabindranath Tagore won Nobel Prize in Literature
159.what: Tagore's Nobel Prize (1913) for 'Gitanjali' made him the first Asian and first non-European to win in literature.
159.connections: The Nobel Prize made Tagore the first Indian to achieve global literary recognition on Western terms. W.B. Yeats wrote the introduction to Gitanjali that brought it to Western attention. The irony: Tagore himself distrusted nationalism and returned his knighthood after Jallianwala Bagh. An internationally celebrated figure who was ambivalent about the nationalist project his celebrity helped legitimise.
159.remember: W.B. Yeats wrote the famous introduction to the English translation of 'Gitanjali', helping it gain global fame.
159.upsc: Tests the impact on Indian pride and Tagore's return of his knighthood in 1919 in protest of Jallianwala Bagh.

160.label: First World War
160.what: World War I began after Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary was assassinated in Sarajevo on 28 June 1914 by Gavrilo Princip. The alliance system dragged most of Europe into war within six weeks.
160.connections: India's contribution to WWI (1.5 million soldiers, 74,000 killed) was the argument that fuelled post-war nationalist expectations. When Britain did not grant self-government after the war, the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms were seen as inadequate. Jallianwala Bagh (1919) followed. The broken promise of post-war recognition is the direct cause of the shift from moderate to radical Indian nationalism.
160.remember: India sent 1.5 million soldiers to fight in WWI for the British. Indians fought in Gallipoli, Mesopotamia and the Western Front. Britain promised India greater self-governance in return. They later reneged with the Rowlatt Act.
160.upsc: Tests the causes (MAIN: Militarism, Alliances, Imperialism, Nationalism), the role of the Ottoman Empire, Indian soldiers in WWI, the Khilafat Movement, and the war's impact on India's independence movement.

161.label: Scramble for Africa
161.what: By 1914 European powers had colonised 90% of Africa, up from 10% in 1880, following the Berlin Conference of 1884-85. Only Ethiopia and Liberia remained independent.
161.connections: Pair with the Berlin Conference (1884) as beginning and completion. Ethiopia's defeat of Italy at Adwa (1896) is the sole exception to complete colonisation. Compare with India's experience: both India and Africa were colonised by the same European powers in overlapping periods using the same justifications. The decolonisation wave (1960) is the World History card that closes the colonial arc.
161.remember: Ethiopia defeated Italy at the Battle of Adwa in 1896, the only African country to defeat a European colonial army in the Scramble era. Emperor Haile Selassie later became a symbol of African resistance. Ethiopia's independence inspired pan-Africanism globally.
161.upsc: Tests the consequences of the Berlin Conference, the artificial borders that cause current African conflicts, and the comparison with Indian decolonisation as part of the global wave after WWII.

162.label: Lucknow Pact
162.what: The Lucknow Pact (1916) was an agreement between the INC and Muslim League; the INC accepted separate electorates in exchange for League support.
162.connections: Jinnah in 1916 was called the 'Ambassador of Hindu-Muslim Unity'. Track him forward: Simon Commission boycott (1928), rejection of Nehru Report (1928), Lahore Resolution for Pakistan (1940), Direct Action Day (1946). The arc from unity to partition runs through almost every major event in this category.
162.remember: The pact was short-lived; the unity faded after the Khilafat and Non-Cooperation movements ended in the early 1920s.
162.upsc: Tests a rare moment of unity. Jinnah was then called the 'Ambassador of Hindu-Muslim Unity'. Tilak and Jinnah were key architects.

163.label: Home Rule League
163.what: Annie Besant (1847-1933), an Irish activist and Theosophist leader, founded the Indian Home Rule League in 1916, advocating for Indian self-governance within the British Empire. She was also the first woman Congress president (1917).
163.connections: Besant confounds every expected category: Irish-Theosophist-Indian nationalist. Her Home Rule League (1916) was the institutional predecessor to the full independence demand. She was interned by the British in 1917, which paradoxically increased Congress support for her. She occupies the middle ground between Gokhale's gradualism and Tilak's confrontation.
163.remember: Annie Besant was an Irish woman who became more Indian than most Indians. She learned Sanskrit, translated Hindu scriptures, and fought for Indian independence. She was interned by the British in 1917 for her activities. The internment made her a nationalist hero overnight.
163.upsc: Tests Besant alongside Tilak (who founded a parallel Home Rule League the same year), her role as first woman Congress president, and the Home Rule Movement as a bridge between the moderate and extremist phases of Congress.

164.label: Balfour Declaration
164.what: The Balfour Declaration of 1917 was a letter from British Foreign Secretary Arthur Balfour to Zionist leader Walter Rothschild, expressing British support for a Jewish national home in Palestine.
164.connections: The Balfour Declaration promised a Jewish homeland in territory Britain did not own, to a Zionist leader, in a letter. The simultaneous promise to protect non-Jewish rights was structurally impossible to keep alongside the first promise. Every subsequent Arab-Israeli conflict traces back to the contradiction in this single document. It is the founding text of the modern Middle East's central conflict.
164.remember: The letter promised a Jewish home while *also* promising to protect non-Jewish rights, two goals that proved irreconcilable.
164.upsc: Tests the Declaration's role in the creation of Israel in 1948, the Arab-Israeli conflict, and India's position (support for Palestinian statehood). UPSC international relations frequently covers the Israel-Palestine issue.

165.label: Russian Revolution
165.what: The Russian Revolution of 1917 began with the February Revolution that overthrew the Tsar, followed by the October Revolution when Lenin's Bolsheviks seized power and established the world's first communist state.
165.connections: The Russian Revolution occurred in two stages: the February Revolution overthrew the Tsar; the October/Bolshevik Revolution replaced the Provisional Government with Lenin's Bolsheviks. Germany provided Lenin a sealed train back to Russia because they hoped the revolution would end Russian participation in WWI. It did. The Bolshevik seizure of power was announced by a single gunshot from the cruiser Aurora. The USSR that resulted lasted 74 years (1917-1991).
165.remember: Lenin returned to Russia from exile in a sealed train provided by Germany. Germany hoped the revolution would take Russia out of WWI. It did. The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk ended Russian participation in 1918. The revolution was announced by a single gunshot from the cruiser Aurora. Lenin had been sent back to Russia by Germany in a sealed train to destabilise the Russian war effort. It was the most consequential train journey in history.
165.upsc: Tests the Bolshevik-Menshevik split and the revolution's influence on Indian socialists like Nehru and Bhagat Singh.

166.label: Champaran Satyagraha
166.what: Gandhi led the Champaran Satyagraha in Bihar in 1917 against the tinkathia system, which forced indigo farmers to cultivate indigo on 3/20 of their land for European planters at fixed prices. It was Gandhi's first major civil disobedience in India.
166.connections: Champaran, Kheda, and the Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) are Gandhi's three Indian apprenticeships: all local, all successful, all before the national scale of Non-Cooperation (1920). Three consecutive years, three different grievances, one method refined each time. Champaran gave Gandhi his Indian debut; Kheda gave him Sardar Patel.
166.remember: Gandhi was ordered to leave Champaran by the British Collector. He refused. He said he recognised the legitimacy of the order but would disobey it as a matter of conscience. The Collector backed down. It was the first time British authority blinked before Gandhi.
166.upsc: Tests this as Gandhi's first application of Satyagraha on Indian soil after South Africa, the tinkathia system, and the Champaran Agrarian Act 1918 that resulted from it.

167.label: Kheda Satyagraha
167.what: Gandhi led the Kheda Satyagraha in 1918 in Gujarat, where farmers demanded suspension of land revenue after crop failure. The government eventually instructed officials to recover taxes only from those who could afford to pay.
167.connections: Champaran (1917), Kheda (1918), Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918): Gandhi's three consecutive Indian apprenticeships, all local, all won. Each refined the method before the national scale of Non-Cooperation (1920). Kheda specifically proved that rural tax resistance could work, a lesson Gandhi applied again and again across the next 30 years.
167.remember: Kheda was where Sardar Patel first worked with Gandhi. Patel had been a successful barrister. After Kheda, he gave it all up for the independence movement. The Iron Man of India was forged in a farmers' tax dispute in Gujarat.
167.upsc: Tests it alongside Champaran (1917) and Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) as Gandhi's early Satyagrahas in India before the national Non-Cooperation Movement. Vallabhbhai Patel was Gandhi's key lieutenant in Kheda.

168.label: Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms
168.what: The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms (1919) introduced 'Dyarchy' in provinces: subjects were split into 'Transferred' and 'Reserved'.
168.connections: The constitutional ladder: Morley-Minto (1909) → Montagu-Chelmsford (1919, Dyarchy) → Simon Commission (1928, 10-year review promised here) → GOI Act 1935 (provincial autonomy). The Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms and the Rowlatt Act were both passed in 1919. One promised more self-governance; the other allowed indefinite detention. Both simultaneously.
