168.remember: Dyarchy failed because the British kept 'Reserved' powers (Law, Finance) while giving Indians 'Transferred' ones (Education, Health).
168.upsc: Tests Dyarchy and the introduction of a bicameral legislature at the centre. It also promised a review in 10 years (Simon Commission).

169.label: Rowlatt Act
169.what: The Rowlatt Act (1919) allowed detention without trial. Gandhi launched the Rowlatt Satyagraha in protest, his first all-India movement.
169.connections: The Rowlatt Act and the Montagu-Chelmsford Reforms passed in the same year. One extended representation, one allowing indefinite detention without trial. The contradiction handed Gandhi his first all-India platform: the Rowlatt Satyagraha. Jallianwala Bagh followed three months after the Act was passed.
169.remember: The popular slogan was 'No Dalil, No Vakil, No Appeal.' It showed that British promises of post-war reform were hollow.
169.upsc: Tests the 'Black Act' and its link to Jallianwala Bagh. Gandhi suspended the Satyagraha in April 1919 due to violence.

170.label: Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
170.what: The Jallianwala Bagh Massacre (13 April 1919) saw General Dyer order firing on a peaceful crowd in Amritsar, killing hundreds.
170.connections: Three responses to Jallianwala Bagh define three different political temperaments in this deck: Tagore returned his knighthood (moral witness), Gandhi launched Non-Cooperation (mass political resistance), Bhagat Singh watched the soil being collected and chose revolution. All three responses are in this game. All three are consequences of the same afternoon.
170.remember: Dyer blocked the only exit before firing. He faced zero charges in Britain and was even praised by the House of Lords.
170.upsc: Tests the date (Baisakhi), Tagore's return of his knighthood, and the Hunter Commission's failure to punish Dyer.

171.label: Khilafat Movement
171.what: The Khilafat Movement (1919–22) was led by the Ali Brothers to protect the Ottoman Caliphate. Gandhi supported it to unite Hindus and Muslims.
171.connections: The Khilafat Movement is Gandhi's attempt to build the Hindu-Muslim unity the Lucknow Pact promised. It worked briefly: the two movements ran together 1920-22. When Turkey abolished the Caliphate in 1924, the religious basis collapsed. Communal tensions returned sharper than before. The failure fed the divergence that eventually produced partition.
171.remember: Indians fought to protect the Turkish Sultan-Caliph, but the Turks, under Mustafa Kemal, eventually abolished the office themselves.
171.upsc: Tests Gandhi's strategy and why the movement collapsed when Turkey itself abolished the Caliphate in 1924.

172.label: Treaty of Versailles
172.what: The Treaty of Versailles (1919) ended WWI but imposed harsh reparations and 'War Guilt' on Germany, fueling the rise of Hitler.
172.connections: Versailles created the conditions for WWII. Keynes's resignation and prediction that the 'Carthaginian Peace' would destroy the European economy was precisely right. The war guilt clause and reparations gave Hitler his political platform. Compare with the Peace of Westphalia (1648): both ended major European wars, but Westphalia was designed to be sustainable; Versailles was designed to punish, and punished the wrong generation.
172.remember: The economist J.M. Keynes resigned in protest, warning that this 'Carthaginian Peace' would destroy the European economy. He was right.
172.upsc: Tests the 'League of Nations' and Article 231 (War Guilt Clause). Reparations were set at 132 billion gold marks.

173.label: Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
173.what: On 13 April 1919 (Baisakhi), General Dyer ordered troops to fire on a peaceful crowd at Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar. Official count 379 dead, actual estimates exceed 1000. Dyer blocked the only exit before ordering firing.
173.connections: Udham Singh witnessed the massacre as a teenager. He waited 21 years, traveled to London, and shot Michael O'Dwyer (the Lieutenant Governor who had backed Dyer) in 1940. The longest-running act of revenge in the independence movement. Dyer himself had died in 1927; O'Dwyer paid the price instead.
173.remember: Dyer was not punished. The House of Lords praised him. The British public raised over 26,000 pounds for him. Rabindranath Tagore wrote to the Viceroy: I wish to stand shorn of all special distinctions, by the side of those of my countrymen who have been humiliated.
173.upsc: Tests the date (Baisakhi), the Hunter Commission findings, Udham Singh's revenge killing of Michael O'Dwyer in London in 1940, and Tagore returning his knighthood in protest.

174.label: Non-Cooperation Movement
174.what: Gandhi launched the Non-Cooperation Movement (1920), calling for the boycott of British schools, courts, and goods. It was the first mass civil disobedience.
174.connections: The trigger was Jallianwala Bagh; the end was Chauri Chaura. Between them, the Non-Cooperation Movement showed mass resistance was possible at national scale. The critical distinction to hold: Non-Cooperation (1920) means refusing to participate; Civil Disobedience (1930) means actively breaking specific laws. Eight years apart. Not interchangeable.
174.remember: Students left British schools and lawyers gave up practice; the British felt genuinely threatened by the scale of the defiance.
174.upsc: Tests Gandhi's methods and the massive participation. It was fueled by anger over Jallianwala Bagh and the Rowlatt Act.

175.label: League of Nations
175.what: The League of Nations (est. 1920) was the first global peace body. It failed because the US never joined and it lacked an army.
175.connections: The League of Nations was the first global peace organisation. The US never joined (Senate rejected it twice), fatally weakening it from birth. India was a founding member despite being a British colony: it voted, sent delegates, and signed treaties as a sovereign state, which gave Indian nationalists both a platform and a precedent. The League failed to prevent WWII; its successor UN (1945) learned its lessons by adding the Security Council veto to give great powers a reason to stay.
175.remember: The US Senate rejected joining the League twice because senators feared it would drag America into foreign wars. Wilson suffered a stroke while campaigning for the League and died before seeing it fail. India sat at the League of Nations as a colony. It voted, sent delegates, and signed treaties as if it were a country. It was simultaneously a subject nation and an international actor. This created the legal precedent for India's immediate full UN membership at independence.
175.upsc: Tests its failures in the 1930s (Manchuria, Ethiopia) and how it led to the creation of the more powerful UN.

176.label: Chauri Chaura incident
176.what: The Chauri Chaura incident (1922) saw a mob burn 22 policemen alive. Gandhi immediately suspended the Non-Cooperation Movement in protest.
176.connections: The eight-year gap between Chauri Chaura (1922) and the Dandi March (1930) was not silence. Bhagat Singh's revolutionary movement filled it. Gandhi's suspension of Non-Cooperation radicalised a generation who felt pacifism had failed at the moment of maximum pressure. Both streams, Gandhian and revolutionary, run parallel through the 1920s.
176.remember: Gandhi called the launch a 'Himalayan Blunder', realizing the masses weren't yet disciplined enough for non-violent resistance.
176.upsc: Tests Gandhi's absolute commitment to non-violence. Leaders like Nehru and Das were frustrated by the sudden halt.

177.label: Self-Respect Movement
177.what: E.V. Ramasamy, known as Periyar, founded the Self-Respect Movement in 1925 in Tamil Nadu to fight Brahmin dominance, caste oppression and superstition, advocating rational thinking and Dravidian identity.
177.connections: Periyar's movement is South India's most radical anti-caste project, targeting Brahmin cultural dominance specifically. This distinguishes it from Ambedkar's constitutional approach and Gandhi's Harijan welfare approach. The 1965 anti-Hindi agitation is the political extension of the same logic: Tamil identity against northern cultural imposition. Compare with Ambedkar's Mahad Satyagraha (1927) as a near-contemporary but differently located action against the same structural problem.
177.remember: Periyar organised Self-Respect marriages: simple ceremonies without priests, rituals, or dowries. He also led the 1965 anti-Hindi agitation that forced the central government to keep English as an official language.
177.upsc: Tests Periyar's role in the Dravidian movement, his fierce opposition to Hindi imposition, advocacy for women's rights and inter-caste marriages, and his influence on Tamil Nadu politics.

178.label: Sarojini Naidu, Congress president
178.what: Sarojini Naidu became the first woman President of the Indian National Congress at the Kanpur session in 1925. Known as the Nightingale of India, she was also a poet and later became Governor of Uttar Pradesh.
178.connections: Naidu's role in Gandhi's campaigns (Salt March 1930) makes her the first major woman figure in mainstream nationalist politics. Her description of herself as a sparrow who became an eagle under Gandhi is the biographical detail. Her appointment as Governor of UP after Independence made her India's first woman governor, connecting this card to Post-Independence.
178.remember: Sarojini Naidu was called the Nightingale of India for her poetry but she called herself a sparrow that had been mistaken for a nightingale. She told Gandhi that it cost a lot of money to keep him in poverty, referring to the expensive arrangements made for his simple lifestyle.
178.upsc: Tests her as first woman INC president, her participation in Salt March, and her role as first woman Governor (of UP) in independent India. Also tested as a poet and women's rights advocate.

179.label: Ambedkar's Mahad Satyagraha
179.what: The Mahad Satyagraha (1927) was Ambedkar's first major civil rights action, demanding Dalit access to the Chawdar tank.
179.connections: Mahad is Ambedkar's first direct-action campaign, 23 years before the Constitution's Article 17 (abolition of untouchability) and 28 years before the Untouchability Offences Act (1955) made it criminal. The comparison with the French Revolution he himself made is accurate: Mahad was the first time Dalits asserted a right in public rather than petitioning for one. That shift from petition to assertion is the political method that leads to the Poona Pact (1932) and eventually the Constitution itself.
179.remember: Ambedkar compared the Mahad struggle to the French Revolution, calling it a fight for 'Equality, Liberty and Fraternity'.
179.upsc: The Mahad Satyagraha is considered the start of Ambedkar's civil rights movement. Tests his methods, the burning of Manusmriti at the event, and its significance as India's first Dalit civil rights action.

180.label: Simon Commission
180.what: The Simon Commission (1928) arrived to review the 1919 reforms but had no Indian members, leading to a nationwide boycott.
180.connections: The chain here: Montagu-Chelmsford promised a 10-year constitutional review → Simon Commission arrives all-white → boycott → Indians draft their own constitution (Nehru Report, 1928) → Jinnah rejects it → Nehru declares Purna Swaraj (1929). Each British provocation produced a more radical Indian response.
180.remember: Lajpat Rai famously said: 'Every blow struck on my body will be the last nail in the coffin of British imperialism.'
180.upsc: Tests the 'All-White' composition and the death of Lala Lajpat Rai after a police lathi charge during a protest.

181.label: Nehru Report
181.what: The Nehru Report, drafted by Motilal Nehru's committee in 1928, was the first attempt by Indians to draft their own constitutional framework. It proposed Dominion Status, joint electorates, and fundamental rights.
181.connections: Jinnah's rejection of the Nehru Report is the moment he stopped trying to work within a united India framework. He responded with his 14 Points, insisting on separate electorates and one-third Muslim representation. A document meant to demonstrate Indian constitutional maturity ended up hardening every division it tried to bridge.
181.remember: The Nehru Report created a rift between father and son. Motilal was willing to accept Dominion Status. Jawaharlal wanted complete independence. The son won , Lahore 1929 declared Purna Swaraj. It was the only political argument a father publicly lost to his son.
181.upsc: Tests it as India's first self-drafted constitutional document, the rejection by Jinnah (who proposed the 14 points as counter), and Jawaharlal Nehru's opposition to Dominion Status (he wanted Purna Swaraj).

182.label: Raman Effect
182.what: C.V. Raman discovered the Raman Effect (inelastic scattering of light) on 28 February 1928. He won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1930, becoming the first Asian to win a Nobel Prize in science.
182.connections: The Raman Effect is in Science & Tech. This is the biographical anchor. The Rs. 200 equipment story is the most important detail: discovery came from insight, not resources. His Nobel (1930) made him the first Asian to win in Physics. He founded the Raman Research Institute in Bangalore and refused to leave India for better-resourced laboratories abroad.
182.remember: Raman made the discovery using equipment costing just Rs. 200. He had been told he needed expensive European laboratories to do serious science. He proved them wrong. National Science Day is celebrated on 28 February every year to commemorate this moment.
182.upsc: Tests the Raman Effect, National Science Day observed on 28 February, and Raman's founding of the Raman Research Institute in Bangalore.

183.label: Purna Swaraj Declaration
183.what: The Lahore Session (1929) under Nehru declared 'Purna Swaraj' (Complete Independence) as the goal of the INC.
183.connections: The 26 January 1930 Independence Day declaration is why 26 January was chosen as Republic Day in 1950. The date carries that Lahore midnight forward into the Republic. Purna Swaraj also set the stage directly for the Dandi March: once independence was the declared goal, the only question was which method would force it.
183.remember: Nehru hoisted the tricolour at midnight on the banks of the Ravi river, ending the period of asking for mere 'Dominion Status'.
183.upsc: Tests the 26 Jan 1930 'Independence Day' declaration. This is why 26 Jan was later chosen as Republic Day in 1950.

184.label: Great Depression
184.what: The Great Depression (1929) began with the Wall Street crash, leading to 25% unemployment in the US and a global trade collapse.
184.connections: The Great Depression's global consequences include Hitler's rise (economic desperation made his nationalism appealing), the end of the gold standard, and Keynesian economics as the new policy framework. In India, the Depression reduced commodity prices, hurting farmers and strengthening the argument for independence from British economic management. The 1930s convergence of global depression and Indian civil disobedience is not coincidental.
184.remember: On a single day (Black Tuesday, 29 October 1929) stocks lost 13 percent of their value. Investors who had borrowed to buy stocks were ruined overnight. Banks called in loans, businesses collapsed, unemployment spread globally.
184.upsc: Tests the rise of fascism (Hitler/Mussolini) as a response to economic misery and the birth of Keynesian economics.

185.label: Child Marriage Restraint Act
185.what: The Child Marriage Restraint Act of 1929, also called the Sarda Act (after its sponsor Harbilas Sarda), set the minimum age of marriage at 18 for men and 14 for women. It was the first national legislation against child marriage.
185.connections: The Sarda Act belongs to the same legislative sequence as Sati Abolition (1829), Widow Remarriage (1856), and Hindu Code Bills (1955): colonial-era and early post-colonial law addressing women's legal personhood. The gap between passing the law and changing practice is most stark here. Child marriage rates remained high well into the post-Independence period despite criminalisation, which is the standing enforcement problem this sequence illustrates.
185.remember: When the Sarda Act was passed, most Indian girls were married before puberty. The average age of marriage for girls in some communities was 5-7 years. Sarda fought for the bill for years against fierce orthodox opposition. The minimum age he achieved was still 14, a compromise from the 16 he sought.
185.upsc: Tests the Sarda Act alongside the Age of Consent Act 1891 as steps in legislating against child marriage, subsequent amendments (1978 raising ages to 21 and 18), and the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act 2006.

186.label: Dandi March / Salt Satyagraha
186.what: Gandhi's Dandi March (1930) was a 241-mile walk to the sea to make salt, defying the British salt tax and launching the Civil Disobedience Movement.
186.connections: This card sits between Purna Swaraj (1929) and the Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931). Purna Swaraj set the goal; the Dandi March set the method; the Pact ended the movement without achieving the goal, but it got Gandhi into the Second Round Table Conference. The salt was the genius: a symbol every Indian understood.
186.remember: It triggered a wave of civil disobedience that saw 60,000 people arrested across India.
186.upsc: Tests the choice of salt as a symbol of common struggle and the mass arrests of 60,000 protesters that followed.

187.label: First Round Table Conference
187.what: The Round Table Conferences (1930–32) in London were meant to discuss India's constitution. The INC only attended the second conference.
187.connections: Three RTCs, three outcomes: First (1930-31, INC absent, inconclusive) → Gandhi-Irwin Pact brings Gandhi to Second (1931, no agreement) → Third (1932, INC absent again, Communal Award announced). The whole sequence ends with the Government of India Act 1935. Three conferences produced one deeply unsatisfactory act.
187.remember: The British realized that no constitutional agreement was possible without the INC; this led to the Gandhi-Irwin Pact.
187.upsc: Tests the failure of the talks due to the Communal Award and the disagreement over separate electorates for Dalits.

188.label: Gandhi-Irwin Pact
188.what: The Gandhi-Irwin Pact (1931) saw Gandhi suspend Civil Disobedience to attend the 2nd Round Table Conference. The British released political prisoners.
188.connections: Three things happened in March 1931: the Pact was signed (5 March), Bhagat Singh was hanged (23 March), Gandhi sailed for London for the Second RTC. Three simultaneous stories with three completely different endings. The pact's survival after Bhagat Singh's execution defines the fault line between the constitutional and revolutionary wings of nationalism.
188.remember: Bhagat Singh was hanged just days after the pact was signed, leading many youth to feel Gandhi had compromised too much.
188.upsc: Tests the controversy: Gandhi failed to secure a stay on the execution of Bhagat Singh, Sukhdev, and Rajguru.

189.label: APJ Abdul Kalam
189.what: A.P.J. Abdul Kalam (1931-2015) was the Missile Man of India who led the SLV-3 and Agni missile programmes, served as President from 2002-2007, and became the most beloved president in India's modern history.
189.connections: Kalam connects Science & Tech (SLV-3, Agni, Pokhran-II) with Personalities. His Missile Man role connects to the nuclear test cards. His presidency (2002-2007) is distinct from his scientific career. His personal austerity (owned nothing, slept four hours, wrote Tamil poetry) became as famous as his scientific work. Compare with Homi Bhabha as the two defining figures of India's defence science.
189.remember: Kalam was a vegetarian who played the veena, wrote Tamil poetry and slept only four hours a night. He owned nothing when he died: his possessions were a few books, some clothes, a laptop and a CD player.
189.upsc: Tests his role in SLV-3 and the Agni missiles, and his focus on youth and 'Vision 2020'.

190.label: Chandrashekhar Azad
190.what: Chandrashekhar Azad, leader of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association, died on 27 February 1931 in Alfred Park (now Azad Park), Allahabad. Surrounded by British police, he shot himself rather than be captured.
190.connections: Azad and Bhagat Singh are the two revolutionary nationalist figures of the same generation and the same organisation (HSRA). Both died in 1931, at 24 and 23 respectively. Azad's vow never to be arrested and his self-shooting at Alfred Park is the biographical complement to Bhagat Singh's gallows composure. The revolutionary tradition they shared was extinguished in the same year.
190.remember: Azad had sworn as a teenager that he would never be arrested. He kept his word by shooting himself. He was 24. He had told Bhagat Singh: the day I am arrested, consider me dead. He was never arrested.
190.upsc: Tests HSRA alongside Bhagat Singh's activities, Azad's vow to never be arrested (he kept it), and his role in organising the revolutionary movement that ran parallel to Gandhi's non-violent independence struggle.

191.label: Poona Pact
191.what: The Poona Pact (1932) was signed between Gandhi and Ambedkar; Ambedkar gave up separate electorates for Dalits in exchange for more reserved seats.
191.connections: Morley-Minto (1909) introduced separate electorates for Muslims. The Communal Award (1932) extended the logic to Dalits. Gandhi's fast against it produced this Pact. Ambedkar never forgave the coercion. His conversion to Buddhism in 1956 was the final rejection of a political system he felt had failed Dalits at this moment.
191.remember: Ambedkar felt coerced into the pact by Gandhi's 'fast unto death', but it prevented a permanent political split between Hindus and Dalits.
191.upsc: Tests the tension between Gandhi's view of 'Harijans' and Ambedkar's demand for Dalit self-determination.

192.label: Hitler
192.what: Adolf Hitler became Chancellor (1933) and established a Nazi dictatorship, reclaiming German territory lost in WWI.
192.connections: Hitler's rise is the case study in democratic systems being used to end democracy. He was appointed Chancellor, not elected dictator: he then used the Reichstag Fire emergency to seize total power. Compare with the Indian Emergency (1975): both are examples of executive power suspending democratic norms using legal mechanisms from within the system. The difference is that India's ended by electoral choice.
192.remember: Hitler wasn't elected dictator; he was appointed Chancellor and then used the 'Reichstag Fire' to seize total power.
192.upsc: Tests the Holocaust, the failure of 'Appeasement', and the Nuremberg Trials' principle of 'Crimes against Humanity'.

193.label: Government of India Act
193.what: The Government of India Act 1935 was the longest Act ever passed by the British Parliament, introducing Provincial Autonomy and planning a federation that was never implemented.
193.connections: The Government of India Act 1935 is the skeleton inside India's Constitution. Federalism, provincial autonomy, emergency provisions, the legislative lists: all adapted from 1935. Indian leaders attacked it as insufficient and used its architecture anyway. The Constitution is in many ways 1935 with democratic legitimacy added.
193.remember: Indian leaders criticised it heavily, but many of its administrative ideas quietly lived on into independent India.
193.upsc: A major blueprint for later constitutional arrangements in India. Tests provincial autonomy, the Federal Court, the RBI, and the federal scheme that remained unimplemented.

194.label: Congress wins provincial elections
194.what: In the 1937 elections under the 1935 Act, the INC formed governments in 7 of 11 provinces, proving its mass electoral appeal.
194.connections: The INC's 1937-39 ministries proved Congress could govern. But governing without League partners gave Jinnah two years of evidence for his argument. When the ministries resigned in 1939 over WWII, Jinnah declared it a 'Day of Deliverance'. The League went from minor party to dominant Muslim political force in the gap between 1937 and 1946.
194.remember: The INC refused to form coalitions with the Muslim League, a decision Jinnah used to fuel his demand for a separate Pakistan.
194.upsc: Tests the 1937–39 INC ministries and their resignation in protest against India being dragged into WWII without consultation.

195.label: Second World War
195.what: World War II (1939–45) began with the invasion of Poland. It was the deadliest conflict in history (70–85 million deaths).
195.connections: India's 2.5 million WWII soldiers without being consulted before being declared a war participant is the political grievance that triggered the Quit India Movement (1942). Compare with WWI: India sent 1.5 million soldiers and waited for reward; WWII, India sent 2.5 million and demanded independence instead. The strategic calculation changed.
195.remember: India provided the largest volunteer army in history for WWII, yet they were not consulted before being declared at war.
195.upsc: Tests causes (appeasement, Versailles), theatres of war, India's involvement (2.5 million soldiers, Quit India Movement 1942 in response), and the founding of the UN and decolonisation as post-war consequences.

196.label: Cripps Mission
196.what: The Cripps Mission (1942) offered 'Dominion Status' after WWII in exchange for war support. Both the INC and League rejected it.
196.connections: The chain of failed British proposals: Cripps Mission (1942, rejected) → Wavell Plan / Simla Conference (1945, collapsed) → Cabinet Mission (1946, collapsed) → Mountbatten Plan (1947, partition). Each attempt was less united and more partition-shaped than the last. By 1947 partition wasn't a failure. It was the plan.
196.remember: Cripps offered provinces the right to secede from the union, which the INC saw as a blueprint for the partition of India.
196.upsc: Tests Gandhi's description: 'A post-dated cheque on a failing bank.' Its failure led directly to the Quit India Movement.

197.label: Quit India Movement
197.what: Gandhi launched the Quit India Movement on 8 August 1942 with the call Do or Die. Top Congress leaders were arrested within hours, after which protests and underground activity spread across many parts of India.
197.connections: Quit India and the INA (1943) are two simultaneous parallel strategies: civilian uprising inside India, and armed force from outside with Japanese backing. Both failed militarily. Both succeeded psychologically: together they convinced the British that the cost of staying outweighed any benefit. The movement that 'failed' helped end the empire.
197.remember: Gandhi was arrested before he could even give his planned speech. The movement continued leaderless for three years.
197.upsc: Tests its spontaneous, leaderless nature, the August Kranti, role of Aruna Asaf Ali (hoisted flag at Gowalia Tank), and underground leaders like Jayaprakash Narayan.

198.label: Indian National Army
198.what: Subhas Chandra Bose reorganised and revitalised the Indian National Army in Southeast Asia in 1943 with Japanese backing, and also proclaimed the Azad Hind government.
198.connections: Pair Bose with Gandhi. Same goal, opposite methods. Gandhi: non-violence, mass civil resistance, moral authority. Bose: armed force, foreign alliances, military confrontation. The INA's failure in battle was reversed in court: the Red Fort trials made the officers heroes, not traitors. Bose's methods failed; his legacy outlasted his contemporaries.
198.remember: Bose did not create the INA from zero. What he did was give it new energy, clearer direction, and enormous symbolic power.
198.upsc: Tests the difference between the INA's initial formation under Mohan Singh in 1942 and Bose's later leadership in 1943, along with the slogans Dilli Chalo and Jai Hind, the Rani of Jhansi Regiment, and the Red Fort trials.

199.label: Bengal famine
199.what: The Bengal famine of 1943 killed between 2 and 3 million people. It was caused not by crop failure but by wartime policies including diversion of food to British troops, denial of boats to prevent Japanese use, and Churchill's refusal to send aid.
199.connections: The famine is Amartya Sen's central case study. It proved famines are caused by policy failure, not food shortage. Place it alongside the Permanent Settlement (1793): Cornwallis's zamindari system created the structural vulnerability that made Bengal's rural poor so exposed in 1943. Colonial land policy and colonial famine are the same story.
199.remember: Churchill reportedly said when asked about the famine: why hasn't Gandhi died yet? Food was being exported from Bengal even as people starved. The denial of denial , this famine was a policy choice, not a natural disaster.
199.upsc: Tests Amartya Sen's analysis that the famine was man-made not natural, the wartime context, Churchill's documented refusal to divert ships carrying food to Bengal, and this as a case study in colonial exploitation.

200.label: INA trials at Red Fort
200.what: The INA Trials (1945–46) at the Red Fort backfired on the British, as the public hailed the INA officers (Sehgal, Dhillon, Shah Nawaz) as heroes.
200.connections: The British intended the trials to punish traitors. They created martyrs instead. The scale of public sympathy was a shock, and the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny followed three months later. By early 1946 the British understood neither the Army nor the Navy could be trusted. The independence timeline accelerated from that realisation.
200.remember: Nehru and other top lawyers defended the officers. The trial unified Indians across religions in support of the INA soldiers.
200.upsc: Tests the impact on the Royal Indian Navy Mutiny and the realization that the Indian Army could no longer be relied upon.

201.label: Hiroshima atomic bombing
201.what: The United States dropped an atomic bomb on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945 and on Nagasaki on 9 August 1945. Japan surrendered on 15 August, ending WWII. About 140,000 people died in Hiroshima immediately.
201.connections: The atomic bombing made the nuclear age real. Connect to India's nuclear cards: Pokhran-I (1974), Pokhran-II (1998), Agni-V (2012). India's position as a nuclear state that also advocates global disarmament is the foreign policy tension this history creates. The Hiroshima survivor community (Hibakusha) maintained the moral case against nuclear weapons; India invokes it while building its own deterrent.
201.remember: The Hiroshima bomb, 'Little Boy', killed 140,000 immediately; the survivor 'Hibakusha' suffered radiation effects for decades.
201.upsc: Tests the start of the Nuclear Age and India's 'No First Use' policy developed in response to nuclear proliferation.

202.label: United Nations
202.what: The United Nations was founded on 24 October 1945 with 51 charter members after WWII. It replaced the failed League of Nations with a more powerful body including the Security Council with five permanent members.
202.connections: The UN replaced the League with one structural improvement: the Security Council veto gives great powers a reason to participate rather than stay out (as the US did with the League). India's 200,000+ peacekeeping troop contributions make it the largest contributor in UN history. India's campaign for a permanent Security Council seat is the ongoing institutional argument this card frames.
202.remember: India has contributed over 200,000 troops to UN peacekeeping missions, more than any other country in history. Yet India does not have a permanent seat on the Security Council, which is its biggest foreign policy grievance.
202.upsc: Tests UN organs and India's demand for a permanent seat on the Security Council based on its peacekeeping role.

203.label: Wavell Plan and Simla Conference
203.what: Viceroy Wavell proposed a plan in June 1945 for an Executive Council of Indians to govern, with equal Hindu-Muslim representation. The Simla Conference collapsed when Jinnah insisted the League alone represent Muslims.
203.connections: The Simla Conference failure (1945) is the penultimate attempt to keep India united before the Cabinet Mission (1946). Jinnah's insistence that the League alone represents Muslims previews exactly the argument that makes the Cabinet Mission grouping scheme unworkable. Each failed conference made the next one start from a weaker position.
203.remember: Gandhi pointed out that Congress had Muslim members and could not be called a Hindu party. Jinnah said only the League spoke for Muslims. This argument about who represents whom was the core of the partition debate. The Simla Conference failure made partition almost inevitable.
203.upsc: Tests this as the last serious pre-partition unity attempt before the Cabinet Mission. Jinnah's insistence that no Congress Muslim could represent Muslims was a key sticking point that previewed partition logic.

204.label: TIFR
204.what: Homi Jehangir Bhabha established the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR) in Bombay in 1945 and later founded the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), creating the foundation for India's nuclear programme.
204.connections: TIFR is the institutional predecessor to India's atomic programme. Bhabha's thorium strategy connects to India's three-stage nuclear power programme. Compare with Vikram Sarabhai (ISRO founding 1969) as the parallel figure in space science. Both Bhabha and Sarabhai died in accidents during their active careers, leaving programmes they designed to be completed by others.
204.remember: Bhabha designed India's nuclear programme around thorium because India has 25% of the world's thorium reserves but very little uranium. If the programme he designed is fully implemented, India's energy needs could be met for thousands of years. The third stage is still being developed.
204.upsc: Tests the Three Stage Nuclear Power Programme Bhabha designed (thorium-based), BARC, TIFR, and his role as chairman of India's Atomic Energy Commission until his death in a plane crash in 1966.

205.label: Cabinet Mission Plan
205.what: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) proposed a three-tier federation to keep India united while giving Muslims autonomy. It eventually failed.
205.connections: The Cabinet Mission's three-tier grouping was its central innovation. Provinces were grouped by religious composition, with maximum autonomy at each tier. INC objected to compulsory grouping; Jinnah accepted then withdrew. Place this between the Wavell Plan (1945) and Direct Action Day (1946): each collapsed negotiation made the next more explosive.
205.remember: This was the last major attempt to prevent partition. Its failure led Jinnah to call for 'Direct Action Day'.
205.upsc: Tests the complex grouping of provinces (A, B, C) and why the INC and League couldn't agree on compulsory grouping.

206.label: Direct Action Day
206.what: Direct Action Day (16 Aug 1946) was Jinnah's call to press for Pakistan, leading to the 'Great Calcutta Killings' and thousands of deaths.
206.connections: Direct Action Day is the point of no return. Before August 1946 partition was a political demand. After the Calcutta killings, communal violence spread to Bihar, Punjab, and Noakhali. Too much blood for a shared state to absorb. The Mountbatten Plan (1947) reflects this reality rather than causing it.
206.remember: Gandhi spent the months leading to independence walking through riot-torn villages in Noakhali, trying to stop the bloodshed.
206.upsc: Marks the point of no return for partition. Communal riots spread from Bengal to Bihar and the Punjab.

207.label: Constituent Assembly first met
207.what: The Constituent Assembly first met on 9 Dec 1946. Its strength was reduced from 389 to 299 after Partition.
207.connections: The Constituent Assembly is the context for every card in this category. It convened under the Cabinet Mission Plan; Partition reduced its strength. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee. The Constitution was adopted on 26 November 1949 and came into force on 26 January 1950. Every amendment and judgment in this category is a conversation with the document this body created.
207.remember: Only 284 members were present on 24 Jan 1950 to sign the final calligraphed copies of the Constitution.
207.upsc: Tests key figures (Sinha, Prasad, Ambedkar) and the 11 sessions held over 2 years, 11 months, and 18 days.

208.label: Royal Indian Navy Mutiny
208.what: In February 1946, sailors of the Royal Indian Navy mutinied in Bombay, raising slogans of Jai Hind and flying Congress, League and Communist flags together. The mutiny spread to 78 ships and 20 shore establishments.
208.connections: Place alongside the INA Trials (six months earlier): two events that gave the British the same message from different directions. The Trials showed civilian sympathy for armed resistance; the Mutiny showed the Navy itself could not be trusted. Together they made the British calculation simple: leave before the army follows.
208.remember: Both Gandhi and Jinnah condemned the mutiny. Sardar Patel negotiated its end by promising amnesty. The sailors felt betrayed. But the British got the message: their grip on the Indian military was gone. Independence was announced within the year.
208.upsc: Tests its significance as proof that the Indian military could no longer be relied upon by the British, directly accelerating the independence timeline. Also tests Congress and League leaders criticising the mutiny as premature.

209.label: Mountbatten Plan
209.what: The Mountbatten Plan (3 June 1947) announced the partition of India and Pakistan. Cyril Radcliffe drew the boundaries in just six weeks.
209.connections: The Radcliffe Line was announced two days after independence. The delay was deliberate: announcing borders before independence would have caused immediate mass movement. Instead it caused mass movement immediately after. Radcliffe left India the day after partition, never returned, and reportedly burned his papers.
209.remember: Radcliffe had never been to India before his six-week mission to divide a subcontinent of 400 million people.
209.upsc: Tests the Radcliffe Line and why the boundaries were only announced two days *after* independence.

210.label: Indian Independence
210.what: India became independent on 15 August 1947 at midnight. Nehru delivered his Tryst with Destiny speech to the Constituent Assembly. The Union Jack was lowered across India.
210.connections: Independence and Partition are the same event. The Tryst with Destiny speech was delivered at midnight while trains carrying refugees were already moving in both directions. Fifteen million displaced, hundreds of thousands killed. The Constitution being drafted in the next room would have to build a nation out of this beginning.
210.remember: Mountbatten chose 15 August to coincide with the second anniversary of Japan's surrender in WWII. Pakistan became independent on 14 August. Both dates share the same midnight origin.
210.upsc: Tests the midnight session, Nehru's speech, Mountbatten as first Governor-General, and Rajendra Prasad as President of the Constituent Assembly.

211.label: Devadasi system
211.what: The Devadasi system (temple sexual servitude) was abolished in Madras (1947) through the efforts of Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddi.
211.connections: The Devadasi abolition happened the same year as Independence, linking social reform and political freedom as simultaneous projects. The tradition-versus-exploitation argument it raised is the central tension across this category: Sati, Devadasi, dowry, and child marriage all involved the same defence. Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddi's dual role (first woman legislator and reform campaigner) connects this card to both the Constitutional and Social Reform arcs.
211.remember: The reform debate was difficult because defenders called it tradition, while reformers pointed out the exploitation hiding inside that tradition.
211.upsc: UPSC tests the social reform context, Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddi's campaign, and the intersection of religion, caste, and gender in colonial and early post-colonial reform.

212.label: Indian Independence Act
212.what: The Indian Independence Act (1947) created the Dominions of India and Pakistan, abolishing the post of Secretary of State.
212.connections: The Independence Act is the legal bridge between colonial and constitutional India. The Governor-General (Mountbatten, then Rajagopalachari) represented the Crown in this gap. The Act also transferred paramountcy over princely states, which is the legal basis for Sardar Patel's integration of 562 states. The Constitution replaced this Act as the governing document when it came into force on 26 January 1950.
212.remember: The King of England remained the formal head of state of India until 26 January 1950, represented by the Governor-General.
212.upsc: Tests the legal mechanism of independence. India remained a 'Dominion' until it became a Republic in 1950.

213.label: B.R. Ambedkar became Law Minister
213.what: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was appointed Law Minister in Nehru's first cabinet and chaired the seven-member Drafting Committee, making him the principal architect of the Constitution.
213.connections: Ambedkar's fingerprints are on Articles 17 (abolition of untouchability), 32 (constitutional remedies), and the reservation provisions. His resignation over the diluted Hindu Code Bill (1951) connects this card directly to the Social Reform category. The man who designed the Constitution's rights framework resigned because the government would not use it to reform Hindu personal law.
213.remember: Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the Soul of the Constitution, saying rights without enforcement are meaningless. He resigned in 1951 saying Nehru lacked the courage to pass the Hindu Code Bill.
213.upsc: One of the most tested UPSC personalities. Tests his role in the Drafting Committee, inclusion of Fundamental Rights, his conversion to Buddhism in 1956, and his resignation over the Hindu Code Bill.

214.label: Nehru's Tryst with Destiny speech
214.what: Nehru delivered his Tryst with Destiny speech to the Constituent Assembly at the stroke of midnight on 14-15 August 1947, marking India's independence from British rule.
214.connections: The speech is India's constitutional moment in oratory: the verbal complement to the written Constitution. Nehru wrote it himself the night before. Compare with Lincoln's Gettysburg Address as a similarly brief, self-authored text that defines a national founding. Every subsequent Independence Day PM speech is measured against this one.
214.remember: The speech was broadcast live on All India Radio. Nehru wrote it himself the night before. It is considered one of the greatest speeches in history and is still studied in political science curricula worldwide.
214.upsc: Tests the exact date and occasion of the speech, the phrase at the stroke of the midnight hour, and its significance as the founding oratorical moment of the Indian republic.

215.label: Marshall Plan
215.what: US Secretary of State George Marshall announced the European Recovery Program (Marshall Plan) in June 1947, offering billions in aid to rebuild war-devastated European economies and prevent communist takeover.
215.connections: The Marshall Plan's strategic logic (economic recovery prevents communist revolution) shaped the Cold War. India declined Marshall Plan-style aid to maintain non-alignment. The Soviet counter (Molotov Plan for Eastern Europe) divided the continent into the two blocs the Non-Aligned Movement (1961) tried to avoid. The Marshall Plan is the economic architecture of the Western side of the Cold War.
215.remember: The USSR called the Marshall Plan dollar imperialism. Stalin ordered Eastern European countries to refuse it. Those that accepted rebuilt into prosperous democracies within 20 years. Those that refused spent 40 years in poverty. Stalin's paranoia cost his allies a generation.
215.upsc: Tests the Cold War context of the Marshall Plan, why the USSR refused it and prevented Eastern European nations from accepting, and how it created the economic division of Europe that defined the Cold War.

216.label: Operation Polo
216.what: Operation Polo (1948) was the military action to integrate Hyderabad into India. Sardar Patel called it a 'Police Action'.
216.connections: Operation Polo is the military bookend to Patel's diplomatic integration. Most states signed voluntarily or under pressure; Hyderabad required force. Pair with Junagadh accession (1948): both are contested integrations involving a ruler whose religious identity conflicted with his population's majority. Both were resolved by Patel within the first year.
216.remember: The Nizam of Hyderabad was the richest man in the world at the time, owning seven percent of US GDP. He attempted to remain independent, buy weapons from Europe, and even approach the UN. Patel ended the issue in four days.
216.upsc: Tests the three difficult integrations: Hyderabad (Military), Junagadh (Referendum), and Kashmir (Instrument of Accession).

217.label: Mahatma Gandhi
217.what: Mahatma Gandhi was assassinated (30 Jan 1948) by Nathuram Godse, who blamed him for partition and perceived pro-Muslim bias.
217.connections: Gandhi's assassination 5 months after Independence is the most consequential early event of the new nation. The ban on RSS that followed and its subsequent lifting shaped Hindu nationalist politics for decades. Gandhi's death transformed him from a politically divisive figure in his final months into an unassailable symbol. Godse's stated reasons are the political context worth knowing.
217.remember: Gandhi had survived five previous assassination attempts. His death led to a massive outpouring of grief and a pause in communal violence.
217.upsc: Tests the immediate aftermath: Nehru's speech ('The light has gone out') and the brief banning of the RSS.

218.label: Universal Declaration of Human Rights
218.what: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) (1948) established 30 articles protecting fundamental human rights globally.
218.connections: Indian delegate Hansa Mehta's change of 'All men' to 'All human beings' is India's single most significant contribution to a UN document. The UDHR has no enforcement mechanism but has shaped domestic constitutions globally, including India's own Fundamental Rights. Compare with the Magna Carta (1215) as the earlier landmark in codifying rights against authority: the UDHR is the Magna Carta's 20th century successor.
218.remember: Indian delegate Hansa Mehta famously insisted on changing 'All men are born free' to 'All human beings are born free'.
218.upsc: Tests specific rights in the UDHR and how they correspond to Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution (Part III). December 10 is observed as Human Rights Day.

219.label: Junagadh accession to India
219.what: The Nawab of Junagadh signed accession to Pakistan despite a Hindu-majority population. After the Nawab fled to Pakistan, India held a referendum in which 99% voted to join India.
219.connections: Pair with Operation Polo (1948). Junagadh's Nawab signed accession to Pakistan despite a Hindu-majority population; Hyderabad's Nizam declared independence despite a Muslim-minority one. Both violated the principle that religion determines accession direction. Pakistan's claim to Junagadh, technically maintained today, is the forgotten irony of the partition logic.
219.remember: Pakistan technically still has a government-in-exile for Junagadh. It is a forgotten irony that Pakistan claims Junagadh by the same accession logic India uses to claim Kashmir.
219.upsc: Tests the three difficult integrations alongside Hyderabad and Kashmir. Junagadh is the only one resolved by referendum. Pakistan still claims Junagadh legally as the original accession was to Pakistan.

220.label: Constitution of India
220.what: The Constitution of India was adopted (26 Nov 1949) after nearly three years of work. It is the longest written constitution in the world.
220.connections: The Constitution was adopted on 26 November 1949 and came into force on 26 January 1950. Adoption means the Constituent Assembly accepted the text; enactment means it became law. The two dates are tested separately. 26 November is now celebrated as Constitution Day (Samvidhan Diwas). The handwritten calligraphed copies in English and Hindi, not typed or printed, is the production detail.
220.remember: The original constitution was handwritten in both English and Hindi, not printed or typed; it took five years to prepare the final calligraphed copies.
220.upsc: Tests 'Constitution Day' (Samvidhan Diwas) and the different sources borrowed (USA: Rights, UK: Parliament, Ireland: DPSP).

221.label: NATO
221.what: NATO (est. 1949) is a Western military alliance based on collective defense ('an attack on one is an attack on all').
221.connections: NATO's collective defense principle is the foundation of the Western security architecture. India's refusal to join any Cold War military bloc (Strategic Autonomy) is the direct counterpoint. The Non-Aligned Movement (1961) is the institutional expression of that refusal. NATO's post-Cold War expansion is the ongoing context this card opens; India's positioning between NATO and Russia in the Ukraine conflict is the contemporary application.
221.remember: India's 'Strategic Autonomy' today traces back to its refusal to join Cold War military blocs in the 1950s.
221.upsc: Tests the Cold War context and India's decision to remain 'Non-Aligned' rather than join NATO or the Warsaw Pact.

222.label: Constitution of India
222.what: The Constitution came into force (26 Jan 1950), making India a Republic. The date was chosen to honor the 1930 Purna Swaraj declaration.
222.connections: 26 January was chosen to coincide with the 1930 Purna Swaraj declaration, connecting the Republic's founding to the independence movement's highest demand. The date is itself a political statement: the Constitution is presented as the fulfilment of what the movement promised. Pair with the adoption card (1949): adoption is the Constituent Assembly accepting the text; enactment is the law coming into force.
222.remember: India chose 26 January 1950 deliberately to connect the new Republic to the independence movement. Every Republic Day parade since has demonstrated India's military strength and cultural diversity to the world.
222.upsc: Tests the distinction between Adoption (Nov 1949) and Commencement (Jan 1950). Dr. Rajendra Prasad became the first President.

223.label: Planning Commission
223.what: The Planning Commission (est. 1950) was created by cabinet resolution to oversee Five-Year Plans. It was replaced by NITI Aayog in 2015.
223.connections: The Planning Commission had no statutory basis: created by cabinet resolution, not Act of Parliament. Yet for 65 years it controlled India's economic priorities more than most statutory bodies. Its replacement by NITI Aayog in 2015 represents the ideological distance between Nehruvian centralised planning and cooperative federalism. The Commission's lack of legal authority combined with total practical power is its defining institutional paradox.
223.remember: The Commission had no statutory powers, yet for 65 years it controlled the economic priorities and resource allocation of India.
223.upsc: Tests the non-constitutional status of the commission and the different models (First: Harrod-Domar, Second: Mahalanobis).

224.label: Korean War
224.what: The Korean War (1950–53) ended in an armistice, not a peace treaty, leaving North and South Korea divided at the 38th parallel.
224.connections: The Korean War ended in armistice, not peace treaty: North and South Korea are technically still at war. India played a significant role in the armistice negotiations and led the Neutral Nations Repatriation Commission that handled POW exchanges. India's diplomatic role in Korea is one of the clearest practical expressions of non-aligned foreign policy: trusted by both sides precisely because it was allied to neither.
224.remember: No peace treaty has ever been signed ending the Korean War. Technically North and South Korea are still at war. The 1953 armistice was a ceasefire, not a peace. The demilitarised zone is the world's most militarised border.
224.upsc: Tests India's role: India led the UN commission that negotiated the POW exchange, Nehru mediated between the USA and China, and India proposed the armistice terms. Korea remains divided at the 38th parallel.

225.label: First Republic Day parade
225.what: The first Republic Day parade was held on 26 January 1950 on Rajpath (now Kartavya Path) in New Delhi, marking the commencement of the Constitution and India becoming a republic.
225.connections: The first Republic Day parade established the tradition of foreign state guests, military display, and cultural tableaux. Rajpath (now Kartavya Path) as the venue was a statement: the British colonial ceremonial axis repurposed for Indian republican spectacle. The Chief Guest tradition is one of India's most consistent diplomatic signals of which relationship the government wants to highlight in a given year.
225.remember: The Chief Guest at the first Republic Day was Indonesian President Sukarno. The tradition of inviting a foreign head of state as Chief Guest began from day one and continues today.
225.upsc: Tests the distinction between Independence Day (15 Aug) and Republic Day (26 Jan), the significance of 26 January (linked to 1930 Purna Swaraj), and the parade as a symbol of military and cultural diversity.

226.label: Hindi declared official language
226.what: When the Constitution came into force in 1950, Article 343 declared Hindi in Devanagari script as the official language of the Union, with English to continue for 15 years as a transitional measure.
226.connections: Article 343 declared Hindi as official language with English as associate language for 15 years. The 1965 deadline became politically impossible: Tamil Nadu erupted in self-immolations, forcing the government to extend English indefinitely. The language question is connected to the States Reorganisation Act (1956) and the Maharashtra-Gujarat creation (1960) as part of the same linguistic politics of the same decade.
226.remember: The 15-year deadline for switching fully to Hindi expired in 1965. Tamil Nadu erupted in violent protests. Self-immolations occurred. The government backed down and retained English. Hindi is official but not the sole language of administration.
226.upsc: Tests Article 343-351 (Official Language provisions), the Official Languages Act 1963 which kept English indefinitely, and the 1965 anti-Hindi agitations in Tamil Nadu that forced the government to retain English.

227.label: Supreme Court of India
227.what: The Supreme Court of India was established on 28 January 1950, replacing the Federal Court of India and the Privy Council as the final court of appeal. Justice Harilal Jekisundas Kania became the first Chief Justice.
227.connections: The Supreme Court's establishment begins the judicial review arc that runs through every landmark judgment in this category: Kesavananda Bharati (1973), Maneka Gandhi (1978), SR Bommai (1994), privacy (2017). Each judgment is the Court negotiating with Parliament over where constitutional power ends. The Court is both the subject and the narrator of this entire category.
227.remember: Until 1950, an Indian's final legal appeal went to the Privy Council in London. Indian citizens were judged ultimately by British lords who had never visited India and knew nothing of Indian conditions. The Supreme Court ended 200 years of external judicial sovereignty.
227.upsc: Tests the Supreme Court's original, appellate, and advisory jurisdiction (Article 143), the distinction from the Federal Court it replaced, and the significance of ending appeals to the Privy Council in London.

228.label: First Five-Year Plan
228.what: India's First Five Year Plan (1951-1956) focused on agriculture and irrigation after the disruption of partition. It was based on the Harrod-Domar growth model and achieved a growth rate of 3.6 percent against a target of 2.1 percent.
228.connections: The First Plan focused on agriculture and irrigation, appropriate after the Partition disruption. Its overachievement (3.6% vs 2.1% target) was partly due to good monsoons. The Five-Year Plan framework was borrowed from Soviet central planning and adapted to a democratic context. The First Plan's success gave the planning model the political legitimacy it needed to continue for 65 years.
228.remember: The First Plan exceeded its targets partly due to good monsoons, providing the new nation with a much-needed early success.
228.upsc: Tests the focus areas of each plan. First (agriculture), Second (heavy industry, Mahalanobis model), Third (self-reliance), Fifth (poverty removal, Garibi Hatao), Eighth (liberalisation, market reforms).

229.label: First General Elections of India
229.what: India's First General Elections (1951–52) were the world's largest democratic exercise, proving democracy could work in a poor, illiterate nation.
229.connections: Universal adult suffrage in a country with 85% illiteracy, 14 official languages, and less than a year of independence is the proof-of-concept moment for democratic theory. The symbol system on ballots solved the literacy problem. The 45% turnout was considered a success. The Congress winning 364 of 489 seats set a dominance pattern that lasted until 1977.
229.remember: Observers predicted collapse; instead, turnout was 45%, and the 'symbol system' allowed millions of non-readers to cast their vote.
229.upsc: Tests the Electoral Commission's role (chief commissioner Sukumar Sen), the use of symbols for illiterate voters, and the significance as proof that democracy could work in a poor, largely illiterate country.

230.label: Vinoba Bhave's Bhoodan Movement
230.what: Vinoba Bhave launched the Bhoodan (land gift) movement in 1951 by walking through India asking landowners to voluntarily donate land to the landless. He collected pledges for over 4 million acres.
230.connections: Bhoodan is Gandhi's land reform method translated into post-Independence practice, rejecting the state-led redistribution that the Five-Year Plans pursued. Vinoba's 70,000 km walk is one of the most extraordinary feats of physical dedication in Indian political history. The movement's practical failure (most pledged land was unusable) is the honest counterpoint to its moral ambition.
230.remember: Vinoba walked 70,000 km over 13 years collecting land pledges. Much of what was pledged was unusable land. Landowners gave away their worst fields. The movement showed the limits of voluntary redistribution against structural inequality.
230.upsc: Tests Bhoodan as a Gandhian alternative to land reform through legislation, the Gramdan (village gift) variant, and why it ultimately failed to redistribute meaningful land despite the large acreage pledged.

231.label: IIT Kharagpur
231.what: IIT Kharagpur was established on 18 August 1951, the first of the Indian Institutes of Technology. It was built on the site of a British detention facility and modelled on MIT, with American technical assistance.
231.connections: The IIT system is the most globally recognised output of Nehruvian educational investment. IIT Kharagpur was built in a former colonial prison, which is the symbolism worth noting. Compare with AIIMS (1956) as the parallel institution in medicine: both were Nehruvian state-building projects that became the country's most contested entrance examinations decades later.
231.remember: IIT Kharagpur was built in a former colonial prison where Indian freedom fighters had been detained. The first students studied in cells where nationalists had been imprisoned. Independence had turned a prison into a university.
231.upsc: Tests the IIT Act 1961, the Institutes of Technology Act, the expansion to 23 IITs today, and the brain drain debate around IIT graduates emigrating. Nehru called the IITs temples of learning.

232.label: First Amendment to Indian Constitution
232.what: The First Amendment (1951) added reasonable restrictions to free speech, inserted Articles 31A and 31B to protect land reform laws, and created the Ninth Schedule which placed certain laws beyond judicial review.
232.connections: The First Amendment fires the opening shot in the Parliament vs. Supreme Court battle over rights versus policy. The Ninth Schedule it created was meant to be immune from judicial review, but the Supreme Court later held in Kesavananda Bharati (1973) that even the Ninth Schedule is subject to basic structure review. The First Amendment set the pattern for a conflict that continued for decades.
232.remember: Nehru passed the First Amendment within a year of the Constitution coming into force. The Supreme Court had struck down some land reform laws. Nehru's response was to create a schedule where laws are immune from judicial review. The Constitution was amended to overrule the Supreme Court.
232.upsc: Tests the Ninth Schedule as one of UPSC's most tested constitutional topics , laws placed in it cannot be challenged in court. The Supreme Court in IR Coelho (2007) held that Ninth Schedule laws can still be reviewed if they violate basic structure.

233.label: Community Development Programme
233.what: India launched its Community Development Programme in October 1952, creating community development blocks across rural India to improve agriculture, education, health, and social life through trained extension workers.
233.connections: Compare with the 73rd Amendment (1992) in the Constitutional category: both attempt rural empowerment through state mechanisms 40 years apart. The CDP's failure through top-down design is the lesson the 73rd Amendment tried to address through constitutional mandate for local self-governance. The Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957), which reviewed the CDP, recommended three-tier Panchayati Raj as the solution, directly leading to that later amendment.
233.remember: The Community Development Programme was India's first attempt at planned rural development. It failed because it relied on government officials rather than elected representatives. The Balwant Rai Mehta Committee's conclusion was simple: you cannot develop people, you can only enable them to develop themselves.
233.upsc: Tests the CDP as the predecessor of Panchayati Raj, the Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) that recommended replacing CDP with elected Panchayats, and the programme's ultimate failure to achieve participation.

234.label: Special Marriage Act
234.what: The Special Marriage Act of 1954 enabled Indians of any religion to marry without religious conversion or ceremonies, with civil registration. It applies to all Indian citizens regardless of religion.
234.connections: Pair with the Hindu Code Bills (1955): where the Hindu Code Bills reformed personal law within Hindu traditions, the Special Marriage Act created a secular alternative that any Indian can use. Together they define the boundary of what independent India was willing to legislate on personal law. The Uniform Civil Code debate, which continues today, starts from the gap between these two approaches.
234.remember: The Special Marriage Act is the closest India has to a Uniform Civil Code for marriage. A Hindu marrying a Muslim can use it without either converting. The 30-day public notice requirement was designed for objections but became a tool to harass inter-faith couples.
234.upsc: Tests the Act as enabling inter-faith marriages, its distinction from personal law marriages, the requirement for 30-day notice (subject to challenge as unconstitutional), and its relevance to debates on Uniform Civil Code.

235.label: Hindu Code Bills
235.what: The Hindu Code Bills (1955–56) reformed Hindu personal law, giving women equal rights in marriage, divorce, and inheritance.
235.connections: The Hindu Code Bills are the culmination of the legal reform arc from Sati Abolition (1829) through Widow Remarriage (1856) and Child Marriage Restraint (1929). They gave Hindu women equal inheritance and divorce rights for the first time. Ambedkar's resignation (which connects to his Constitutional card) over the dilution is the political detail. The four separate acts that resulted are the Hindu Marriage Act, Hindu Succession Act, Hindu Minority and Guardianship Act, and Hindu Adoptions and Maintenance Act.
235.remember: Ambedkar resigned saying Nehru lacked the courage to pass the full bill; it was later passed as four separate acts.
235.upsc: Tests Ambedkar's role as Law Minister and his resignation when the comprehensive bill was initially delayed.

236.label: Untouchability Offences Act
236.what: The Untouchability Offences Act (1955) criminalised the practice of untouchability, implementing Article 17 of the Constitution.
236.connections: Article 17 abolished untouchability in 1950; this Act made it criminally enforceable in 1955. The five-year gap between constitutional abolition and criminal law illustrates the enforcement distance that runs throughout this category. The arc from Mahad Satyagraha (1927) to Article 17 (1950) to this Act (1955) is 28 years from Ambedkar's first direct action to criminal law. The Protection of Civil Rights Act (1976) later strengthened it further.
236.remember: Article 17 is the only Fundamental Right that is 'absolute', it cannot be restricted under any circumstances.
236.upsc: Tests the difference between the 1955 Act and the later, stronger 1989 SC/ST Prevention of Atrocities Act.

237.label: States Reorganisation Act
237.what: The States Reorganisation Act (1956) created states along linguistic lines. Fourteen states and six UTs were initially formed.
237.connections: Linguistic reorganisation is the most significant redrawing of India's internal map since Independence. Potti Sriramulu's fast-to-death (1952) for Andhra forced Nehru's hand. The 1956 Act created 14 states on linguistic lines. Maharashtra-Gujarat (1960) and the northeastern state formations are its extensions. The principle it established (states follow languages) shaped Indian federalism more than any other single decision.
237.remember: Potti Sriramulu fasted to death demanding a separate Andhra state for Telugu speakers. He died after 58 days. Riots broke out. Nehru immediately announced the creation of Andhra Pradesh. Statehood by martyrdom.
237.upsc: Tests the Fazl Ali Commission and Andhra Pradesh as the first linguistic state (following Potti Sriramulu's fast).

238.label: India's first nuclear reactor Apsara
238.what: Apsara (1956) was India's (and Asia's) first nuclear reactor, designed by Homi Bhabha and built at Trombay.
238.connections: Apsara is the beginning of India's nuclear technology arc. Bhabha's civilian-first strategy made the nuclear programme politically defensible. The arc: Apsara (1956, reactor) → Pokhran-I (1974, first test) → Pokhran-II (1998, weapons-state declaration) → Agni-V (2012, delivery capability). The 18-year gap between reactor and bomb is the arc of India's nuclear maturation.
238.remember: Nehru suggested the name 'Apsara' (nymph); the reactor was critical for producing medical and industrial isotopes.
238.upsc: Tests Bhabha's 'Three-Stage Nuclear Power Programme' and the early push for nuclear self-sufficiency.

239.label: First Five-Year Plan completed
239.what: India's First Five Year Plan (1951-1956) concluded having exceeded its growth target of 2.1%, achieving 3.6% GDP growth. It focused on agriculture, irrigation, and power generation.
239.connections: The First Plan's above-target completion gave the planning model political legitimacy. Nehru's 'dams are the temples of modern India' belongs to this period: Bhakra-Nangal, Hirakud, Damodar Valley. The metaphor is the most compressed expression of Nehruvian secular developmentalism: replace religious infrastructure with industrial infrastructure as the object of national devotion.
239.remember: Nehru called dams the temples of modern India. The Bhakra-Nangal project, inaugurated during this period, remains one of Asia's largest dams and symbolised independent India's industrial ambition.
239.upsc: Tests the Harrod-Domar model used for the First Plan, the focus areas, and the contrast with the Second Plan (Mahalanobis model, heavy industry). Bhakra-Nangal dam was a flagship project.

240.label: B.R. Ambedkar converted to Buddhism
240.what: On 14 October 1956, B.R. Ambedkar converted to Buddhism at a public ceremony in Nagpur along with approximately 600,000 of his followers. He died six weeks later on 6 December 1956.
240.connections: Ambedkar announced in 1935 he would not die a Hindu; he kept his word in 1956. He chose Buddhism rather than Islam or Christianity deliberately: he wanted a religion that was non-Brahminical yet Indian in origin. His conversion with 600,000 followers is the largest mass religious conversion in modern Indian history. Pair with the birth card (1891) and Constitutional drafting card (1947) to see the complete arc.
240.remember: Ambedkar had announced he would not die a Hindu 21 years before he converted. He kept his word. He chose Buddhism because it was born in India, had no caste system, and emphasised reason over revelation. He died six weeks after the conversion, having fulfilled his final vow.
240.upsc: Tests the date and significance of the conversion, the Nagpur ceremony, Ambedkar's reasons (Buddhism rejected caste, was indigenous to India), and the Navayana Buddhism he practiced as distinct from traditional Buddhism.

241.label: AIIMS established in New Delhi
241.what: All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) was established in New Delhi in 1956 as an autonomous institution for medical education and research, created by an Act of Parliament.
241.connections: AIIMS being more competitive than IIT is the most striking admissions fact in Indian education. Built in the same Nehruvian decade as IIT Kharagpur (1951), it represents the state's simultaneous investment in technical and medical excellence. The AIIMS model (world-class excellence at the top, primary health neglect at the base) is the persistent criticism of Indian health policy.
241.remember: AIIMS New Delhi remains the most competitive educational institution in India. More students compete for AIIMS MBBS seats than for any other course in any country. The entrance exam is harder to crack than IIT-JEE. A doctor from AIIMS is recognised globally.
241.upsc: Tests AIIMS as an Institute of National Importance, the AIIMS Act 1956, the expansion to 22 AIIMS across India, and the institution's role in producing India's medical leadership.

242.label: Sputnik: first artificial satellite
242.what: Sputnik 1 (1957) was the first artificial satellite, launched by the USSR, triggering the Space Race with the US.
242.connections: Sputnik triggered the Space Race, which produced NASA, the Apollo programme, and eventually ARPANET (the internet's predecessor, funded by DARPA created in Sputnik's wake). Connect to India's space programme: ISRO was founded in 1969 (the same year Apollo 11 landed). The global space race that Sputnik started created the technological context into which India's space ambitions were born.
242.remember: Sputnik was the size of a beach ball and weighed 83 kg. The USA was humiliated. Congress immediately passed the National Defense Education Act pouring money into science education. The Space Race had begun.
242.upsc: Tests NASA's formation as a response and Sputnik's role in inspiring Vikram Sarabhai to start India's space program.

243.label: India's First Commonwealth gold
243.what: Milkha Singh won the gold medal in the 400m at the 1958 Commonwealth Games in Cardiff, becoming the first Indian athlete to win gold at the Commonwealth Games. He survived the 1947 partition as an orphan.
243.connections: Milkha's Commonwealth gold (1958) came 11 years after watching his family killed in Partition. His near-miss at Rome Olympics (1960) defines his career as much as his wins. The Flying Sikh title was given by Ayub Khan of Pakistan, his country's adversary, after Milkha beat Pakistani runners. Being honoured by your rival's head of state is the extraordinary footnote.
243.remember: Milkha Singh watched his parents and siblings killed in the 1947 partition. He ran, literally, for his life. He channelled that trauma into athletics. Pakistani General Ayub Khan called him the Flying Sikh after he defeated Pakistan's best runner. The man who survived partition became a symbol of partition's potential.
243.upsc: Tests Indian sporting history, the 1960 Rome Olympics where Milkha came fourth by 0.1 seconds (still discussed as India's greatest near-miss), and his personal story from partition refugee to national hero.

244.label: DRDO
244.what: The Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) was established on 1 January 1958 by integrating existing defence research institutions. It develops military technology including missiles, aircraft, and electronic warfare systems.
244.connections: DRDO's Mission Shakti (2019) made India one of four nations with anti-satellite capability. The 1958 founding reflects the post-Independence recognition that technology dependence is strategic vulnerability. Compare with TIFR (1945) as fundamental science versus DRDO as applied defence technology: India needed both, built both, in the same decade.
244.remember: DRDO launched Mission Shakti on 27 March 2019, destroying a live Indian satellite in low earth orbit with an anti-satellite missile. India became the fourth country after USA, Russia and China to demonstrate this capability. PM Modi announced it in a national address.
244.upsc: Tests DRDO's key programmes: Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme (Agni, Prithvi, Akash, Trishul, Nag), the Tejas light combat aircraft, and the ASAT test in 2019 (Mission Shakti).

245.label: India's first computer TIFRAC
245.what: TIFRAC (Tata Institute of Fundamental Research Automatic Calculator) became operational in 1960 at TIFR Mumbai. It was one of the first computers in Asia and the first computer designed and built in India.
245.connections: TIFRAC is the first instance of the technology-denial-and-domestic-development pattern that defines Indian science history. The pattern repeats: Cray supercomputers denied → Param built (1991); cryogenic engine technology denied by Russia → developed indigenously for GSLV Mk-III. TIFRAC is the first chapter of that recurring story.
245.remember: TIFRAC was built by a team led by R. Narasimhan. It took 5 years to build. The machine weighed several tonnes and occupied an entire room. India's IT journey from TIFRAC to a global software power took 60 years.
245.upsc: Tests India's early computing history. UPSC may ask about the transition from TIFRAC to modern computing, C-DAC's development of the Param supercomputer in 1991, and India's IT policy evolution.

246.label: Decolonisation wave in Africa
246.what: 1960 is called the Year of Africa , 17 African nations gained independence in a single year. British PM Macmillan's Wind of Change speech acknowledged that African nationalism was an unstoppable force.
246.connections: The Year of Africa (17 nations independent in 1960) is the political fulfilment of what WWII had made ideologically untenable. Macmillan's Wind of Change speech was delivered in South Africa, telling the apartheid government that the empire was ending. Compare with India's independence (1947) as the first major British decolonisation: India's independence made the case for African independence intellectually irresistible.
246.remember: Macmillan's Wind of Change speech was delivered in South Africa. He was telling the apartheid government to the face that colonialism was over. The South African PM did not clap. South Africa left the Commonwealth the following year.
246.upsc: Tests the decolonisation wave and its link to the Non-Aligned Movement, India's role in supporting African independence through the UN, and the Bandung Conference (1955) which preceded it.

247.label: Maharashtra and Gujarat created
247.what: Maharashtra and Gujarat were carved out of the bilingual Bombay State on 1 May 1960 following the Samyukta Maharashtra movement. The bifurcation completed the linguistic reorganisation of western India.
247.connections: The linguistic reorganisation of states is the most significant redrawing of India's internal map after Independence. The States Reorganisation Act (1956) established the principle; Maharashtra-Gujarat (1960) is the most contested application of it. The principle that states should follow linguistic lines was resisted by the Centre but ultimately accepted. Compare with the French model of administrative uniformity versus India's solution of linguistic federalism as alternative approaches to managing diversity.
247.remember: Bombay city was the contentious prize. Both Maharashtra and Gujarat claimed it. The States Reorganisation Commission of 1955 had actually recommended keeping Bombay under central administration to avoid the dispute. The demand for Bombay as Maharashtra's capital was only won after 105 people died.
247.upsc: Tests the Samyukta Maharashtra movement (1956-1960), the deaths of 105 protesters in police firing that preceded the bifurcation, and 1 May as Maharashtra Day and Gujarat Day.

248.label: Non-Aligned Movement
248.what: The Non-Aligned Movement took institutional shape at Belgrade in 1961 under leaders including Nehru, Nasser, Tito, Sukarno, and Nkrumah, as newly independent states tried to avoid being locked into either Cold War bloc.
248.connections: The NAM was Nehru's most significant geopolitical contribution. In a bipolar Cold War world, it created a third category that refused to join either bloc. Nehru, Nasser, Tito, Sukarno, and Nkrumah at Belgrade in 1961 is the founding moment. The principle of strategic autonomy the NAM represented continues in India's foreign policy today, even if the movement itself lost practical relevance after the Cold War.
248.remember: Non-alignment never meant sitting quietly on the fence. It meant keeping your freedom to choose.
248.upsc: Tests the five 'Panchsheel' principles (1954) and India's leadership role in the decolonised 'Third World'.

249.label: Goa liberation
249.what: India launched Operation Vijay on 18 December 1961, and the Indian Army entered Goa, ending 451 years of Portuguese colonial rule. Portugal surrendered in 36 hours.
249.connections: Operation Vijay ended 451 years of Portuguese presence, the longest European colonial occupation on the subcontinent. The USA and UK condemned it at the UN; the Soviet Union vetoed the resolution. Pair the Colonial India card (Portuguese captured Goa, 1510) with this one: the 451-year span between the two cards is the complete Portuguese India arc.
249.remember: The USA and UK condemned India's military action in the UN Security Council. The Soviet Union vetoed the resolution against India. Nehru was called a hypocrite for using force after preaching non-violence. He said Goa was never a colony by choice.
249.upsc: Tests why Nehru delayed action on Goa (non-violence principle, international pressure), the criticism from Western powers, and the contrast with India's position on other colonial territories.

250.label: Dowry Prohibition Act
