250.what: The Dowry Prohibition Act of 1961 made the giving and taking of dowry a criminal offence punishable with imprisonment. Despite the law, dowry deaths continue and the Dowry Prohibition Rules were strengthened in 1985.
250.connections: The Dowry Prohibition Act is the clearest example in this category of the gap between legislation and social change. The Domestic Violence Act (2005) addresses related harm through a different mechanism: instead of criminalising a transaction, it recognises a pattern of behaviour as the harm. Together the two cards frame a central question in Indian social reform: when does legal reform actually change social behaviour, and when does it only change the form the harm takes?
250.remember: India records approximately 7,000 dowry deaths per year despite the law. The 1961 Act was well-intentioned but enforcement has been historically weak. The 1983 amendment which added dowry harassment as a cognisable offence was more effective but led to misuse complaints.
250.upsc: Tests Section 498A IPC (dowry harassment), Section 304B IPC (dowry death), the 1983 amendments adding these sections, and India's statistics on dowry deaths which remain among the highest in the world.

251.label: Sino-Indian War
251.what: The India-China War (1962) saw China invade across the McMahon Line and Aksai Chin. India suffered a humiliating defeat.
251.connections: The 1962 war is the most significant foreign policy failure of Nehru's tenure. The Hindi-Chini bhai-bhai slogan of the 1950s made the betrayal more psychologically damaging than a purely military defeat. Aksai Chin remains unresolved today. Nehru's health declined rapidly after the war; he died 18 months later. Compare with Kargil (1999): different adversary, different outcome, same Himalayan strategic terrain.
251.remember: The shock of this betrayal by China is often said to have contributed to Nehru's rapid decline in health shortly after.
251.upsc: Tests the border dispute, the failure of the 'Forward Policy', and the impact on Nehru's health and foreign policy legacy.

252.label: Cuban Missile Crisis
252.what: The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) was a 13-day standoff between the US and USSR over Soviet nukes in Cuba. It nearly led to nuclear war.
252.connections: The Cuban Missile Crisis came closer to nuclear war than any other moment in history. Vasili Arkhipov's refusal to authorise a nuclear torpedo launch from a Soviet submarine may have been the single act that prevented nuclear war. The crisis directly produced the Moscow-Washington hotline (red phone). Pair with the resolution card (#272): the 13-day arc from confrontation to secret deal is one history's most consequential diplomatic negotiations.
252.remember: One Soviet officer, Vasili Arkhipov, saved the world by refusing to authorize a nuclear torpedo launch during the crisis.
252.upsc: Tests the resolution (hotline established) and the shift toward 'Détente' (easing of tensions) between the superpowers.

253.label: Cuban Missile Crisis
253.what: The 13-day Cuban Missile Crisis ended on 28 October 1962 when Khrushchev agreed to remove Soviet missiles from Cuba. The secret deal also included US removal of Jupiter missiles from Turkey and a no-invasion pledge on Cuba.
253.connections: The public deal: Soviets remove Cuba missiles, US promises not to invade Cuba. The secret deal: US removes Turkey missiles (which was the actual Soviet demand). The gap between the public narrative and the actual resolution is the most important detail. The crisis also produced the direct Moscow-Washington communication line. Pair with the beginning card (#160): together they tell the complete 13-day arc.
253.remember: The public deal was: Soviets remove Cuba missiles, US promises not to invade Cuba. The secret deal was: US removes missiles from Turkey within 6 months. Kennedy never admitted to the Turkey part. Khrushchev kept the secret too. The world never knew how close it came.
253.upsc: Tests the resolution mechanism, the establishment of the Moscow-Washington hotline, and the shift toward nuclear arms control that followed (Partial Test Ban Treaty 1963, NPT 1968).

254.label: Lal Bahadur Shastri
254.what: Lal Bahadur Shastri became India's second Prime Minister on 9 June 1964 after Jawaharlal Nehru died in office. He served until his sudden death in Tashkent on 11 January 1966.
254.connections: Shastri's brevity in office (1964-1966) produced two of independent India's most significant moments: the 1965 war and his death at Tashkent the night after signing the peace agreement. Jai Jawan Jai Kisan is his legacy: soldier and farmer as twin foundations of national security and food security. His unexplained death in Tashkent remains the most questioned death of any Indian PM.
254.remember: Shastri was so small in physical stature that his feet barely touched the floor when seated in Nehru's chair. He was so frugal that after his death his family was found to be in debt. His simplicity made him beloved.
254.upsc: Tests his slogan Jai Jawan Jai Kisan, leadership in the 1965 war, the Tashkent Agreement, and his death in mysterious circumstances. He remains the only PM to die outside India while in office.

255.label: Indo-Pak War
255.what: The India-Pakistan War of 1965 began with Pakistan's Operation Gibraltar. It ended with the UN-brokered Tashkent Agreement.
255.connections: The 1965 war ended in military stalemate but diplomatic success. Pakistan's Operation Gibraltar triggered the conventional response. The Tashkent Agreement restored the pre-war status quo. Shastri's death hours after signing it is the biographical shadow. The war reinforced Pakistan's belief in military parity with India, a calculation the 1971 war decisively corrected.
255.remember: Shastri is the only Indian PM to die in office abroad; the circumstances of his death in the USSR remain a subject of mystery.
255.upsc: Tests Lal Bahadur Shastri's leadership ('Jai Jawan Jai Kisan') and his sudden death in Tashkent after signing the peace deal.

256.label: Green Revolution in India
256.what: The Green Revolution (c. 1966) introduced high-yielding seeds (HYV) and fertilizers, making India food-surplus within a decade.
256.connections: India imported 10 million tonnes of wheat in 1966; by 1972 it was exporting. The HYV seeds, chemical fertilisers, and irrigation infrastructure transformed Punjab and Haryana. Swaminathan's later regret about soil degradation and groundwater depletion is the long-term cost acknowledged by the architect. Pair with the Swaminathan Personalities card for the human story behind the policy numbers.
256.remember: India imported 10 million tonnes of wheat in 1966. By 1972 it exported wheat for the first time. The same land, same farmers, same country. The difference was a seed developed in Mexico by Norman Borlaug.
256.upsc: Tests M.S. Swaminathan's role, the geographic concentration (Punjab, Haryana, western UP), the long-term problems (groundwater depletion, soil degradation, farmer debt), and the second Green Revolution debate.

257.label: Indira Gandhi
257.what: Indira Gandhi became Prime Minister in January 1966 after Lal Bahadur Shastri's death, becoming India's first and only woman Prime Minister. She served until 1977 and again from 1980 until her assassination in 1984.
257.connections: Indira Gandhi's arc from 'Goongi Gudiya' to the most dominant Indian politician of the 20th century is one of political history's most complete reversals of underestimation. Bank nationalisation (1969), Bangladesh war (1971), Emergency (1975), Operation Blue Star (1984), assassination (1984) are the five defining Post-Independence cards that radiate from this anchor.
257.remember: Party elders thought she would be a 'Goongi Gudiya' (dumb doll), but she quickly outmanoeuvred them to take total control.
257.upsc: Tests her populist policies (Bank nationalisation, Privy Purses abolition) and the eventual imposition of the Emergency.

258.label: India's Green Revolution wheat variety
258.what: The Green Revolution from 1966 introduced High Yielding Varieties of wheat and rice developed by Norman Borlaug and adapted by M.S. Swaminathan, transforming India from a food-deficit to a food-surplus nation.
258.connections: The Science & Tech card for the Green Revolution focuses on the HYV seed technology. Compare with the Post-Independence Green Revolution card (1966) which covers policy impact. Together they tell the complete story: this card is the science (HYV seeds, fertiliser, irrigation); the Post-Independence card is the political and social consequence (food surplus, soil degradation, water depletion).
258.remember: It helped India escape famine-era vulnerability, but the later bill showed up in groundwater stress, chemical dependence, and regional imbalance.
258.upsc: Tests M.S. Swaminathan's role as father of Green Revolution in India, the states most affected (Punjab, Haryana, western UP), and the long-term problems: groundwater depletion, soil degradation, regional inequality.

259.label: M.S. Swaminathan Green Revolution
259.what: M.S. Swaminathan collaborated with Norman Borlaug to develop High Yielding Variety wheat seeds adapted to Indian conditions, launching the Green Revolution that transformed India from a food-deficit to food-surplus nation.
259.connections: Swaminathan's collaboration with Borlaug on HYV seeds connects to the Green Revolution Post-Independence card (1966). His later regret about the environmental costs (soil degradation, groundwater depletion) is the most intellectually honest statement made by any figure in this category about the long-term costs of a policy that saved millions of lives.
259.remember: Swaminathan said later in life that the Green Revolution was a mixed blessing. He had saved millions from starvation but created dependence on chemical fertilisers and depleted groundwater. He spent the second half of his career designing the Evergreen Revolution to fix what the first revolution broke.
259.upsc: Tests Swaminathan's specific contribution (adapting Borlaug's Mexican wheat to Indian conditions), his Swaminathan Commission Report (2004-2006) on farmer welfare, and the criticism that the Green Revolution's ecological costs were not anticipated.

260.label: Bank Nationalisation
260.what: Bank Nationalisation (1969) saw Indira Gandhi take over 14 private banks to expand credit to farmers and small industries.
260.connections: Bank nationalisation was Indira Gandhi's most popular early economic policy. Its political significance exceeds its economic one: she used it to split the Congress and emerge as champion of the poor. The Garibi Hatao (remove poverty) slogan launched the same year made this the most populist political moment of the first two decades. It is the economic complement to the Bangladesh war as the two pillars of her 1971 dominance.
260.remember: It was one of the most popular socialist moves in Indian history, transforming Indira into a champion of the poor overnight.
260.upsc: Tests the political context (her split with the Congress 'Syndicate') and its lasting impact on Indian banking accessibility.

261.label: Moon landing (Apollo 11)
261.what: Apollo 11 (1969) saw Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin become the first humans to walk on the Moon.
261.connections: The Apollo 11 landing is the endpoint of the Space Race that Sputnik (1957) started. Armstrong's missing 'a' was a transmission glitch that ambiguated the most quoted line in exploration history. Connect to India's Chandrayaan-3 (2023): India landed at the lunar south pole 54 years after the first human landing, on a budget that would not have funded a single Apollo mission year.
261.remember: Armstrong's famous quote: 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind', the 'a' was lost in transmission.
261.upsc: Tests the Space Race context, the Cold War competition with the Soviet Union, and how the Moon landing inspired India's space programme. Vikram Sarabhai, who founded ISRO, died in 1971 before seeing India's own achievements.

262.label: ISRO
262.what: The Indian Space Research Organisation was established on 15 August 1969 under the visionary physicist Vikram Sarabhai, who believed space technology could solve India's developmental problems.
262.connections: ISRO's founding story (rockets transported on bicycles, launched from a church near the magnetic equator at Thumba) is the most vivid origin story in Indian science. Vikram Sarabhai designed a space programme for a developing country because he understood that space technology has direct civilian applications: communications, weather forecasting, remote sensing. Every INSAT and Chandrayaan mission is his institutional descendant. Note: card #235 in Post-Independence covers the same event and is merged into this one.
262.remember: India's first rocket parts were transported on a bicycle; today ISRO launches satellites for dozens of other nations.
262.upsc: Tests ISRO's philosophy and its evolution from transport by bicycle to a global leader in low-cost satellite launches.

263.label: Operation Flood (White Revolution)
263.what: Operation Flood (1970) led by Verghese Kurien made India the world's largest milk producer via the 'Amul' model.
263.connections: Verghese Kurien's White Revolution is the food security parallel to the Green Revolution: where Swaminathan worked on grains, Kurien worked on dairy. Both transformed India from deficit to surplus. The Amul model (cooperatives owned by producers, not corporations) is the institutional innovation: it kept value with farmers. Compare with SEWA (1972) in Social Reform as a roughly contemporary cooperative model applied to informal labour.
263.remember: Amul started with just two village cooperatives; today it is a multi-billion dollar brand owned by 3.6 million farmers.
263.upsc: Tests Verghese Kurien as the Milkman of India, the Amul cooperative model in Anand Gujarat, the three phases of Operation Flood (1970-1996), and India's current status as top milk producer.

264.label: Bangladesh Liberation War
264.what: The Bangladesh Liberation War (1971) ended with Pakistan's surrender and the creation of Bangladesh. India intervened after mass killings in East Pakistan.
264.connections: The 1971 war is India's most complete military and strategic victory. Pakistan signed the Instrument of Surrender in Dhaka on 16 December 1971. The creation of Bangladesh split Pakistan geographically and psychologically. The Soviet treaty (August 1971) deterred US intervention. The 93,000 Pakistani POWs gave India leverage at Simla (1972) that historians argue was underused by not pressing for a final Kashmir settlement.
264.remember: Pakistan's General Niazi surrendered to Indian General Jagjit Singh Aurora in Dhaka on 16 December 1971. Over 90,000 Pakistani soldiers laid down their arms, the largest surrender since WWII. Indira Gandhi was called Durga.
264.upsc: Tests the Simla Agreement (1972), the 93,000 Pakistani POWs, Indira Gandhi's international diplomacy, the US-Pakistan-China axis against India, and the India-Soviet Treaty of Friendship signed in August 1971.

265.label: Privy Purses
265.what: Indira Gandhi abolished the privy purses paid to former rulers of princely states through the 26th Constitutional Amendment, ending an annual payment that had been guaranteed at the time of accession.
265.connections: The abolition of privy purses removed the financial entitlements guaranteed to former rulers as part of the 1947 integration agreements. Abolishing a constitutional guarantee required a constitutional amendment, which Indira used in 1971 alongside bank nationalisation and the Bangladesh war victory to consolidate total political dominance. It completes the integration project Patel began in 1947.
265.remember: The Nizam of Hyderabad received Rs. 50 lakh annually as privy purse. The Maharaja of Mysore received Rs. 26 lakh. Total annual outflow was about Rs. 5 crore. Indira abolished it partly for savings, mostly for politics.
265.upsc: Tests the 26th Amendment, the Supreme Court's initial striking down of the Presidential Order (before the Amendment), and this as part of Indira's Garibi Hatao socialist agenda before the 1971 elections.

266.label: Simla Agreement
266.what: The Simla Agreement was signed by Indira Gandhi and Zulfikar Ali Bhutto in July 1972 after the 1971 war. Both sides agreed to resolve disputes bilaterally, and the ceasefire line in Jammu and Kashmir was converted into the Line of Control.
266.connections: The Simla Agreement converted the 1971 ceasefire line into the Line of Control and committed both sides to bilateral resolution. The 93,000 POW question was India's primary leverage. Critics argue Indira should have pressed for a final Kashmir resolution at this moment of maximum advantage. The bilateral framework it created has since been used by Pakistan to resist third-party mediation.
266.remember: India released 93,000 POWs in exchange for the agreement; critics argue Gandhi should have pushed for a final Kashmir settlement then.
266.upsc: Tests the principle of resolving disputes 'bilaterally' without third-party interference (like the UN).

267.label: SEWA
267.what: Ela Bhatt founded the Self Employed Women's Association (SEWA) in Ahmedabad in 1972, organising women workers in the informal economy , rag pickers, street vendors, home-based workers , into a trade union.
267.connections: SEWA is the institutional model for organising informal economy workers. The bank started with Rs. 10 membership fees from rag pickers and is now a major cooperative. Compare with the Servants of India Society (1905) as a different model of organised social service. SEWA's significance is proving the informal economy can be organised without formal employment contracts.
267.remember: SEWA Bank was started with Rs. 10 membership fees from rag pickers and vegetable vendors. It is now a cooperative bank with hundreds of thousands of members. The women who swept streets and sold vegetables created their own bank because no commercial bank would serve them.
267.upsc: Tests SEWA as India's largest trade union for informal sector workers, Ela Bhatt's contribution to microfinance and women's economic empowerment, and SEWA Bank as a pioneer of financial inclusion for poor women.

268.label: Dalit Panthers
268.what: Namdeo Dhasal founded the Dalit Panthers in Mumbai in 1972, inspired by the American Black Panthers. It was a militant response to continuing atrocities against Dalits despite constitutional protections.
268.connections: The Dalit Panthers connect the American civil rights movement to Indian caste politics explicitly: the name is borrowed deliberately. They represent the shift from Ambedkar's constitutional method (20+ years of legal strategy) to confrontational political mobilisation. Compare with Periyar's Self-Respect Movement (1925) as an earlier example of the same shift. The Mandal implementation (1990) is the electoral expression of the same Dalit and OBC political assertion the Panthers represented.
268.remember: The Dalit Panthers' manifesto called for land redistribution, equal wages, and an end to untouchability through collective resistance, not just constitutional appeals. It represented the frustration of a generation that had waited 25 years for Ambedkar's constitutional promises to materialise.
268.upsc: Tests the Dalit Panthers as a response to the failure of constitutional protections to prevent Dalit atrocities, Dhasal's poetry as a vehicle for Dalit assertion, and the broader context of Ambedkar's legacy in post-independence Maharashtra.

269.label: Project Tiger
269.what: Project Tiger was launched on 1 April 1973 by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi to protect tigers from extinction. Nine tiger reserves were initially created across India.
269.connections: Project Tiger is one of the world's most successful wildlife conservation programmes. The tiger population had fallen from 40,000 at Independence to 1,827 by 1973; it is now above 3,000. The programme is cited globally as evidence that government-led conservation with community involvement can reverse species decline. Indira Gandhi's personal commitment to conservation is a detail frequently omitted from her political biography.
269.remember: India's tiger population had fallen from an estimated 40,000 at independence to just 1,827 when Project Tiger launched. The 2022 census counted 3,167 tigers. India now hosts about 75% of the world's striking tiger population.
269.upsc: Tests the Wildlife Protection Act 1972 which preceded it, the current number of tiger reserves, India's tiger census, and the National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) established under the 2006 amendment.

270.label: Kesavananda Bharati judgment
270.what: The Supreme Court in Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala (1973) held 7-6 that while Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution, it cannot destroy its basic structure. This is the most important constitutional judgment in Indian history.
270.connections: The Basic Structure doctrine is the Constitution's immune system: Parliament can change everything except the core. The 42nd Amendment (1976) was partly a response to this judgment, attempting to override it during the Emergency. The 44th Amendment (1978) restored the balance. Compare with the NJAC judgment (2015) as the most recent major assertion of judicial independence against a unanimous Parliament.
270.remember: It was decided by just ONE vote (7-6). If one judge had changed his mind, politicians could technically have voted to end democracy or secularism tomorrow.
270.upsc: The single most important judgment for UPSC. It saved Indian democracy from becoming a legal dictatorship.

271.label: India's first nuclear test (Pokhran-I)
271.what: India's first nuclear test, 'Smiling Buddha' (1974), was conducted at Pokhran. India called it a 'Peaceful Nuclear Explosion' (PNE).
271.connections: Smiling Buddha (1974) was called a Peaceful Nuclear Explosion to maintain non-weaponisation claims. The NSG was created specifically in response to it, and for 32 years India was denied nuclear technology. Pokhran-II (1998) ended that isolation. The two Pokhran tests bracket a 24-year period of nuclear exclusion. Note: card #240 covers the same event and should be merged into this one.
271.remember: The USA was completely surprised. India had deceived them by disguising preparations as a military exercise. Henry Kissinger was furious. Pakistan's Bhutto told his generals: we will eat grass if necessary, but we will make a bomb.
271.upsc: Tests the international reaction (Pakistan accelerated its own programme, USA imposed sanctions), the difference from Pokhran-II (1998), and India's nuclear doctrine of no-first-use.

272.label: Jayaprakash Narayan's Total Revolution
272.what: Jayaprakash Narayan (JP) launched his Total Revolution movement in 1974, calling for transformation of all aspects of Indian society. His Bihar movement against corruption became the catalyst for the 1975 Emergency.
272.connections: JP's Total Revolution movement directly precedes the Emergency (1975). His call for soldiers not to follow unconstitutional orders was the most dangerous statement made to Indira Gandhi, contributing to the Emergency decision. The students he mobilised produced Nitish Kumar, Lalu Prasad, and the non-Congress political class that eventually produced the V.P. Singh government.
272.remember: JP told the army and police publicly that they were not bound to follow unconstitutional orders. This was the speech that Indira Gandhi used as justification for declaring Emergency. Indira feared a repeat of 1917 Russia. JP was arrested within hours of the Emergency declaration.
272.upsc: Tests JP's Total Revolution concept (political, social, economic, cultural, educational, moral, intellectual revolutions together), his call to the police and military to disobey unjust orders, and the formation of the Janata Party that defeated Indira in 1977.

273.label: Emergency in India
273.what: The Emergency (1975–77) was declared by Indira Gandhi, suspending fundamental rights and censoring the press for 21 months.
273.connections: The Emergency (1975-77) is the only period in independent India's history when democracy was suspended. The 42nd Amendment (1976) was passed during this period with opposition MPs in jail. Indira called elections in 1977 believing she would win; she lost to the Janata coalition. The Emergency's ending by Indira's own electoral decision is the defining paradox: the person who suspended democracy restored it.
273.remember: Opponents were arrested at midnight before the announcement. It only ended when Indira called for elections in 1977 and lost.
273.upsc: Tests the 44th Amendment that strengthened safeguards against Emergency abuse, the JP Movement that preceded it, the 1977 election defeat, and the Emergency as a turning point in Indian democracy.

274.label: Vietnam War
274.what: The Vietnam War ended on 30 April 1975 when North Vietnamese forces captured Saigon, reunifying Vietnam under communist rule. The USA lost 58,000 soldiers and the war cost over 100 billion dollars.
274.connections: The helicopter evacuation from the Saigon embassy roof is the image that defined defeat for a generation of American policymakers. The war's end also demonstrated that military power alone cannot overcome political will: North Vietnam had a fraction of the US's resources and won. Compare with the Korean War (armistice 1953): both ended without clear American victory, shaping US military doctrine for decades.
274.remember: The last US personnel fled Saigon by helicopter from the embassy roof as the city fell. The iconic photograph of the evacuation became a symbol of American defeat. Vietnam was later the fastest growing economy in Asia.
274.upsc: Tests India's position (supported North Vietnam diplomatically while maintaining ties with USA), the domino theory, and how Vietnam's defeat of a superpower influenced liberation movements worldwide.

275.label: India's first satellite Aryabhata
275.what: Aryabhata (1975) was India's first satellite, launched by a Soviet rocket. It studied X-rays and solar physics.
275.connections: Aryabhata is the first chapter of India's space self-reliance arc. Launched on a Soviet rocket because India could not yet build its own launch vehicle, it proved India could design and build complex satellites. The arc runs: Aryabhata (satellite, Soviet rocket, 1975) → SLV-3 (own rocket, 1980) → PSLV (reliable workhorse, 1994) → GSLV Mk-III (heavy lift, 2017) → Chandrayaan-3 (lunar landing, 2023).
275.remember: Although it failed after four days in orbit, Aryabhata proved India could build complex satellites indigenously.
275.upsc: Tests that India did not launch Aryabhata itself but relied on a Soviet rocket, marking India's entry into the space age. UPSC traces India's growing launch independence from 1975 onward.

276.label: 42nd Constitutional Amendment
276.what: The 42nd Amendment (1976), the 'Mini-Constitution', added 'Socialist, Secular, Integrity' to the Preamble and curtailed the courts.
276.connections: Pair with the 44th Amendment (1978) as cause and correction: the 42nd was Emergency-era constitutional damage; the 44th was the post-Emergency repair. The 42nd also attempted to override Kesavananda Bharati (1973) by reducing judicial review. The Supreme Court held in Minerva Mills (1980) that even this was unconstitutional. The Parliament-vs-Court conflict continues through every decade in this category.
276.remember: This was the only time the Preamble was ever amended; the changes were made while many opposition MPs were in jail.
276.upsc: Tests the addition of 'Fundamental Duties' (Art 51A) and the sweeping powers given to the executive during the Emergency.

277.label: 44th Constitutional Amendment
277.what: The 44th Amendment (1978) restored many rights, reclassifying the 'Right to Property' as a legal right under Article 300A.
277.connections: The 44th Amendment pairs with the 42nd (1976) as post-Emergency repair. Its most structurally significant change is the demotion of the Right to Property from fundamental right to legal right, covered separately in the Right to Property card. Making Article 21 non-suspendable during emergencies is the most durable reform: it means no future Emergency can legalise arbitrary detention the way 1975 did.
277.remember: It ensured that Article 21 (Life and Liberty) cannot be suspended even during an emergency, a direct result of 1975 lessons.
277.upsc: Tests the safeguards added against Emergency abuse (Cabinet written advice, 'Armed Rebellion' term).

278.label: Menaka Gandhi judgment
278.what: The Supreme Court in Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India (1978) held that the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to live with human dignity and cannot be taken away by any arbitrary procedure. It expanded Article 21 to cover due process.
278.connections: Maneka Gandhi transformed Article 21 from a narrow procedural guarantee into a broad substantive right. Before this judgment, the state could restrict life and liberty by any procedure established by law, however arbitrary. After it, the procedure must also be just, fair, and reasonable. This judgment is the direct foundation for the right to privacy (2017) and every other right subsequently read into Article 21.
278.remember: The government had impounded Maneka Gandhi's passport without giving reasons. The Supreme Court said the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21, and any procedure curtailing it must be fair, just, and reasonable. That last phrase opened Article 21 to become the most expansive right in the Constitution.
278.upsc: Tests the shift from the narrow AK Gopalan (1950) interpretation to the expansive Maneka Gandhi reading of Article 21, and how subsequent judgments used Maneka to derive rights to education, health, livelihood, and shelter from Article 21.

279.label: Right to Property
279.what: The 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) removed the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights (Article 19 and Article 31) and made it a constitutional right under Article 300A , enforceable but not a fundamental right.
279.connections: The arc runs: First Amendment (1951) added the Ninth Schedule to protect land reform from the right to property; this card removes the right to property from fundamental rights entirely 27 years later. The conflict between property rights and redistributive policy runs through the first three decades of Indian constitutionalism. This card is the resolution. Pair with the Hindu Code Bills (1955) in Social Reform: both address the same underlying tension between inherited rights and legislative reform.
279.remember: Property was the most litigated Fundamental Right in India's first 28 years. Zamindars used it to challenge land reform. The government used successive amendments to restrict it. Eventually Indira Gandhi removed it entirely. Property went from being among the most sacred rights to not being a fundamental right at all.
279.upsc: Tests the progression: Property as FR in original Constitution, curtailed by 1st and 4th Amendments, removed entirely by 44th Amendment. Article 300A still protects property from arbitrary acquisition without compensation.

280.label: Mother Teresa won Nobel Peace Prize
280.what: Mother Teresa (1910–1997) won the Nobel Peace Prize (1979) for her work with the poor in Calcutta via the Missionaries of Charity.
280.connections: Mother Teresa's refusal of the Nobel banquet and donation of the entire prize money is the biographical detail that defines her relationship with wealth. Her work in Calcutta's slums is the institutional expression of Ramakrishna's teaching. Compare with Amartya Sen's Nobel (1998) as the other major Nobel in this category: one addresses poverty through service, one through economic theory.
280.remember: Teresa donated the entire prize money, and the cost of the formal banquet, directly to her work in the slums.
280.upsc: Tests her Indian citizenship (1948), her Bharat Ratna (1980), and her canonisation as 'Saint Teresa' in 2016.

281.label: Iran hostage crisis
281.what: Iranian revolutionaries seized the US Embassy in Tehran on 4 November 1979 and held 52 Americans hostage for 444 days following the Islamic Revolution and US support for the Shah.
281.connections: The hostages were released the exact minute Ronald Reagan was inaugurated, fuelling the October Surprise theory (that Reagan's team negotiated the delay to damage Carter). The crisis ended the Carter presidency. It also produced the Delta Force rescue failure that led to a wholesale reform of US special operations capability. The post-hostage-crisis US Special Operations Command is one of the most significant institutional consequences.
281.remember: The hostages were released on the exact minute Ronald Reagan was inaugurated as President, 20 January 1981. They had been held 444 days. Carter had spent his entire presidency trying to free them. The timing was deliberate humiliation.
281.upsc: Tests the Islamic Revolution of 1979, Khomeini's ideology, the US-Iran relationship breakdown, and the crisis as the beginning of the current Iran-US standoff that affects oil prices and regional security.

282.label: India's first SLV-3 rocket launch
282.what: India's first Satellite Launch Vehicle SLV-3 successfully placed the Rohini satellite into orbit on 18 July 1980. The mission was led by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, making India one of six nations with indigenous launch capability.
282.connections: SLV-3 made India the sixth nation with indigenous launch capability. Kalam's public acceptance of responsibility for the 1979 failure before the 1980 success is the leadership detail. Owning a launch vehicle means not depending on foreign goodwill for satellite access: every PSLV and GSLV flight is built on the foundation SLV-3 proved.
282.remember: After the 1979 test failed, Kalam took responsibility; the 1980 success made India the 6th nation with launch capability.
282.upsc: Tests Abdul Kalam's role, the significance of indigenous launch capability, and the failed first attempt in 1979. SLV-3 was the foundation for India's later ASLV and PSLV programmes.

283.label: Mandal Commission Report
283.what: The Second Backward Classes Commission, chaired by B.P. Mandal, submitted its report in 1980 recommending 27% reservation in central government jobs for Other Backward Classes. It sat on a shelf for 10 years before V.P. Singh implemented it in 1990.
283.connections: The Mandal Commission report was submitted to Indira Gandhi who buried it for political reasons. V.P. Singh's 1990 implementation (Social Reform card) triggered nationwide protests. The 10-year gap between submission and implementation is the political story: the OBC mobilisation that grew in the intervening decade made Mandal politically irresistible by 1990. The report was not new in 1990; its political moment had finally arrived.
283.remember: The report was submitted to Indira Gandhi who buried it for political reasons. V.P. Singh implemented it in 1990 knowing it would be politically explosive. It ended his government. But it permanently changed Indian electoral politics.
283.upsc: Tests the distinction between the report submission (1980) and implementation (1990), the Indra Sawhney case (1992), the 50% cap on total reservations, and the exclusion of the creamy layer.

284.label: APPLE satellite by India
284.what: APPLE (Ariane Passenger Payload Experiment) was India's first experimental geostationary communication satellite, launched in 1981 by the European Ariane rocket. It demonstrated India's ability to build communication satellites.
284.connections: The bullock cart image (APPLE transported by bullock cart because the suspension was better than a truck) is the most photographed moment in Indian space history. The satellite validated Indian geostationary technology. INSAT-1B (1983) and every subsequent communication satellite in India's network descends directly from APPLE.
284.remember: During testing, the satellite was famously transported on a bullock cart to avoid vibration, an iconic image of Indian science.
284.upsc: Tests the sequence of India's satellite development: Aryabhata (1975), Bhaskara (1979), APPLE (1981), INSAT-1 (1982). APPLE proved the technology used in INSAT satellites.

285.label: INSAT-1B, India's first weather satellite
285.what: INSAT-1B, launched in 1983, was the first fully operational satellite of the Indian National Satellite System. It provided telecommunications, broadcasting, and meteorological services, transforming weather prediction across India.
285.connections: Before INSAT, India had no cyclone early warning system. After INSAT, coastal fishermen received weather warnings that saved thousands of lives annually. The same satellite infrastructure supported Doordarshan's national broadcast expansion. Compare with Chandrayaan missions as prestige science versus INSAT as applied civilian technology: India needed both, and INSAT proved the civilian case for space investment.
285.remember: Before INSAT, India had no early warning for cyclones. After INSAT, fishermen received weather warnings and coastal communities were evacuated. The same satellite that brought Doordarshan to villages also started saving lives from storms. Technology's highest purpose is when it saves lives.
285.upsc: Tests the INSAT series as India's first operational satellite system, its role in Doordarshan expansion (television reached rural India via INSAT), and the improvement in cyclone prediction that reduced coastal deaths.

286.label: Operation Blue Star
286.what: Operation Blue Star (1984) saw the Indian Army storm the Golden Temple to remove Sikh militants led by Bhindranwale.
286.connections: Operation Blue Star is the most consequential unilateral decision of Indira Gandhi's second term. The damage to the Akal Takht created a wound in Sikh sentiment that the stated military objective could not justify. The assassination chain runs: Blue Star (June 1984), assassination by Sikh bodyguards (October 1984), anti-Sikh riots (1984). These four events are inseparable.
286.remember: The damage to the Akal Takht deeply wounded Sikh sentiment, leading to decades of political and social fallout in Punjab.
286.upsc: Tests the Khalistan movement context, the political miscalculations that led Indira to allow Bhindranwale into the temple complex, and the consequences: Indira's assassination by her Sikh bodyguards five months later.

287.label: Bhopal Gas Tragedy
287.what: The Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984) was the world's worst industrial disaster, caused by a methyl isocyanate leak from a Union Carbide plant.
287.connections: Bhopal is the world's worst industrial disaster by death toll. The settlement (Rs. 715 crore for 500,000 victims) is the corporate liability failure that defined India's environmental law framework for a generation. The site remains contaminated 40 years later. Union Carbide was acquired by Dow Chemical, which inherited the liability dispute. The compensation per victim remains among the lowest in industrial accident history globally.
287.remember: Even 40 years later, the site remains contaminated, and the long-term health effects still impact thousands of survivors.
287.upsc: Tests the legal aftermath, environmental liability laws, and the failure to bring CEO Warren Anderson to justice.

288.label: Indira Gandhi
288.what: Indira Gandhi was assassinated (31 Oct 1984) by her Sikh bodyguards in retaliation for Blue Star, followed by anti-Sikh riots in Delhi.
288.connections: Indira's assassination by her Sikh bodyguards was the direct consequence of Blue Star. She had been warned to remove them and refused. The anti-Sikh riots that followed were the worst post-independence communal violence in northern India. Rajiv Gandhi's 404-seat sympathy victory in the December 1984 election is the largest majority in Indian electoral history, directly caused by her death.
288.remember: Indira had been warned to remove her Sikh bodyguards after Operation Blue Star. She refused, saying she trusted them. Her last public words the night before were about not minding if she died, if India's flame of democracy burned on.
288.upsc: Tests the 1984 anti-Sikh riots, the political consequences (Rajiv Gandhi's landslide victory), and the riots as a defining moment in Indian communal history. The riots' political responsibility remains contested.

289.label: Rajiv Gandhi
289.what: Rajiv Gandhi became Prime Minister at 40, the youngest in India's history, after his mother Indira's assassination in 1984. Congress won 404 of 514 seats in the subsequent election, the largest majority in Indian history.
289.connections: Rajiv's 404-seat majority after Indira's assassination is the largest in Indian electoral history. His technocratic push (computers in government, telecom liberalisation) is the early predecessor to the 1991 LPG reforms. The Bofors scandal ended his political career. His assassination (1991) while campaigning to return to power is in Post-Independence. The arc: sympathy victory, technocratic promise, corruption scandal, attempted comeback, assassination.
289.remember: A pilot by training, Rajiv brought a technocratic approach to the office, pushing computers into Indian bureaucracy.
289.upsc: Tests his modernisation initiatives (IT industry, telecom), the Bofors scandal, the Shah Bano case, the Rajiv-Longowal Accord, and his assassination in 1991 by an LTTE suicide bomber.

290.label: Indian Airlines hijacking to Lahore
290.what: The 1984 hijacking of an Indian Airlines aircraft to Lahore was one of several incidents that accelerated India's anti-terrorism legislation, eventually leading to TADA (Terrorist and Disruptive Activities Prevention Act) in 1985.
290.connections: TADA's enactment followed the hijacking. Its widespread misuse (leading to lapse in 1995 after Supreme Court rebuke) established the pattern that repeats with each anti-terrorism law India passes: emergency powers enacted in response to a specific incident, misused broadly, legally challenged, lapsed or reformed. TADA to POTA to UAPA is the sequence.
290.remember: TADA was so widely misused that it was allowed to lapse in 1995 after a Supreme Court rebuke. It was replaced by POTA in 2002 after the Parliament attack, which was itself repealed in 2004. UAPA is now the primary tool.
290.upsc: Tests TADA as India's first major anti-terrorism law, its controversial provisions (preventive detention, confessions to police), and the lineage from TADA to POTA to UAPA used today.

291.label: 52nd Amendment: Anti-Defection Law
291.what: The 52nd Amendment (1985) introduced the 'Anti-Defection Law' via the Tenth Schedule to stop 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' politics.
291.connections: The Anti-Defection Law is an attempt to stabilise democratic institutions against political manipulation. Pair with the SR Bommai judgment (1994), which addressed a related problem: governments being dismissed through unconstitutional use of Article 356. Both address the same underlying question: how do you protect democratic structures from being gamed by those within them?
291.remember: A member is disqualified if they voluntarily give up party membership or vote against their party's 'Whip' in the house.
291.upsc: Tests the role of the Speaker in disqualifying members and the 2003 amendment (91st) that removed 'splits' as an exception.

292.label: Berlin Wall
292.what: The Berlin Wall fell (1989), leading to German reunification (1990) and the collapse of communist regimes across Eastern Europe.
292.connections: The Wall fell because of a press conference mistake: an official read from the wrong notes and announced border crossings were open immediately rather than gradually. East Germans flooded the checkpoints before anyone could reverse the announcement. The fall led to German reunification (1990) and the collapse of communist regimes across Eastern Europe within two years. Compare with the Soviet dissolution (1991) as the political culmination of the same process.
292.remember: The wall actually fell because of a mistake; a government official accidentally announced that border crossings were open 'immediately,' and the crowds just took over.
292.upsc: Tests the Cold War context, German reunification, and how the fall of the Wall triggered the collapse of communist regimes across Eastern Europe and ultimately the Soviet Union in 1991.

293.label: Mandal Commission report
293.what: The Mandal Commission (1980) recommended 27% reservation for OBCs in govt jobs, implemented by V.P. Singh in 1990.
293.connections: Mandal is the most politically explosive social reform decision of independent India. It extended the reservation framework Ambedkar built into the Constitution for SCs and STs by covering OBCs, completing the constitutional intent. The Supreme Court upheld it in Indra Sawhney (1992) but capped total reservations at 50%. Pair with Ambedkar's resignation (1951) over diluted reform: both are moments where the gap between constitutional design and political will becomes visible.
293.remember: The implementation triggered massive protests by upper-caste students and fundamentally changed Indian electoral politics.
293.upsc: One of the most socially significant decisions in post-independence India. Tests the Indra Sawhney case (1992) which upheld OBC reservations but capped total reservations at 50 percent.

294.label: Liberalisation Reforms (LPG)
294.what: The 1991 budget by Finance Minister Manmohan Singh, under PM Narasimha Rao, dismantled the Licence Raj and opened India to foreign investment. The reforms are called LPG: Liberalisation, Privatisation, Globalisation.
294.connections: The 1991 reforms are the most consequential economic policy decision in post-Independence India. The gold airlift to London as collateral is the humiliation that made the reforms politically possible. Manmohan Singh's 'no power on earth can stop an idea whose time has come' is the oratorical companion to the policy. The shift from planned economy to market orientation changed every subsequent Indian economic story.
294.remember: India had so little foreign exchange in June 1991 that gold was physically flown to the Bank of England as collateral. The reforms were born of crisis, not ideology.
294.upsc: Tests the balance of payments crisis that triggered reform (India had two weeks of foreign exchange left), the role of Narasimha Rao as PM, and the specific LPG reforms.

295.label: Soviet Union
295.what: The Soviet Union formally dissolved on 25 December 1991 when Mikhail Gorbachev resigned and the USSR ceased to exist. Fifteen independent republics emerged, ending the Cold War that had dominated world politics since 1945.
295.connections: The Soviet Union dissolved without a shot being fired. Gorbachev's reforms (Glasnost and Perestroika) unintentionally destroyed the system they were meant to save. The Cold War ending reshaped India's foreign policy (loss of the reliable Soviet partner), its defence procurement, and its economic model: the 1991 LPG reforms happened in the same year as the Soviet collapse. The two events are not unconnected.
295.remember: The mightiest military empire in history collapsed without a shot being fired, essentially ending in a series of press conferences.
295.upsc: Tests reasons (economic stagnation, Gorbachev's Glasnost/Perestroika) and the impact on India's 'pro-Soviet' foreign policy.

296.label: Narasimha Rao
296.what: P.V. Narasimha Rao became Prime Minister in June 1991 leading a minority Congress government. Despite having no majority, he pushed through the most sweeping economic reforms in India's history alongside Finance Minister Manmohan Singh.
296.connections: Narasimha Rao is the accidental PM who became the most consequential reformer. No political base, no majority, a crisis. He used the crisis to implement reforms no politically strong PM had dared attempt. His management of the Babri Masjid issue (1992) is the shadow over his legacy. Compare with Manmohan Singh: both were technocratic reformers constrained by coalition politics, but Rao had more freedom because his political weakness paradoxically reduced opposition.
296.remember: Rao won a crucial confidence vote in 1993 by what critics called vote-buying. He was later charged in the JMM bribery case. A Congress PM who transformed India economically was later frozen out of the party he served. His statue at the AICC was installed only in 2023.
296.upsc: Tests Rao's political manoeuvring to survive as a minority PM, his role in liberalisation alongside Singh, the Look East Policy he initiated, and his handling of the Babri Masjid demolition controversy.

297.label: Rajiv Gandhi
297.what: Rajiv Gandhi was assassinated on 21 May 1991 by an LTTE suicide bomber named Thenmozhi Rajaratnam at an election rally in Sriperumbudur, Tamil Nadu.
297.connections: Rajiv's assassination by an LTTE suicide bomber is the most vivid scene in Indian political history. The bomb concealed in a garland, the embrace, the 14 others killed. He was assassinated while trying to return to power after losing in 1989 over Bofors. The LTTE's motive was the 1987 IPKF deployment to Sri Lanka, which turned into a military disaster and created the enemy who killed him.
297.remember: The bomb was concealed in a garland. Rajiv bent to receive it. The assassin hugged him. Fourteen others died with him. The photographer Haribabu, who died in the blast, captured the final moments on film. His camera was found intact.
297.upsc: Tests the LTTE motive (Indian Peacekeeping Force sent to Sri Lanka by Rajiv in 1987), the IPKF controversy, and the assassination's impact on the 1991 elections which Congress subsequently won.

298.label: Ratan Tata modernised Tata Group
298.what: Ratan Tata took over as chairman of the Tata Group in 1991 and transformed it from a domestic conglomerate into a global company, acquiring Tetley (2000), Corus Steel (2007), and Jaguar Land Rover (2008).
298.connections: Ratan Tata's transformation of Tata Group maps onto the 1991 LPG reforms: he took over just as the economy opened. The JLR acquisition (bought for less than Ford had paid) is the clearest symbol of Indian capital reversing the colonial direction of ownership. Compare with the EIC entry (1608): foreign company enters India to extract value versus Indian company acquires British brand at a discount centuries later.
298.remember: Tata bought Jaguar Land Rover from Ford for 2.3 billion dollars in 2008. Ford had paid 2.5 billion for it in 1989 and was desperate to sell. The company the British created to symbolise British engineering prestige is now owned by an Indian company and makes most of its profits in China.
298.upsc: Tests Tata Group's global acquisitions as examples of Indian corporate internationalisation, the significance of an Indian company buying British and European icons, and Ratan Tata's role in India's economic liberalisation era.

299.label: Param supercomputer developed
299.what: India's Centre for Development of Advanced Computing (C-DAC) developed the Param 8000 supercomputer in 1991 after the USA denied India access to Cray supercomputers citing security concerns. It was one of the fastest computers in the world at the time.
299.connections: Param is the second instance of the technology-denial-and-domestic-development pattern (after TIFRAC 1960). The US denied Cray supercomputers fearing Indian nuclear use; India built Param, then exported it to Germany. The pattern runs: TIFRAC (1960) → Param (1991) → cryogenic engines (denied, then developed for GSLV). Each denial produced a domestic capability.
299.remember: The USA denied India Cray supercomputers because it feared India would use them for nuclear weapons design. India built its own in 18 months. The technology denial that was meant to keep India weak gave India a domestic supercomputing industry. It is the recurring pattern of India's technology story.
299.upsc: Tests the technology denial that motivated domestic development, C-DAC's role in India's IT ecosystem, and the Param series as the foundation of India's high-performance computing capability.

300.label: Babri Masjid
300.what: The Babri Masjid at Ayodhya, traditionally attributed to Mir Baqi in the 16th century, was demolished by a mob on 6 December 1992. The demolition triggered major communal riots in several parts of India.
300.connections: The Babri Masjid demolition is the most divisive single event in post-Independence Indian history. The Supreme Court later held the demolition illegal but awarded the site for temple construction. That distinction (illegal act, legal outcome) is the judicial paradox. The 1993 Mumbai bombings were the retaliatory response. The 2019 Ayodhya verdict resolved the legal dispute while leaving the political wound open.
300.remember: The Supreme Court held the demolition to be illegal, even though it awarded the disputed site for temple construction on the basis of its assessment of the title evidence.
300.upsc: UPSC tests the legal dispute, the political impact, and the Supreme Court's 2019 judgment awarding the disputed land for a Ram temple while directing that an alternative plot be allotted for a mosque.

301.label: 73rd Amendment: Panchayati Raj
301.what: The 73rd Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj, adding the Eleventh Schedule with 29 subjects.
301.connections: Pair with the 74th Amendment (1992) for the complete picture. Both were passed in the same year as part of the same decentralisation push. Together they are the most significant devolution of power since Independence. The gap between constitutional mandate and actual empowerment of Panchayats is a standing exam topic: states retain discretion over transferring functions and funds, which many have not done.
301.remember: Before 1992, Panchayats were only a Directive Principle (Art 40); the amendment made them a mandatory third tier of govt.
301.upsc: Tests the three-tier structure, reservation for women (33%), and State Finance/Election Commissions.

302.label: 74th Amendment: Urban Local Bodies
302.what: The 74th Amendment (1992) granted constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (Municipalities), adding the Twelfth Schedule.
302.connections: The companion to the 73rd Amendment: where the 73rd covers rural governance (Panchayats), the 74th covers urban governance (Municipalities). The practical enforcement gap is similar in both cases: constitutional status does not guarantee actual powers or funds being devolved. As India urbanises, the 74th Amendment's implementation becomes more consequential than the 73rd's.
302.remember: Like the 73rd, it ensures direct elections and fixed five-year terms for urban local governments.
302.upsc: Often tested alongside the 73rd Amendment. Know the Twelfth Schedule, types of urban local bodies, reservation provisions, and State Election and Finance Commissions.

303.label: PSLV first successful flight
303.what: The PSLV (successful since 1994) is ISRO's 'workhorse' rocket, known for its high reliability and multiple satellite launches.
303.connections: The PSLV is ISRO's most commercially significant achievement: over 50 successful flights, launches for 34 countries, the 104-satellite single-launch record. Chandrayaan-1 (2008) and Mangalyaan (2013) both used the PSLV. It is the rocket that funded ISRO's ambitions through commercial revenue. Compare with GSLV Mk-III (2017) as the heavy-lift successor for larger payloads.
303.remember: The PSLV has had over 50 successful flights, becoming one of the most reliable rockets in the world today.
303.upsc: PSLV is the most tested ISRO rocket. Tests its success rate, payload capacity, and landmark missions: Chandrayaan-1 (2008), Mangalyaan (2013), and the record 104 satellites in one launch (2017).

304.label: Apartheid in South Africa
304.what: South Africa held its first fully democratic elections in April 1994, ending the apartheid system. Nelson Mandela, released from prison in 1990 after 27 years, became the first Black President.
304.connections: India imposed sanctions on South Africa in 1946, 48 years before the West followed. Mandela's first state visit as President was to India; he never forgot. The ANC-INC connection is one of the less-known chapters of both movements' histories. Compare with the UDHR (1948) as the international human rights framework that apartheid directly violated: India supported both the human rights document and the anti-apartheid sanctions from the beginning.
304.remember: India imposed sanctions on South Africa in 1946, 48 years before the West. Mandela never forgot this. He visited India before any Western country after his release. Nelson Mandela and Jawaharlal Nehru had corresponded from their respective prisons.
304.upsc: Tests India's historic anti-apartheid stance (India was the first country to impose sanctions on South Africa in 1946), the UN role, Mandela's influence on Indian foreign policy, and the Bharat Ratna awarded to Mandela.

305.label: SR Bommai judgment
305.what: The Supreme Court in SR Bommai vs Union of India (1994) held that the imposition of President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review and that a dismissed state government must be allowed to prove its majority on the floor of the House.
305.connections: SR Bommai is to federalism what Kesavananda Bharati is to parliamentary power: a judicial restraint on executive overreach. The judgment also declared secularism a basic feature of the Constitution, which has implications beyond Article 356. Pair with the Anti-Defection Law (1985): both attempt to protect democratic institutions from internal manipulation, one in Parliament, one in the Centre-state relationship.
305.remember: President's Rule was used 90 times between 1950 and 1994, almost always to dismiss opposition-party state governments. The SR Bommai judgment made this politically harder. After 1994, President's Rule has been imposed fewer than 20 times. One Supreme Court judgment changed how India's federal system works.
305.upsc: Tests Article 356 (President's Rule), the Sarkaria Commission recommendations incorporated in the judgment, and the floor test requirement. Article 356 had been used 125 times by 1994, often for political purposes.

306.label: World Trade Organization
306.what: The World Trade Organization (WTO) (est. 1995) replaced GATT to provide a global framework for trade rules and disputes.
306.connections: The WTO's most significant use for India is the TRIPS flexibilities on pharmaceutical patents: India argued successfully that public health emergencies allow compulsory licensing of patented drugs, enabling production of generic versions the developing world could afford. This is why Indian generics companies supply most of the developing world's medicines. Every global health and IP debate connects back to this card.
306.remember: India has used WTO rules to ensure it can produce life-saving drugs cheaply, a major win for public health in developing nations.
306.upsc: Tests the 'TRIPS' agreement and India's role in protecting its generic medicine industry from Western patent laws.

307.label: Kyoto Protocol
307.what: The Kyoto Protocol was adopted in December 1997, the first international treaty with binding greenhouse gas reduction targets for developed nations. The USA signed but never ratified. It came into force in 2005.
307.connections: The Kyoto Protocol's failure is as important as its ambition. The US Senate voted 95-0 against ratification before it was even signed. Compare with the Paris Agreement (2015): Paris learned from Kyoto by making commitments nationally determined (voluntary) rather than imposed. That structural change is why the US could join and re-join Paris but never joined Kyoto. The two cards together define the evolution of climate diplomacy.
307.remember: The US Senate voted 95-0 against ratifying Kyoto before it was even signed. The world's largest emitter at the time refused to be bound. India and China were exempt as developing nations. This fundamental unfairness drove the 20-year impasse in climate negotiations before Paris.
307.upsc: Tests the distinction between Kyoto (binding only for developed nations) and Paris Agreement (voluntary NDCs for all), the Common But Differentiated Responsibilities principle, and India's position as a developing nation exempt from Kyoto targets.

308.label: Vishaka Guidelines on sexual harassment
308.what: The Supreme Court in Vishaka vs State of Rajasthan (1997) laid down binding guidelines on prevention of sexual harassment at the workplace, filling a legislative vacuum. These guidelines had the force of law until Parliament passed the POSH Act in 2013.
308.connections: Vishaka is the Supreme Court's most explicit exercise of law-making in the absence of legislation, using Article 21 (right to life with dignity) as authority. The POSH Act (Prevention of Sexual Harassment at Workplace) was passed in 2013, replacing the guidelines with actual legislation after 16 years. Pair with the Domestic Violence Act (2005) in Social Reform as another example of legislation filling a gap in legal protection for women.
308.remember: The Vishaka Guidelines came from a case where a woman was gang-raped for stopping a child marriage. The perpetrators were acquitted. The outrage led to a PIL. The Supreme Court, finding no law on workplace sexual harassment, simply wrote one. A court created law because Parliament had failed to.
308.upsc: These guidelines had the force of law until the POSH Act was finally passed in 2013.

309.label: Pokhran-II (Operation Shakti)
309.what: Pokhran-II (1998) consisted of five nuclear tests under PM Vajpayee. India formally declared itself a nuclear weapons state.
309.connections: Pokhran-II formally made India a nuclear weapons state. The deception of US spy satellites is the operational legend. The US sanctions that followed were brief and largely ineffective. The Nuclear Deal with the US (2008) ended the 34-year nuclear isolation that began after Pokhran-I (1974). Pair the two Pokhran cards as the beginning and end of India's nuclear exclusion period.
309.remember: India deceived US spy satellites by having scientists wear civilian clothes, limiting movements at night, and conducting tests quickly. The CIA was embarrassed. The director of the DIA resigned. India had outmanoeuvred American intelligence.
309.upsc: Tests the code name Operation Shakti, the political consequences (US sanctions, Pakistan's tests), India's nuclear doctrine (no-first-use, minimum credible deterrence), and the US-India civilian nuclear deal (2008).

310.label: Amartya Sen won Nobel Prize in Economics
310.what: Amartya Sen won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 1998 for contributions to welfare economics, social choice theory, and his analysis showing famines result from policy failure and inequality, not food shortage.
310.connections: Sen's proof that famines do not occur in functioning democracies is the most politically useful theorem in this category. It connects to the National Food Security Act (2013), the Right to Food case (2001), and every Bengal Famine colonial card. His capability approach influenced the Human Development Index. Compare with Swaminathan: both addressed food security, one through economics, one through agronomy.
310.remember: Sen proved that famines almost never happen in functioning democracies because a free press and elections force governments to act. The 1943 Bengal famine killed 2 to 3 million people while food was being exported from India.
310.upsc: Know his 'Capability Approach' and his finding that no functioning democracy has ever had a major famine because a free press and elections force leaders to act.

311.label: Kargil War
311.what: The Kargil War (1999) saw India evict Pakistani infiltrators from high-altitude posts in Kashmir. It was a limited 'televised' war.
311.connections: Kargil is the only war fought between two nuclear-armed states in recorded history, giving it strategic significance beyond its military scale. Pakistan's high-altitude infiltration, India's tactical difficulty retaking mountain posts, and Clinton's pressure on Nawaz Sharif to withdraw are the three elements. The war began the ascendancy of the Indian military as a public institution in ways not seen since 1971.
311.remember: Nawaz Sharif flew to Washington on 4 July 1999 (US Independence Day) and asked Clinton to help him. Clinton told him to withdraw. Pakistan did. The war was the first armed conflict between two nuclear-armed states.
311.upsc: Tests the Lahore Declaration signed just three months before (Vajpayee's bus diplomacy), the evidence of Pakistani army involvement, international pressure on Pakistan, and the Kargil Review Committee report.

312.label: Atal Bihari Vajpayee
312.what: Atal Bihari Vajpayee became Prime Minister for the third time in October 1999 after the NDA coalition won elections, becoming the first non-Congress PM to serve a full five-year term.
312.connections: Vajpayee is the only BJP PM before Modi to complete a full term. His coalition management (NDA with 24 parties) is a masterclass in Indian coalition arithmetic. The Lahore bus diplomacy (1999) just before Kargil is the defining paradox of his career: peace initiative and war in the same year with the same neighbour. The Golden Quadrilateral highway project is his most tangible infrastructure legacy.
312.remember: Vajpayee was a poet and orator before a politician. He was the only BJP leader Nehru praised. He introduced the Golden Quadrilateral highway project connecting the four metros, the largest highway project in Indian history at the time.
312.upsc: Tests his key policies: Pokhran-II (1998), Lahore bus diplomacy, Kargil war, National Highways Development Project (Golden Quadrilateral), Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, and the India-Pakistan Composite Dialogue.

313.label: Parliament of India
313.what: The Parliament Attack (2001) by Lashkar-e-Taiba terrorists led to a massive military standoff (Operation Parakram) with Pakistan.
313.connections: The Parliament attack came three months after 9/11. India's military mobilisation (Operation Parakram) kept a million soldiers on the Pakistan border for 10 months without crossing it. That coercive diplomacy without escalation shaped India's approach to sub-conventional conflict for the next two decades, directly informing the doctrinal thinking behind the 2016 surgical strikes.
313.remember: Terrorists used a fake home ministry pass to enter; they were stopped just outside the main building where MPs were present.
313.upsc: Tests Operation Parakram (India's military mobilisation), the Agra Summit failure between Vajpayee and Musharraf, and how the attack transformed India's counter-terrorism strategy.

314.label: 9/11 attacks on USA
314.what: The 9/11 Attacks (2001) saw Al-Qaeda hijack planes to hit the Twin Towers and Pentagon, killing nearly 3,000 people.
314.connections: The 9/11 attacks killed nearly 3,000 people and triggered responses that killed hundreds of thousands. For India, 9/11 reframed Pakistan's support for cross-border militancy from a bilateral issue to a global one: the same month, the US was asking Pakistan to choose sides. The Parliament Attack (2001) in Post-Independence happened three months later, in a world already primed for the terrorism framing.
314.remember: The attacks led to the 'Department of Homeland Security' and changed global aviation and security protocols forever.
314.upsc: Tests the US 'War on Terror', invasion of Afghanistan, and the transformation of India-US strategic relations.

315.label: Right to Food campaign and NFSA
315.what: The Supreme Court's 2001 interim order in the Right to Food case directed all state governments to implement the mid-day meal scheme in primary schools, a landmark order that transformed school attendance and child nutrition.
315.connections: The Supreme Court's 2001 Right to Food orders are the judicial origin of the national mid-day meal programme expansion. Before the orders, mid-day meals existed in some states but not universally. The Court made them mandatory. The National Food Security Act (2013) is the legislative completion of what the Court began through judicial direction in 2001. This card and the NFSA card are a direct sequence.
315.remember: Before mid-day meals, many poor children came to school hungry and left after the first period. The Supreme Court fed India's children with a court order. School enrolment increased by 15-25% in states that implemented it seriously.
315.upsc: Tests the Right to Food case (PUCL vs Union of India), the Supreme Court's use of PILs to enforce Directive Principles, and the journey from the 2001 order to the National Food Security Act 2013.

316.label: 86th Amendment: Right to Education
316.what: The 86th Amendment of 2002 inserted Article 21A making free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14. It also added a Fundamental Duty for parents to provide education.
316.connections: The seven-year gap between constitutional amendment (2002) and implementing legislation (2009) illustrates the distance between constitutional rights and actual enforcement. Compare with the privacy judgment (2017): both are rights added to or read into Article 21. Pair with Ambedkar's view (1947) that education is the prerequisite for social equality, making this amendment the constitutional completion of his vision.
316.remember: Education for children below 6 remains a Directive Principle (Art 45), while 6–14 is a legally enforceable Fundamental Right.
316.upsc: This amendment enabled the Right to Education Act 2009. UPSC tests Article 21A, the age group, and the distinction between the constitutional amendment and the implementing legislation.

317.label: Indian Ocean Tsunami
317.what: The Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami on 26 December 2004 killed over 230,000 people across 14 countries. India's Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala were severely affected.
317.connections: The 2004 tsunami revealed the absence of an Indian Ocean early warning system: the Pacific system existed, the Indian Ocean had nothing. India subsequently invested in the Indian Tsunami Early Warning System (ITEWS). Compare with INSAT-1B (1983): both are examples of disaster forcing investment in early warning infrastructure. The lesson took a different catastrophe to learn each time.
317.remember: The tsunami was caused by the third largest earthquake ever recorded. It released energy equivalent to 23,000 Hiroshima bombs. The waves travelled 8000 km. Indian villages that had maintained ancient oral traditions warning of water receding before a wave survived. Villages that had lost these traditions did not.
317.upsc: Tests India's disaster management response, the Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System established afterward, the Disaster Management Act 2005, and the NDMA (National Disaster Management Authority) created as a result.

318.label: Right to Information Act
318.what: The Right to Information Act (2005) gives citizens the right to request info from public authorities. It is a vital anti-corruption tool.
318.connections: The RTI Act grew from a Rajasthan grassroots movement where villagers used the right to inspect muster rolls to document corruption in public works. That origin (ordinary people defeating bureaucratic secrecy with a ledger) is why RTI is considered India's most successful transparency legislation. Compare with the Lokpal Act (2013): RTI addresses information access; Lokpal addresses institutional accountability. Both respond to the same governance problem.
318.remember: The law was born from a grassroots movement where villagers demanded to see muster rolls to check if they were being paid fairly.
318.upsc: Tests RTI exemptions, the role of Information Commissioners, and its roots in the MKSS movement in Rajasthan.

319.label: MNREGA
319.what: MGNREGA (2005) guarantees 100 days of manual work to rural households. It is the world's largest social welfare program.
319.connections: MGNREGA converted employment from a government scheme into a legal right: failure to provide work within 15 days triggers unemployment allowance. It is the world's largest social welfare programme by participation. Compare with the Community Development Programme (1952) in Social Reform: both attempt rural welfare through state machinery, but MGNREGA builds in legal rights that the CDP lacked.
319.remember: MGNREGA made employment a legal right, not a government scheme. If work is not provided within 15 days, the applicant is entitled to an unemployment allowance. This legal enforceability was unprecedented.
319.upsc: Tests the legal framework (demand-driven, time-bound, legally enforceable), outcomes (financial inclusion, women's participation, distress migration reduction), and critiques (corruption, fiscal burden).

320.label: National Rural Health Mission
320.what: The National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) was launched on 5 April 2005 to improve healthcare delivery in rural India through community health workers (ASHA), improved infrastructure, and flexible funding to states.
320.connections: The NRHM's ASHA worker programme (nearly a million women trained as community health workers) is the last-mile public health infrastructure that later delivered COVID vaccines in 2021. It is the closest post-Independence India has come to the Bhakti tradition of service as vocation at national scale. Compare with the Community Development Programme (1952) in Social Reform: both attempt rural service delivery, but ASHA workers are trained local women, not outside bureaucrats.
320.remember: India trained nearly 1 million women as ASHA workers across rural India. These women , most without formal education beyond Class 8 , became the frontline of India's health system. They are credited with saving hundreds of thousands of lives by improving vaccination rates and maternal care.
320.upsc: Tests the ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) programme, the district hospital strengthening, and NRHM's merger into the National Health Mission in 2013. IMR and MMR reductions are attributed partly to NRHM.

321.label: Domestic Violence Act
321.what: The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 was India's first comprehensive civil law addressing domestic violence, recognising physical, emotional, sexual, verbal, and economic abuse. It created a new class of Protection Officers.
321.connections: Pair with the Dowry Prohibition Act (1961): where that Act criminalises a transaction, this Act recognises a pattern of behaviour. The two together cover different dimensions of the same harm. Connect to Vishaka Guidelines (1997) in the Constitutional category as another example of rights created to fill gaps in legal protection for women. Both Vishaka and the DVA address harms that were legally invisible before the intervention.
321.remember: Before 2005, India had criminal laws against specific acts like dowry harassment, but no law that recognised the pattern of domestic violence as a whole. A wife could complain about being beaten, but the law had no concept of emotional abuse, financial control, or the repeated pattern that constitutes domestic violence.
321.upsc: Tests the Act alongside Section 498A IPC and the Dowry Prohibition Act as the framework of women's protection laws, the civil versus criminal remedy distinction, and the residence rights provision that keeps women in the matrimonial home.

322.label: 26/11 Mumbai Attacks
322.what: The 26/11 Mumbai Attacks (2008) saw 10 terrorists strike multiple targets. It led to the creation of the National Investigation Agency (NIA).
322.connections: The 26/11 attacks are the most significant terrorist event in India's post-Independence history by strategic impact. Ten attackers paralysed India's financial capital for 60 hours. The NIA and NSG reorganisation followed directly. The attacks were planned over months; the Pakistani connection was documented and became the permanent framework for India-Pakistan relations for the following decade.
322.remember: The attacks were planned for months. The terrorists were trained in Pakistan and navigated using GPS and satellite phones with handlers in Pakistan. Kasab's conviction took three years. He was hanged in 2012.
322.upsc: Tests the investigation that proved Pakistan-based origins, the lone survivor Ajmal Kasab's trial and execution, the breakdown in India-Pakistan talks, and the National Investigation Agency formed as a response.

323.label: Global Financial Crisis
323.what: The 2008 Global Financial Crisis was triggered by the US housing bubble and the collapse of Lehman Brothers.
323.connections: India emerged from the 2008 crisis relatively unscathed because RBI's conservative banking regulations limited Indian banks' exposure to toxic assets. Compare with India's 1991 crisis: the earlier crisis came from fiscal mismanagement; 2008 showed that well-regulated domestic banks can provide insulation from external shocks. The contrast between 1991 (India in crisis while world was stable) and 2008 (India stable while world was in crisis) tells the full story of 17 years of reform.
323.remember: India emerged from the 2008 crisis relatively unscathed because the RBI had maintained conservative banking regulations. Governor Y.V. Reddy who had resisted financial deregulation was later credited with saving India.
323.upsc: Tests the causes (deregulation) and why India remained relatively insulated due to conservative RBI banking rules.

324.label: Chandrayaan-1
324.what: Chandrayaan-1, launched on 22 October 2008, was India's first lunar mission. Its Moon Impact Probe became the first Indian object to touch the Moon, and the mission's instruments confirmed the presence of water molecules in the lunar soil.
324.connections: Chandrayaan-1 discovered water molecules on the Moon's surface in 2008, changing the calculus of all future lunar missions. India found water on the Moon before landing there. The Moon Impact Probe, painted with the Indian tricolour, was deliberately crashed at the lunar south pole. That water discovery made the south pole the most strategically important lunar location, which is exactly why Chandrayaan-3 (2023) targeted the same region 15 years later.
324.remember: India 'planted its flag' by crashing the Moon Impact Probe, painted with the tricolour, into the lunar south pole.
324.upsc: Tests the water discovery, the Moon Impact Probe that carried the Indian flag, and that NASA's instrument on board made the water detection. The mission ended in 2009 when contact was lost.

325.label: Right to Education Act
325.what: The Right to Education Act 2009 implemented Article 21A, mandating free and compulsory education for all children aged 6 to 14. It required private schools to reserve 25 percent seats for economically weaker sections.
325.connections: The RTE Act is the implementation of Article 21A (2002): amendment first, legislation seven years later. Compare with the National Food Security Act (2013) as another example of converting a constitutional aspiration into a legal entitlement. Both are post-2000 attempts to make Directive Principles justiciable through legislation. The Savitribai Phule school (1848) in Social Reform is the earliest institutional predecessor to what this Act attempts at national scale.
325.remember: The Supreme Court upheld the 25% reservation for EWS, calling it a 'reasonable restriction' on the rights of private schools.
