325.upsc: Tests the 25 percent reservation requirement for private schools, the no-detention policy (later amended), and the role of neighbourhood schools. The Society for Unaided Private Schools case upheld the 25 percent rule.

326.label: Aadhaar
326.what: The Unique Identification Authority of India (UIDAI) was established in 2009 to create Aadhaar, a 12-digit biometric identity number linked to fingerprints and iris scans. Over 1.3 billion Indians have Aadhaar numbers.
326.connections: Aadhaar's significance is not the database but the infrastructure it enabled: direct benefit transfer (DBT), Jan Dhan Yojana, digital payments through UPI. The right to privacy judgment (2017) in the Constitutional category directly challenged Aadhaar's mandatory nature. The two cards are a direct tension: the identification system versus the constitutional right that questioned its scope. That tension is still unresolved.
326.remember: Aadhaar is the world's largest biometric database. It has enrolled more people than any identification system in history. The first person to be enrolled was Ranjana Sonawane from Maharashtra in 2010. The system she helped launch eventually covered all 1.3 billion Indians.
326.upsc: Tests the Aadhaar Act 2016, the Supreme Court's 2018 partial upholding of Aadhaar (constitutional but cannot be mandatory for private services), and the JAM (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) trinity for welfare delivery.

327.label: Agni-V missile tested
327.what: India successfully tested the Agni-V intercontinental ballistic missile on 19 April 2012, with a range of over 5,000 km capable of reaching most of Asia and parts of Europe. India joined the elite club of nations with ICBM capability.
327.connections: Agni-V's range (5,000+ km) is the strategic significance: it can reach Beijing and most of Europe. Compare with Pokhran-II (1998) as the weapons-state declaration and Agni-V as the delivery capability maturation. The nuclear triad (land, sea, air delivery) and credible minimum deterrence are the strategic concepts this card supports.
327.remember: For UPSC, the important thing is strategic reach and deterrence, not one dramatic city-to-city time estimate.
327.upsc: Tests India's missile programme, strategic deterrence, and the distinction between conventional defence projects and the nuclear-capable delivery system debate.

328.label: Mangalyaan (Mars Orbiter Mission)
328.what: Mangalyaan, the Mars Orbiter Mission, was launched in November 2013 and entered Mars orbit on 24 September 2014. India became the first country to reach Mars on its first attempt, at a cost of Rs. 450 crore.
328.connections: Mangalyaan entered Mars orbit on 24 September 2014 on the first attempt, making India the only country to achieve this. The cost comparison is the most cited detail: India reached Mars for Rs. 450 crore; the Hollywood film Gravity released the same year had a higher budget. Compare with ISRO's founding (1969) when rockets were transported on bicycles: the distance between those two images is the entire Indian space programme arc.
328.remember: ISRO scientists celebrated the orbit entry at 7:41 AM on 24 Sep; the mission lasted far longer than its 6-month design. India reached Mars for Rs. 450 crore. The Hollywood film Gravity, released the same year, had a budget of Rs. 700 crore. ISRO scientists used their mobile phones as processing units to simulate trajectory. The mission operated for 7 years, far beyond its planned 6-month life.
328.upsc: Tests the mission's low cost (cheaper than the movie 'Gravity') and its success in studying the Martian atmosphere.

329.label: National Food Security Act
329.what: The National Food Security Act (2013) legally entitles 67% of India's population to subsidised food grains.
329.connections: The NFSA gives legal teeth to Article 21 (right to life) by treating food as a justiciable right rather than a welfare policy. It connects to the Directive Principle under Article 47 (duty to raise nutrition levels). Compare with the Right to Education Act (2009) as the same pattern: constitutional aspiration converted into legal entitlement. The Maternity Benefit Amendment Act (2017) in Social Reform extends the same logic to workplace rights.
329.remember: The act also provides for a maternity benefit of ₹6,000 for pregnant women, a key part of India's nutritional safety net.
329.upsc: Tests the coverage (75% rural, 50% urban) and the prices: Rice ₹3, Wheat ₹2, Coarse grains ₹1 per kg.

330.label: Lokpal and Lokayukta Act
330.what: The Lokpal and Lokayukta Act 2013 established the Lokpal (national anti-corruption ombudsman) and mandated Lokayuktas in states. It was the result of the Anna Hazare-led Jan Lokpal movement that brought millions to the streets.
330.connections: The Lokpal arc is the most recent mass mobilisation in this category. Compare with the Servants of India Society (1905): Gokhale's trained public servants versus a statutory ombudsman, both attempting to solve unaccountable governance a century apart. The six-year gap between the Act and the first Lokpal appointment illustrates the recurring pattern: legislation passes, enforcement lags, social pressure moves to the next issue.
330.remember: Anna Hazare's fast in 2011 at Ramlila Maidan brought more people to a protest than any event since independence. The Parliament was forced to pass a resolution supporting the Jan Lokpal concept. The final law was weaker than demanded, but an institution that did not exist was created.
330.upsc: Tests the Anna Hazare movement (2011), the demands for an independent anti-corruption body, the difference between the demanded Jan Lokpal and the enacted Lokpal, and the first Lokpal appointment in 2019.

331.label: Jan Dhan Yojana
331.what: Jan Dhan Yojana (2014) aimed for universal financial inclusion. Over 500 million bank accounts have been opened under the scheme.
331.connections: Jan Dhan's significance is in enabling direct benefit transfer (DBT): bank account linked to Aadhaar linked to mobile creates a pipeline that bypasses intermediaries. The LPG subsidy example (from middlemen to direct beneficiaries) is the clearest illustration. 500 million accounts opened is the scale metric. Compare with RTI Act (2005): both address accountability, one through transparency, one through financial disintermediation.
331.remember: Before Jan Dhan, LPG subsidies were transferred to middlemen who sold subsidised cylinders at market price. After linking bank accounts to Aadhaar, subsidies went directly to beneficiaries. Corruption was structurally eliminated.
331.upsc: Tests the Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile (JAM) trinity that transformed welfare delivery, the elimination of leakages in subsidy transfer, and the World Bank's recognition as the largest financial inclusion programme.

332.label: Polio eradication in India
332.what: The World Health Organisation certified India polio-free on 27 March 2014, three years after the last reported case in 2011. The Pulse Polio immunisation programme, launched in 1995, was the world's largest public health campaign.
332.connections: India's polio eradication came from a combination of oral vaccine, cold chain management, pulse immunisation days (targeting every child under 5 simultaneously), and ASHA workers for last-mile delivery. The same public health infrastructure delivered COVID vaccines in 2021. India's polio eradication is considered by WHO as the most challenging eradication in history, which makes the 2014 certification the harder-won achievement.
332.remember: India was considered the hardest country in the world to eradicate polio because of dense population, poor sanitation, and high birth rate. When India was certified polio-free, health officials cried. It was the public health equivalent of a moon landing.
332.upsc: Tests the Pulse Polio programme, the two oral polio vaccine drops given to children under 5, India's status as one of the most difficult countries to eradicate polio due to population density, and the public health significance.

333.label: NALSA judgment on transgender rights
333.what: The Supreme Court's NALSA vs Union of India judgment in April 2014 recognised transgender persons as a third gender and directed the government to provide reservations and rights. It was India's most significant LGBTQ rights ruling before Section 377.
333.connections: NALSA and Section 377 decriminalisation (2018) are a direct two-step sequence: recognition of identity (2014) followed by removal of criminalisation (2018). Both are built on Article 21 as expanded by Maneka Gandhi (1978) and the right to privacy judgment (2017). The NALSA argument that colonial law erased pre-existing Indian recognition is the same argument made about Section 377: both are restorations as much as innovations.
333.remember: The NALSA judgment was based partly on India's ancient recognition of the hijra community in Mughal court records and Hindu texts. The court said India has historically known a third gender and the Constitution cannot be read to deny them rights. History was used to advance modernity.
333.upsc: Tests the NALSA judgment alongside the Section 377 decriminalisation (2018) as milestones in LGBTQ rights, the self-identification principle it established, and the Transgender Persons Protection of Rights Act 2019.

334.label: Paris Climate Agreement
334.what: The Paris Agreement was adopted under the UNFCCC in December 2015. It aims to hold the increase in global average temperature to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels while pursuing efforts to limit it to 1.5°C, and it entered into force in 2016.
334.connections: The Paris Agreement's key innovation over Kyoto (1997) is nationally determined contributions (NDCs): countries set their own targets rather than having binding ones imposed. India's position (rich nations caused the problem and should fund the transition; India's 2070 net-zero target) is the explicit equity argument. India is simultaneously one of the most climate-vulnerable nations and one of the largest future emitters. That tension defines its negotiating position.
334.remember: India argues that rich nations caused the problem and should pay for the transition; India's 2070 target gives it time to develop.
334.upsc: Tests India's 'Net Zero 2070' commitment and the principle of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities' (CBDR).

335.label: Digital India
335.what: The Digital India programme was launched on 1 July 2015 with the goal of transforming India into a digitally empowered society through broadband connectivity, digital literacy, and e-governance services.
335.connections: Digital India's most measurable output is UPI: India now processes more digital transactions than the USA, UK, Germany, France, and China combined. That transformation from cash-dominated economy to global digital payments leader happened within a decade. Compare with TIFRAC (1960): India's first computer was a single machine; its digital infrastructure now handles billions of daily transactions.
335.remember: India's UPI (Unified Payments Interface) processes more digital transactions than the USA, UK, Germany, France and Australia combined. A programme launched to bring internet to villages created the world's most used digital payment system. India went from cash-only to digital-first in under a decade.
335.upsc: Tests the three vision areas (digital infrastructure, governance and services on demand, digital empowerment), the BharatNet project for rural broadband, and achievements like UPI's global expansion.

336.label: NJAC Act
336.what: The Supreme Court struck down the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) Act in 2015, which would have given the executive a role in judicial appointments. The collegium system (judges appointing judges) was upheld.
336.connections: The NJAC judgment is the most recent major assertion of judicial independence against a unanimous Parliament, placing it in the same category as Kesavananda Bharati (1973). Both cases involved the Court striking down something Parliament very much wanted to do. The tension between judicial independence and democratic accountability in judicial appointments is the live constitutional debate this card represents.
336.remember: The NJAC had been passed unanimously by Parliament and ratified by all state legislatures , the most broadly supported constitutional amendment in recent history. The Supreme Court struck it down anyway. Five judges overruled every elected representative in the country. Whether that is judicial independence or judicial overreach is the central debate.
336.upsc: Tests the collegium system (no constitutional basis, evolved through Three Judges Cases), the NJAC as the government's attempt to reclaim appointment power, and the tension between judicial independence and executive accountability.

337.label: Surgical Strikes across LoC
337.what: The 2016 Surgical Strikes were conducted by Indian Special Forces across the LoC in response to the Uri terror attack.
337.connections: The 2016 strikes are significant less for military impact than for doctrine change: for the first time India publicly acknowledged a cross-LoC military action rather than keeping it covert. The press conference announcing them signalled a willingness to use information as deterrence. Compare with Balakot airstrike (2019) as the next escalation in the same doctrine of public coercive action.
337.remember: Unlike previous covert actions, the government held a press conference to announce the strikes, signaling a new assertive stance.
337.upsc: UPSC tests the distinction between the Line of Control and the international border, India's stated doctrine on cross-border terrorism, and the diplomatic consequences.

338.label: Demonetisation
338.what: PM Modi announced on 8 November 2016 that Rs. 500 and Rs. 1000 currency notes would cease to be legal tender from midnight, affecting 86% of India's currency in circulation.
338.connections: Demonetisation invalidated Rs. 86% of currency in circulation overnight. The stated goals (black money, counterfeit currency, cashless economy) were not clearly met by available evidence. The cash shortage, queue deaths, and informal economy contraction are the human cost. It remains the most contested economic policy decision of the post-1991 era. Compare with 1991 LPG reforms as the prior landmark economic disruption.
338.remember: India ran out of new notes to replace the demonetised ones for weeks. People died waiting in queues. The informal economy, which was 90% cash-based, was devastated. The Supreme Court later upheld the decision 4-1 but called the process flawed.
338.upsc: Tests the stated objectives (black money, counterfeiting, digital payments), the economic disruption, the RBI's role, GDP impact debates, and the Supreme Court's 2023 upholding of the decision.

339.label: Goods and Services Tax
339.what: The Goods and Services Tax (GST) (2017) replaced 17 central and state taxes with a single indirect tax system.
339.connections: GST replaced 17 central and state taxes with a single indirect tax, requiring a constitutional amendment allowing both Centre and States to tax the same transaction. The 17-year implementation timeline (first proposed 2000, implemented 2017) reflects the federal negotiation required. The 'one nation, one tax' framing versus the multiple GST slabs in practice is the gap between political pitch and operational complexity.
339.remember: The reform took 17 years to implement, requiring a constitutional amendment to allow both Center and States to tax services.
339.upsc: Tests the four GST slabs (0, 5, 12, 18, 28 percent), the GST Council's composition, the dual structure (CGST and SGST), the impact on manufacturing and the informal economy, and implementation challenges.

340.label: GSLV Mk-III first flight
340.what: The GSLV Mk-III (now LVM3) completed its first developmental flight in June 2017. It is India's heaviest rocket, capable of placing 4-tonne satellites into geostationary orbit, ending dependence on foreign rockets for heavy payloads.
340.connections: The GSLV Mk-III (now LVM3) required indigenous cryogenic engine technology after the US pressured Russia not to transfer it in 1992. India spent a decade developing it independently. The pattern (denied technology, built it) runs from TIFRAC (1960) through Param (1991) to cryogenic engines. The Chandrayaan-3 mission in 2023 used the LVM3, closing the circle from denial to lunar landing.
340.remember: India struggled for decades to develop cryogenic engine technology after the USA pressured Russia not to transfer it in the 1990s. Mk-III represents India's indigenous solution after 20 years of effort.
340.upsc: Tests the distinction between PSLV, GSLV and GSLV Mk-III. Mk-III uses India's own cryogenic engine, ending dependence on Russia for cryogenic technology. Used for Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3.

341.label: Maternity Benefit Amendment Act
341.what: The Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017 extended paid maternity leave from 12 to 26 weeks for women working in establishments with 10 or more employees, making India's maternity leave one of the most generous in the world.
341.connections: Pair with the National Food Security Act (2013) in the Constitutional category as part of the post-2010 welfare legislation arc. Both address the gap between constitutional aspiration and material conditions for women. The informal economy exclusion is the enforcement gap in this card: the same structural problem that appears in dowry, child marriage, and untouchability laws. Legislation covers the formal; the informal remains outside.
341.remember: India's 26 weeks of paid maternity leave is more than the USA (which has zero guaranteed paid leave), more than China (18 weeks), and close to Scandinavian countries. A developing country with a stronger maternity leave policy than the world's largest economy.
341.upsc: Tests the amendment alongside the Sexual Harassment at Workplace Act 2013 and the Maternity Benefit Act 1961 as examples of women's labour rights legislation. India's 26-week leave is compared globally.

342.label: Right to privacy declared Fundamental
342.what: In Justice KS Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2017), a nine-judge Supreme Court bench unanimously held that the right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21. The case arose from a challenge to the Aadhaar programme.
342.connections: The privacy judgment overruled two earlier Supreme Court judgments (MP Sharma 1954, Kharak Singh 1962) that had denied privacy as a fundamental right. It is built on Maneka Gandhi (1978) and the chain of Article 21 expansions since. Its immediate context was the Aadhaar challenge. It provided the constitutional foundation for the Section 377 decriminalisation (2018) in Social Reform, one year later. Privacy established the principle; Section 377 applied it.
342.remember: India spent 63 years without a constitutional right to privacy. The government had argued in court that Indians have no fundamental right to privacy and the Founding Fathers never intended one. Nine judges disagreed unanimously. The right that authoritarian governments fear most was declared fundamental.
342.upsc: Tests the right to privacy as part of Article 21, the overruling of MP Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) which had held privacy was not a fundamental right, and the subsequent implications for data protection law.

343.label: Section 377 decriminalised
343.what: The Supreme Court unanimously struck down Section 377 of the IPC on 6 September 2018, decriminalising consensual same-sex relations between adults. The law dated from 1861 and had been used to harass the LGBTQ community.
343.connections: Section 377 is the endpoint of a two-card sequence with NALSA (2014): recognition of identity, then removal of criminalisation. The right to privacy judgment (2017) in the Constitutional category provided the direct constitutional foundation one year earlier. Privacy established the principle; Section 377 applied it. The colonial origin of the law (Macaulay, 1860) also connects it to the broader argument that several post-2014 social reform judgments have made: Indian tradition recognised what colonial law prohibited.
343.remember: Section 377 was written by Lord Macaulay in 1860. Britain decriminalised homosexuality in 1967. India was still using a British Victorian-era law to criminalise its citizens 157 years after it was written, 51 years after Britain itself abandoned it.
343.upsc: Tests the journey from Naz Foundation (2009 Delhi HC struck down 377) to Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013 Supreme Court reinstated 377) to Navtej Johar (2018 Supreme Court final decriminalisation).

344.label: Balakot airstrike
344.what: On 26 February 2019, India stated that the Indian Air Force had struck a Jaish-e-Mohammed facility at Balakot in Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province following the Pulwama attack that killed 40 CRPF personnel.
344.connections: The Balakot airstrike was the first Indian airstrike inside Pakistan since 1971. Its strategic effectiveness is debated; its domestic political significance in election year 2019 is less disputed. Compare with the 2016 surgical strikes as the prior step in the same doctrine of public coercive action. The two together represent a shift from covert to declared cross-border military action.
344.remember: It was the first time since 1971 that Indian jets crossed the international border to strike inside Pakistan proper.
344.upsc: UPSC tests the sequence from Pulwama to Balakot, Pakistan's response, the aerial engagement that followed, and the return of Wing Commander Abhinandan Varthaman.

345.label: Article 370
345.what: The Abrogation of Article 370 (2019) removed Jammu & Kashmir's special status and bifurcated the state into two Union Territories.
345.connections: The abrogation of Article 370 removed J&K's special status and bifurcated it into two Union Territories. The constitutional mechanism (using Article 370 itself via Presidential order) is the legal creativity employed. The Supreme Court upheld it in 2023. Connect to Operation Polo (1948) and Junagadh accession (1948): all three are assertions of Indian sovereignty over territories that had special or contested arrangements.
345.remember: Article 370 was removed using Article 370 itself, since the President can modify the article. The legal creativity of using the provision to abolish itself was debated by constitutional scholars for years afterward.
345.upsc: Tests the constitutional mechanism used (Presidential Order under Article 370 and 367), the Supreme Court's 2023 upholding of the abrogation, the statehood restoration promise, and the impact on J&K's political landscape.

346.label: Chandrayaan-2
346.what: Chandrayaan-2 was launched on 22 July 2019. The Vikram lander lost communication during descent and crashed near the south pole on 7 September 2019. The orbiter however continues to function and has mapped the lunar surface.
346.connections: Chandrayaan-2's Vikram lander crashed 2.1 km from the surface. The orbiter component continues to function and collect data. The lesson learned from the crash directly informed Chandrayaan-3's successful landing in 2023. The failure and success are a direct pair: the same organisation, the same target site, four years apart, one failure and one success.
346.remember: PM Modi was watching live when Vikram crashed. He walked to the ISRO director and hugged him saying don't worry, India is proud of you. That image, of a leader consoling scientists after failure rather than punishing them, is credited with giving ISRO the confidence to try again and succeed.
346.upsc: Tests the failure analysis (braking software error in last 2.1 km), PM Modi's presence at mission control and public consolation of ISRO scientists, and how the failure led directly to the successful Chandrayaan-3.

347.label: COVID-19 first case in India
347.what: India's first confirmed COVID-19 case was reported in Kerala on 30 January 2020. Later that year India imposed one of the world's strictest lockdowns and eventually launched one of the world's largest vaccination drives.
347.connections: India's COVID response combined one of the world's most stringent early lockdowns with the Vaccine Maitri programme that distributed vaccines to 90 countries before the Delta wave forced a pause. The 4-hour notice for the national lockdown and the reverse migration crisis it triggered are the humanitarian cost. The vaccine programme is the diplomatic achievement. Both are part of the same card.
347.remember: India gave COVID vaccines to over 90 countries through the Vaccine Maitri programme before the Delta wave forced India to suspend exports. The programme was a major soft power success until domestic needs overwhelmed supply.
347.upsc: Tests the Disaster Management Act, the public-health response, the lockdown, and Vaccine Maitri.

348.label: Cyclone Amphan devastated Bengal
348.what: Cyclone Amphan made landfall in West Bengal in May 2020 as an extremely severe storm and caused enormous damage in eastern India and Bangladesh.
348.connections: Amphan is the test case for India's early warning and mass evacuation capability: 5 million people evacuated before landfall. Compare with the 2004 tsunami (no warning system, no coordinated evacuation). The improvement in early warning and evacuation capacity between those two events is one of India's most significant disaster management achievements. INSAT (1983) made the warning possible; the NDMA made the evacuation systematic.
348.remember: India evacuated 5 million people before Amphan struck. Despite being the strongest Bay of Bengal cyclone in recorded history, deaths were under 100 due to the evacuation. In 1999 a weaker Odisha cyclone killed 10,000 with no warning system. India's investment in disaster preparedness saved millions of lives.
348.upsc: Tests the IMD's warning systems, large-scale evacuation, disaster management, and the growing discussion about climate-linked cyclone intensity in the Bay of Bengal.

349.label: India's COVID vaccination programme
349.what: India launched the world's largest vaccination programme on 16 January 2021, using indigenously developed Covaxin (Bharat Biotech) and licensed Covishield (Oxford-AstraZeneca manufactured by SII). India administered over 2 billion doses.
349.connections: India's COVID vaccination programme administered 25 million doses in a single day in June 2021, a world record. CoWIN's management is the technology story; the ASHA worker network is the last-mile delivery story; Vaccine Maitri (vaccines to 90 countries) is the foreign policy story. The same public health infrastructure built for polio eradication (2014) delivered the world-record vaccination day.
349.remember: India administered 25 million doses in a single day in June 2021, a world record. The CoWIN app that managed bookings was subsequently offered to other countries as a public good. India's digital vaccination infrastructure is now a model being studied globally.
349.upsc: Tests the two vaccines (Covaxin as India's first indigenously developed COVID vaccine), CoWIN platform for digital vaccination management, the Vaccine Maitri programme that supplied vaccines to 90 countries, and the ICMR-BBT collaboration.

350.label: Chandrayaan-3
350.what: Chandrayaan-3 landed the Vikram lander near the Moon's south pole on 23 August 2023, making India the first country to land near the lunar south pole and the fourth to soft-land on the Moon.
350.connections: India landing at the lunar south pole makes Chandrayaan-3 the direct payoff of Chandrayaan-1's water discovery in 2008: the 15-year arc from finding water to landing where the water is. Russia's Luna-25 crashing days earlier is the competitive context. The Chandrayaan arc (1, 2 failure, 3 success) is the most complete iterative development story in this category.
350.remember: India landed just days after Russia's Luna-25 crashed attempting the same feat. The south pole matters because of confirmed water ice, which could support future human settlements on the Moon.
350.upsc: Tests the Pragyan rover, the landing site named Shiv Shakti Point, the significance of south pole water ice deposits, and that Chandrayaan-2 had failed to land in 2019.

351.label: Aditya-L1 solar mission
351.what: Aditya-L1 (2023) is India's first solar mission, positioned at the Lagrange Point 1 to study the Sun continuously.
351.connections: Aditya-L1 studies the Sun from the L1 Lagrange point, 1.5 million km from Earth, allowing uninterrupted solar observation with no eclipses and minimal fuel. After Chandrayaan-3 (Moon, August 2023), Aditya-L1 (Sun, September 2023) continued ISRO's pattern of ambitious firsts on low budgets. The two launches within the same month are the clearest demonstration of how much ISRO's throughput has grown since the bicycle-and-church days.
351.remember: L1 is a 'gravitational sweet spot' 1.5 million km from Earth, allowing uninterrupted solar observation without eclipses.
351.upsc: Tests the concept of Lagrange points, Aditya-L1's scientific objectives (studying solar wind, corona, and solar flares), and its significance as India's first dedicated solar mission.
